At that point I believe we were too far to intercept the course.  Approach asked if we were going through course.  They gave us a heading to the south to reintercept.  Captain left autopilot on and the aircraft was slow turning and I didn't take the plane and turn the autopilot off.  Approach called traffic 1;000 ft above on the 27L approach.  We had the traffic and were given an additional turn south; and decent to 4;000 ft and recleared for the Visual for 28C. [Reporter stated a contributing] cause was end of a 4 day trip and we were tired.  Also; we had more discussion in sterile [cockpit] than normal as an aircraft ahead of us had reported a drone approximately 400 ft below them on final.  I was uncertain which aircraft this was relative to our position on the approach; so we were discussing that. [Reporter resolves to] pay closer attention.  Disengage autopilot to correct more quickly.
Report_2_Narrative: I was pilot flying.  We were vectored to the ILS for runway 28C in ORD. We were instructed to join localizer for 28C. I was planning to use LNAV  and VNAV until outside the final approach fix. As we turned to vector to join; I paused before hitting LNAV because usually the aircraft would turn towards course immediately and if you wait it will fly the heading and then join. Approach had us a little too close for that and with 15 knots of wind from south we flew through the course. The FO pointed that out and I turned at same time as approach gave us another heading to join. We were also given a lower altitude. Auto pilot was on. We then joined the localizer and continued the approach. We were vectored close in and with the 15 knot cross wind; pushed through localizer. I needed to do better monitoring of where the course was.

Report_1_Narrative: Several times during my flight I had to inform customer about the company face covering policy. During boarding I informed customer about company face covering policy. After the beverage service while I was picking up used service items I told passenger that his mask needed to cover his nose as well as his mouth. During my cabin security walk throughs; I reminded the passenger about the face covering policy. During seat belt compliance checks I also had to remind customer about company face covering policy. I consulted a member of my crew. The A Flight Attendant was informed and the Captain notified. The Captain made a PA announcement. Then the customer was given a warning. The flight continued with no further incident.

Passengers seated in [row] XX [seats] 2345 were instructed several times by Flight Attendant C to keep their face coverings on at all times. They continued to disregard her request. Please put them in our no fly list. They are the type of passengers that will never follow our travel requirements.

Touch and Go on Runway 35 at Logan Cache airport during takeoff aircraft behind us went around and as we were climbing out they stayed on top of us. we got a traffic alert and immediately started looking for them and once we saw how close they were we made a turn to the right because there was traffic to the left in the pattern and the other aircraft was to the left and behind [us]. No one was injured and no damage occurred.

While working the mid shift; I had Aircraft X inbound to an airport. They requested direct to an IAF for the ILS Approach. I had the approach plate pulled up on our display; issued direct to the fix and gave the aircraft approach clearance with a restriction to cross a fix at or above 10;000 ft. The fix is in a 10;000 ft. Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA) so that cleared the terrain. I missed on the approach that the fix has an altitude of 13;000 ft. in an MIA of 10;000 ft.; the next fix has an altitude of 12;000 ft. in an MIA of 11;000 ft.; and then the next fix has a crossing altitude of 8;200 ft. in a MIA of 9;000 ft. I misinterpreted the information on the approach plate and crossed the aircraft at 10;000 ft. to initiate the approach which was not a safe altitude according to the approach plate or my MIAs as the aircraft descended to 10;000 ft. and proceeded to enter an 11;000 ft. MIA. I believe at this point I had the aircraft switched over to CTAF or shortly after I had the MSAW (Minimum Safe Altitude Warning) alert flashing 11;000 ft. It did not occur to me to double check that the crossing restriction was ok; seeing as how I had cleared the aircraft on the approach.It is not often that we clear aircraft for the ILS from over that fix due to the fact that Approach works the airspace throughout the day and most of the aircraft arriving at this airport on the midshift come from the east; which we usually give them the arc for the approach. We have other approaches in our airspace that have an approach altitude higher than the terrain. This was an assumption on my part that the altitude was ok for the approach. This is not a system wide issue; but a lack of thoroughly reviewing the approach plate on my part to ensure altitudes given are safe for the approach and above the terrain.

I was sequencing Aircraft X to follow behind 2 other aircraft. The vector I put Aircraft X on; put him into a 6400 ft. Minimum Vectoring Altitude and he was level at 6000 ft. When I noticed the error; I vectored him away from terrain; climbed him to 6400 ft.; and gave him a low altitude alert. After the alert; Aircraft X said he had the terrain in sight. I should have spent more time evaluating Aircraft X's heading. Since the winds were pushing aircraft further to the east; having a better vector away from terrain to compensate for the wind would have helped the issue.

Being controlled by Lubbock Approach; we were being vectored for a visual approach. We called the airport in sight; but were not cleared due to a small aircraft 10 miles north of the field flying southeast. We then were about 7 NM NE of the airport flying a 260 heading perpendicular to final approach course. We were notified of the traffic; but never saw them throughout the entire encounter. We were cleared down to 5;000 feet and the small aircraft was to maintain VFR at or below 4;500. The small aircraft  called us in sight and Lubbock Approach told them to maintain visual separation. We were then cleared the visual approach for RWY 17R; and transferred to Tower frequency. After switching to Tower and flying approximately a 45 degree intercept to a 3 NM final; I noticed on TCAS that the small aircraft traffic looked to have turned to fly directly underneath us. At this point traffic was about 2-3 o' clock at 1-2 miles and approximately 500 feet below us. I began calling out the traffic's altitude in reference to ours to my First Officer (Pilot Flying). I also commanded that he leveled off our descent. When passing directly under us according to TCAS the traffic was only 200 feet below us. Then directly off our left only 100 feet below us. During all of this; we somehow DID NOT get a TA or an RA. We continued our visual approach and landed without any irregularities. After arriving at the gate; I called Approach to complain in which they replied he had us in sight and the only amount of separation that is required is just visual separation. Therefore; no regulations were broken. However; I felt unsafe and if a passenger had seen traffic passing below us with minimal separation; it does not reflect well on the company or 121 industry as a whole.

We were departing from Pittsburgh Airport. We took off on runway 28R and were assigned runway heading.Our flight plan first fix was AHTIY (then to BURGS). We briefed the route during preflight and of course verified that the course looked like it should.Shortly after takeoff; we were given a turn to the left to 'eighty' which I know from prior experience is how they pronounce that first fix; AHTIY. We brought that point up in our flight plan; inserted it into the FMS (Flight Management System) direct; agreed; then activated. The turn was a very sharp left. Once we realized we were turning toward the airport and away from our course; we briefly discussed amongst ourselves that the seemed strange. I immediately called up to verify that our clearance was a left turn direct 'eighty' (or AHTIY.) ATC (Air Traffic Control) confirmed but immediately told us to turn right to a heading of 180 'one eighty'. We started a turn to 180 but it only added to the confusion because now I thought that perhaps when I had heard 'eighty' earlier maybe I misunderstood and it was actually a heading of 'one eighty'.Once we were on that heading; we were cleared again 'direct AHTIY'. I read it back very slowly and asked to confirm that we would make a right turn direct to the fix. He said; 'Affirmative... Direct Alpha Hotel Tango India Yankee.' It was then that I realized that the point I had on my flight plan in the FMS had the T & the I switched... AHITY. I proceeded to type in the correct spelling; we agreed and flew toward AHTIY. At this point; I thought that the point AHITY actually existed over the top of Pittsburgh Airport. I found that to be a really strange placement for such a closely spelled GPS (Global Positioning System) fix to AHTIY. Upon beginning this report; I looked at my charts to verify where that fix was located. The fix doesn't seem to exist. I racked my brain to try to figure out how the fix existed in that plane but not on my chart. Then I remembered that pilots can create their own waypoints. Normally; if a fix does not exist; the FMS pulls up an option to create a pilot waypoint. I can tell you with 100% certainty but this did not happen to me when I was entering the flight plan. However; because it took my false waypoint without the prompt to create it; I can deduce that someone created that way point in that database prior to my typing it. At this point; someone must have created a misspelled version of AHTIY as AHITY. It was then that I remembered a detail that I had deemed unimportant prior to this. When we began entering the flight plan; the First Officer was holding the paperwork. He had started to enter the flight plan and then stopped. When I asked if he was doing it or if he wanted me to; he said that he decided to stop and get the ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) first so I could do it. He spelled the first point for me as AHITY and I typed it. I then picked up the other copy of the flight plan and told him I would use that for the routing if he wanted to get the ATIS. I continued with BURGS and so on. I wanted to confirm my suspicions about this waypoint so I looked up the crew for the outbound flight on that aircraft. The First Officer happens to be a friend of mine. I called her and I asked her to pull up the pilot created waypoint list. She did; and sure enough there was a fix called AHITY. I had her take a photo of the coordinates for that point and send it to me. I also asked her to please delete the point from the database. I spoke with my First Officer today about user created waypoints. He says he did not create AHITY. Either way; it was a point in the pilot waypoint database and thus allowed me to use it in my flight plan.An unfortunate series of events seems to have led up to us turning toward a non-existent point. Luckily there was no mention of pilot deviation or loss of separation with any aircraft; but a mistake was still made.In the future; I will make apoint to double check the spelling of all waypoints along the route myself. We did not catch it on our verbal route review because we read it as the first fix despite the 2 letters being switched. It didn't look much different. I have certainly learned a lot from this experience; and will do my best to ensure that this does not happen again to me or anyone that I fly with. The only suggestion I have is perhaps to request that the company send a reminder to the pilot group to refresh everyone about pilot created waypoints. I would emphasize that if you do create a waypoint; you should only do it if you are doing it purposefully and using the coordinates on your chart. Then; after the flight; you should delete that point so that we don't have non-existent points in our database.

Climbing out of ZZZ to FL380. Being very light and a slight wind shift; we went through FL370 at about 3;000 FPM. It went to altitude alert almost immediately before I could use vertical speed to arrest the rate. ATC gave us traffic at FL390 that we had in sight for a minute or so. The TCAS gave us the caution traffic then immediately a RA of level off. We leveled off at FL380 and traffic was at FL390.With our very light loads arresting climb rate needs to happen before a thousand feet.

Departing ZZZ the pilot flying rotated to about 12 degrees. The aircraft was relatively lightly loaded. During the climb below acceleration height the airspeed increased (possibly due to being lightly loaded and current winds). I told the pilot flying to increase pitch; but the airspeed reached around 165 (possibly 166) for around 1 second. The airspeed stayed well below the AFM flaps 15 limit. I contacted the company for clarification on whether or not this required a logbook entry and was advised that this did not require an entry.  This was the last leg of the trip that night after sitting around for several hours. I should have been more proactive in identifying the airspeed trend increase and brought it to the PF's attention sooner.Being more proactive in identifying the airspeed trend would have allowed me to bring it to the PF's attention sooner.

Uniformed crew member was reminded by both flight attendants multiple times to comply with our mask policy and keep her mask over both her nose and mouth. At one point FA B walked by her and reminded her politely to wear her mask; it was off at this point; and the employee responded 'clearly I'm drinking this water; thank you.' Unfortunately the passenger was not drinking water when she was reminded or else FA B would have not said anything to her. The employee was given a warning at this point; after both FAs reminded her at least one more time. Totaling 6 times she was kindly reminded. The Flight Deck was notified after the warning was given and the passenger complied after the warning was given.
Report_2_Narrative: A uniformed flight crew passenger would not comply with our company mask policy for either FA's. We both asked her three times to comply with our mask policy. And our next step was to check one last time to see without asking her again if she complied on her own; and she had not. We then issued her a warning; a fresh mask and ask if she had any more questions or excuses. Then she complied the rest of the flight.

Report_1_Narrative: Passengers in X3; X4 and Y3; Y4 were non-compliant with wearing masks. They kept taking the masks off and we had to give them a warning. The passengers continued to take the face masks off. We told the Captain and he informed Dispatch. We tried to give them a few chances; but they just continued being non-compliant.

I repeatedly asked Employee X to place his mask over his mouth and nose at least 10 times.  Even the Lead Cleaner asked him to comply and he refused. The Captain came off the flight and asked him; he said he can't breathe with it on and still refused to cover his mouth and nose. Then he put it somewhat over his mouth; but would not cover his nose. He totally ignored his Lead Supervisor and both myself and the Captain.

Upon landing the aircraft yawed to the right and I corrected to the left. Upon the loss of control I aborted the landing. Upon climb out the left wheel hit a runway sign. While I was climbing out the airplane rolled to the left and I decided to land in a field off the end of the runway. The left wing impacted the ground first and rolled the plane around the nose coming to a stop upright. My friend and I exited the aircraft.

Upon beginning the take-off roll it seemed as if I had full power from the engine but the aircraft did not seem to be accelerating normally. I decided to abort the takeoff and reduced power. While slowing down the aircraft veered sharply to the right and full left rudder did not seem to be effective. I rolled into the dirt between the runways and at the last second the aircraft nosed over causing the propeller to strike the dirt. There was little other damage to the aircraft. Upon further reflection; I believe that the 'brake hold lever' was the reason for this incident. While waiting for takeoff I used the brake hold lever to maintain my position in line. When it was my turn to take-off I taxied seemingly normally onto the runway (position and hold) and then was released for take-off. Prior to the aircraft being towed off the dirt area I checked the brake hold lever and it was still 'On.' I believe that the brake hold lever was probably unevenly holding more right brake than left causing uncontrollable veering to the right. The labels for the brake hold lever are not easily visible from the pilot or co-pilot seats so it is easy to forget which position is 'On' and which is 'Off.' Moving the pedals a certain amount should automatically release the brake hold or give the pilot a warning.

40 minutes out of ZZZ near Abilene TX; received ATC transponder 1 and 2 Fail. Followed by a GNS 1 and 2 Fail. Reported event to ATC and Maintenance Control; followed QRH procedures. Another aircraft in the area reported same thing. ATC suspected GPS jamming event. GPS military jamming.

Captain called to advise that because there are both live animal and dry ice on board they must run air conditioning pack which is a 1.1 % drag/overburn. He advised same issue happened out of ZZZ and delay incurred on both flights. I advised him I would report this issue; since there is no alert generated from weight/balance system to flight planning like there would be in dry [ice] supplemental procedures and/or carp messages. This scenario will continue to occur. I advised him I have to recalculate flight plan with 1.1 % over burn and changed payload since it is lighter than original plan. Sent mini flight plan. The cause is no alert program within weight/balance system to notify dispatcher in advance. I suggested to have the it people with weight/balance and flight planning create a fix (similar to dry ice supplemental procedures and captain report).

During vectors into OKC from the east; the Approach Controller assigned us Runway 17R differently from the one we had setup (17L). After re-configuring for the ILS 17R; I asked the Pilot Monitoring to brief the approach so I can concentrate on flying (Approximately 14;000 feet MSL and descending to 4;000 feet). During the brief a bright shining white light; like a spotlight shined at us; caught our attention and cause a distraction. We still conducted the briefing; including the highest elevation figure. As I kept flying I was paying attention if the light shined us again; in the event it was a laser that needed to be reported. I had the terrain page setup from the previous briefing and both pilots were aware of the obstacle northeast of the airport (Antenna 2758 feet MSL). After the briefing was complete; we were given a vector (a 90 degree angle) for the approach within a close proximity of the Final Approach Fix (minimum altitude at FAF 3;000 feet MSL). This vector would have flown us south of the obstacle depicted in the plan view section of the approach chart. Approximately 18NM from the airport the Approach Controller queried the visual contact of the airport. Pilot Monitoring told me he had contact with it and after I picked it up in sight I asked to call for it if he still had it. At that point the Approach Controller cleared us for the visual approach for [Runway] 17R. Slightly after; the Approach Controller notified us of traffic on the approach to the parallel runway. Both pilots were trying to ID the traffic since it was below our altitude and we had to cross his approach path. We seemed clear of the traffic; but a strong wind from the south was drifting us north which cause a concern for us due to the known obstacle. The aircraft was just approaching 4;000 feet of altitude and started to level off; when I set 3;000n in the Altitude Select box. The aircraft transitioned into a shallow descent instead of holding 4;000 since it wasn't fully captured. While having a discussion about the wind I adjusted my heading and kept the descent to 3;000 feet so we could be close to level and slow when we intercepted the course outside of the FAF. Around 3500 feet we both remembered the obstacles when we saw its lights ahead and below us. I already started a transition into a climb when the Pilot Monitoring verbally reminded me of the obstacles. During the climb we got a 'Caution Obstacle'; due to the projected descent; which was removed shortly since we were already compensating. We were 500 to 700 feet above the highest obstacle. The obstacle represented on the MFD was not the highest elevation figure on the IAP chart. This depicted obstacle was part of another group of towers not depicted on the chart but they can be seen on the VFR chart in the Jeppesen app. Since it was a visual approach; I had focused on maintaining the airport inside; and started to descend and slow down so we could be on a good position for the stable approach. Following the event; we landed with no issues and had a discussion at the gate on how we could have mitigated our error. We were below the MSA and had loss awareness of the obstacles due to other distractions and the tight vector to the approach with the visual approach. Several factors played key roles for the loss of situational awareness that led us to be at a lower altitude for our location. Flags:-A night Visual approach into an airport were an obstacle is a threat.-A night Visual approach to an airport were the elevation of the airport is higher than we are normally used to.-An arrival consisting of an extended base into a visual approach rather than a downwind arrival.-The change of runway that led to a new setup and briefing.-The distractions caused by the wind-drift; traffic; and the bright light.-The tight vector given and accepted for the original approach. Our error was the premature descent to 3000 feet with the goal of not being high once we intercept the final course with short distance to the final fix. Although the setup for the approach included the awareness of terrain and using the terrain displayed as an aid; we should have use TEM to establish a plan to avoid the premature descent. A briefed plan of using the last assigned altitude of 4;000 feet until intercepting would have avoided this. During the approach brief; I should have asked my Pilot Monitoring to intervene in the event I set a lower altitude while maneuvering for the visual (mitigation plan in the vent of losing SA). I realized that our briefings cover informational items that could be threats but we should discuss mitigating plans for them. Other actions that could have been taken are: 1) denying the visual at night due to the obstacle; 2) requesting a vector for the visual; and 3) querying if a lower altitude was needed which would have led the Controller to keep us at 4;000 feet.
Report_2_Narrative: I was Pilot Monitoring. Approximately 20min out we were giving the option to change our landings intentions from [Runway] 17L to 17R which we took. Captain asked me if I could brief the approach which I did and then we performed the runway change items from the checklist. During this briefing moment we both noticed a bright light from about a 12 o'clock position which caught our attention. Captain was aware of the obstacles to the northeast of the airport (antennas) and turned on the terrain. At approximately 18 miles out we were asked if we had the airport in sight. We both saw it and were cleared for the visual approach Runway 17R. Winds were out of the south at approximately 26kt pushing us to the north since we were coming from the east. We were at 4;000 ft. and began a gradual descent. ATC advised us of traffic at 11 o'clock landing on the parallel runway. Looking for the traffic we began to noticed the red lights of the obstacles (towers) on the northeast of the airport. I pointed them out as [did] the Captain. The altitude window was set for 3;000 ft. we were currently at 3;400 ft. Captain turned off the autopilot and began a climb. As we were climbing we received a Caution for the obstacle but it cleared quickly since the Captain had already began the climb. Once cleared we continue to intercept the final approach course and perform a normal landing. We both had a long discussion after the flight and both agreed that we should had mitigated this by simply staying at 4;000ft when cleared for the visual approach since we knew about the MSA which is 3;800 ft. Also the parallel traffic played a role on this since we started looking for it due to it being in our path for intercepting the final approach course of [Runway] 17R. As a Pilot Monitoring I should had advised the Captain that selecting 3;000 ft. and continuing the descent once cleared for the visual would had put us close to the highest obstacle stated in the approach plate. I will keep this in consideration for future flights to come ensuring we are not at a lower altitude than the MSA not established on a final approach course as well as keeping in mind obstacles around us at all times.

Report_1_Narrative: Tower was closed at Reading and I was about to takeoff from Runway 13. I knew a Citation had just landed on Runway 36 so I announced I was taking off Runway 13 twice; once as I was entering the runway and once when I started the takeoff roll. I noticed the Citation on Taxiway Delta as I was in the takeoff roll. The Citation continued to cross Runway 13 as I was taking off. I aborted the takeoff and got off on Taxiway Foxtrot. The Citation then called and announced they were clear of the runway.  Be aware aircraft may not be on the right frequency at RDG and/or may not be making announcements.

The mission was a short flight. Aircraft was level at 3;000 feet after initial climb when clearance was received to climb to 5;000 feet.  I thought I heard 9;000 feet and both of us failed to confirm with each other due to short communication from person in jump seat.  I began a rather aggressive climb to exit the turbulence and detected command bars moving downward as I passed 5;100 feet.  I reduced power and returned to assigned altitude while verifying clearance.  Aircraft reached 5;450 feet.  Total time above 5;000 was approximately 8-10 seconds.  No communication from ATC or action required.Due to COVID-19 slowdown I had not flown in 4 weeks and my last flight before that was 5 weeks before that.  Clearly flight discipline suffered from lack of recent experience and teamwork.  I allowed a distraction to prevent me from completing an altitude readback with PNF and neither one of us performed the '1;000 feet to go' call prior to reaching assigned altitude.

I cannot count how many times recently the pilots are not wearing masks. I was sitting up front for my flight to ZZZ when the flight deck came out of cockpit to find a seat. No one wearing a mask. One was but around his chin.  I moved to a different Zone.  Then additional pilots came on; no masks. It's either our policy or not. I do not feel it is my place as a FA to point this out to pilots.  I know quite a few crews are getting sick. It makes me very uneasy to fly in these conditions.  I have family members I return home to.  This is not limited to pilots even though it's very prevalent.  Some FAs will also take off masks when passengers leave. It is alarming and I feel a very unsafe workplace. We can't have 1/2 the workforce adhering to a serious policy regarding a pandemic and 1/2 not. The precautions such as masks are only effective if there is 100% compliance.  I do not want to wear a mask for hours; sometimes 20+; but I do.  [Cause is a] total disregard for policy and others safety. Safety and security is either important to the company or not. My suggestion would be a no exceptions policy or we will continue to see a rise in sick flight crews.

Flight Attendant (FA) B came to me to let me know that the passenger seated in X1 was repeatedly reminded to put on his mask appropriately over his nose and mouth.  He would do it and then later remove it. He was told at least 5 times before the crew let me know that they had each told the passenger to comply.  As the only FA who had not approached the passenger I went up to ask him to comply with our mask policy.  After I left the passenger; the crew let me know that he took it off again and that other passengers around him were asking to make sure he was wearing his mask.  I then went to grab one of our on board masks and policy document to give to him.  When giving him one of our masks he grabbed it; put it on and then closed his eyes and proceeded to go to sleep. I tried to give him an explanation of the policy document but he proceeded to ignore me.  After beginning service FA D and FA C saw him again not complying.  As a result; the flight deck was notified by FA A of the non-compliant passenger.  FA A also went to speak to the passenger one more time to make sure he was aware of our mask policy and what was being provided by the policy document.Make sure the passenger is spoken to about our mask policy.

I was training in Aircraft X; doing pattern work; and while on final to [Runway] 7R; I was looking out to my left and saw an Aircraft Y starting his turn from downwind to final for 7L. We originally were offset to the right as a precaution for traffic on 7L. As I was getting closer to the runway; I looked back again and saw that the Aircraft Y overshot his base to final turn and was on path to hit us (noticed him about 200-300 feet away). I took evasive action and went full power and a sharp turn to the right to get out of his path. About 3 seconds later he intercepted our path and if not for evasive maneuver; we would've had a mid-air collision.

[We] departed AUO to complete a Commercial Pilot Stage Check. The objective was to complete a practice ILS approach to Runway 36 at AUO; with simulated vectors to the final approach course. The private; instrument rated pilot was the pilot flying (PF). He was wearing view limiting 'foggles'. The aircraft is equipped with an ADS-B traffic awareness system; displayed on the MFD. We were established on the localizer for Runway 36 at approximately 9 miles from the runway. The PF made his initial radio call on CTAF that we were 9 mile south on the ILS Runway 36. There was one other aircraft approximately 3 miles ahead of us; on final; on the ILS as well. We were approximately a 6 mile final when [Aircraft Y] reported 10 miles straight in for Runway 36. We subsequently made another distance call to alert the Aircraft Y of our location. By this time; we had the aircraft on the ADS-B display. That aircraft was at a higher altitude but substantially faster than our final approach speed of 90 KIAS. We had no visual contact with the Aircraft Y; our only reference was the track on our MFD. When he called 5 mile final; I asked over CTAF if he intended to overfly us on final. The response was; 'we will sidestep to the left'. When the ADS-B showed +300 and very close behind us; I stated to the student 'I have the flight controls' and descended and banked right. The pilot of the jet stated that he had us in sight no factor. We then saw the Aircraft Y slightly below and to our left as he passed by. The other aircraft ahead of us called low approach only and went around. We sidestepped to the right and climbed back to pattern altitude on an upwind leg. The Aircraft Y landed without further incident.

I was told by our company Chief Pilot; my 61.58 check-ride had lapsed after I had been flying PIC for several flights in our aircraft under Part 91. I was told I had operated the aircraft as PIC outside of my 61.58 requirements. I had received a check-ride which I was told was for my PIC currency under 61.58 with our Chief Pilot/Check Airman. My company says they have no record of a signoff of my completion of the 61.58 flight during my 2 month COVID-19 SFAR put forth by the FAA. I was told by my Chief Pilot I am illegally operating as PIC 3 months after I had been evaluated after the check-ride. My usual check-ride month is X; and fell under the SFAR time period and our training facility was essentially closed due to COVID-19; so an in-house check-ride to satisfy my 61.58 requirements was performed; within the SFAR grace period specifications. Apparently my company never logged/lost the flight training records and put me in a situation where I am in violation of 61.58. In the near future my company is going to perform another in-house check-ride with the same Chief Pilot/Check Airman to satisfy my 61.58 requirements. In the meantime I will not be performing any PIC duties going forward until my new signoff is complete.I had understood my 61.58 requirements were to be met that day; so I continued to operate our aircraft as PIC under normal circumstances. It wasn't until I was to be contracted out by my company to ferry an aircraft that this clerical error was discovered in my records. I have a log book entry of this flight occurring and there is company records of this flight occurring as well as a training flight. My company should be held accountable for any issues that arise from this in the future as I performed the check-ride to satisfaction and was under the false assumption that my Check Airman followed through with his paperwork for company records. Going forward; I will now require a copy of my training record for my own personal files.

I am writing this report to report a near miss situation in the standard traffic pattern for Runway 27L at Gillespie Field. My student and I were practicing traffic pattern operations at SEE. After checking upwind ; crosswind; downwind; clear we made a left turn into the pattern for Runway 27L. When my student looked to check the distance from aircraft to [the] runway; he also looked back and noticed that there was another aircraft right on our tail - same altitude; less than 100 feet behind us - same altitude. Tower advised that [he] was too close to us and told them to offset to the south for spacing. Aircraft Y offset to the south towards our 5 o'clock position and we got cleared to land #2 for Runway 27L.

I was with my student in initial climb out for Runway 21. We were roughly 1200 feet MSL. We made all the proper calls. Winds were favoring Runway 21. When we were 1200 feet MSL; a Piper made a short final call for Runway 3. The Piper did not make any calls before that. We had to turn to avoid them. It was a really really close encounter.

Aircraft X was on heading 360 and instructed to descend to 6;000 ft.  I observed Aircraft X descend through 6;000 and at 5;800 I issued a low altitude alert; the MVA was 5;600.  Aircraft X did not respond and kept descending.  I issued another low altitude alert; climb and maintain 6;000 immediately and when their data block displayed 5;100 they reported level at 6;000.  I told them altitude indicates 5;100; they asked to verify altimeter 30.25 and I gave them the current altimeter 29.24.  After a couple miles they showed a climb and climbed to 6;000.It was apparent watching the replay that Aircraft X check on indicating 12;100 and reporting level at 13;000.  For some reason I did not catch it.  I even descended them to 8;000 and they leveled at an indicated 7;000 and I did not notice.  I think this is because 7;000 is a normal altitude that I would descend them to at that location but I had left them a little higher.  When I descended them to 6;000 and they indicated lower it was apparent something was wrong and I was immediately concerned about the MVA so I issued a low altitude alert before they went below the MVA and a climb to 6;000.A snowstorm had just blown through the airspace and there was still stiff winds out of the west gusting to 30 kts.  I had a sequence developing for ZZZ and Aircraft X was near the front of it.  I recall planning ahead; looking at strips; using the STARS (Standard Terminal Automation Replacement System) to determine how much the wind was pushing the vectors.  Also ZZZ had many taxiways closed from the snow so I was vectoring for much greater distance than usual (10 miles on final).  I must not have looked at Aircraft X's altitude when they checked on and just used the reported altitude.  While I am happy I got a low altitude alert out when they were near terrain; I wish that I had caught it on check-on.I found it strange that their altitude in STARS was accurate but not in the cockpit.  I thought the altimeter setting would change what STARS reported. I have to be diligent to verify altitudes when the aircraft check on; so altimeter discrepancies can be caught early.
Report_2_Narrative: Descending into ZZZ through FL 180 we completed the Approach Checklist and verified 30.24 altimeter setting. On vectors to final as we were leveling off; Approach Control asked us to climb to 6;000 ft. We were indicating 6;300 at that time; so the clearance just sounded odd. The Approach Controller said that we were at 5;200 and for us to climb to 6;000 and turn to a heading of 030. I immediately clicked the autopilot/auto-throttles off and started my turn towards 030 heading and started climbing; as my Pilot Monitoring verbalized that we had the wrong altimeter setting of 30.24 instead of 29.24. We leveled off at 6;000 on a 030 heading; and at that time I engaged the autopilot and auto-throttles. We got vectored for approach and landed uneventfully on Runway XX. We were asked to contact TRACON on landline by the Ground Controller as we taxied to the gate. We realized that we had unintentionally set altimeter setting of 30.24 in error instead of 29.24 which directly led to the altitude deviation. We failed to catch our error during crosschecks as we ran the checklist due to expectation bias. Lack of digital ATIS was also a contributing factor; however; we should have trapped this error as we updated the new weather. We needed to be more vigilant to trap these kind of errors.

Report_1_Narrative: As the pandemic is surging to record levels again; I feel compelled to highlight an ongoing and now seemingly institutionally entrenched intentional violation of basic company safety policy regarding mask wear in the ZZZ base pilot lounge.  According to the policy letter from management; employees are required to wear face coverings in crew bases whenever not actively eating; drinking; or taking medicine.  This does not occur with the vast majority of employees I observe regularly regardless of time of day; number of occupants; lack or not of social distancing; and is an ongoing situation.  I have even heard some say they spend more time in the lounge these days in order to avoid the gate/terminal areas where other work group members might express the need for compliance.  The ZZZ base pilot lounge is seen by nearly everyone as a 'safe space' to willfully flaunt company face covering policy and could easily end up being a major source of COVID-19 transmission; resulting in increased sick calls; company health expenses; or even employee deaths.This starts at the top.  I can't speak about other bases; but there is no messaging; signage; or even leadership by good example by base leadership for this simplest and most effective of pandemic mitigations.  If company policy is meant to actually have a positive effect and not be simply lip service; leadership needs to inform and demand compliance and show leadership by demonstrating compliance.  This needs to be standard across ALL employee work spaces and leadership must be held accountable.  I refuse to spend any more time than absolutely necessary in my company workspace of the pilot lounge because it jeopardizes my health and that of my co-workers and family.

After all my pre-flight duties were complete; the Flight Attendant offered me a cup of coffee; which I took; and removed my mask to drink it; I'm pretty sure I also removed my mask on flight xxx after all of my pre-flight duties were complete to consume coffee also.

Forgot to submit my COVID assessment prior to the first leg in the morning.  I did the assessment and became distracted through my preparation to begin my day and forgot to submit my COVID assessment.  I submitted my assessment once the first flight segment was completed.[Cause was being] distracted and not following personal reminding procedures.  Maybe there is a way that we can acknowledge that we have performed the COVID assessment in the same way we acknowledge that we are FIT for Duty.  This would make a normal part of the procedures we already have in place.

We were cleared for the visual approach Runway 17 into Asheville. We were at 6000 feet coming in from the southeast and we had the GPS runway 17 loaded in the FMS. The Captain set 4000 ft in the Altitude Selector and we looked at the GPS plate confirmed 4000 ft at FAF and saw the highest obstacle of 4368 ft to our right in the distance.  We were VMC at night and we saw the higher terrain to the right of us and we saw [lower] terrain in front of us. We both thought we could get through with the [lower] terrain ahead. As we crossed over the [lower] terrain we got the GPWS 'terrain' and pull up. The Captain quickly clicked off the autopilot and arrested the descent at 4700 ft MSL. No limitations were exceeded as we were at Flap 8.  We continued on the approach and landed without further incident. We saw the higher terrain to our left which I pointed out and we both agreed we could set the FAF altitude and descend. The Captain even slowed his descent to 0.9 from 1.5 as we crossed the terrain. We may have been too focused on the highest obstacle and higher terrain. We clicked off the autopilot and I called out the altitude we were at cleared his FD. We continued since we arrested the descent and no further incident took place. Next time I will suggest to wait until we cross over the terrain before descending from 6000 feet.

We briefed the route and departure; to include the [engine failure] procedures. We confirmed the route in the FMS. After being cleared for takeoff and reaching 400 AGL; We turned to heading 189 and hit moderate or greater turbulence and mountain waves; which significantly affected us. I had trouble communicating with ATC and performing cockpit duties due to turbulence. Just before reaching 1;000 AGL; Tower said switch to departure. Upon contacting departure we were told 'upon reaching 8;000; proceed direct to a fix'; but the radio quality of the transmission was poor and we couldn't clearly hear the name of the fix. I realized the mountain was on our path and for an immediate left turn to avoid terrain. The Spokane Departure Controller cleared us direct to a fix by name but not the identifier or phonetic spelling; which I couldn't understand due to significant turbulence and poor radio quality. We continued normal flap retraction callouts and takeoff profile. We were given heading a 120; to which we turned; and it took several radio calls between the Captain and ATC to get clarification on the identifier to which we were cleared. This was due to scratchy radios; the turbulence; and some possible confusion on the part of the controller who was talking with us and another company aircraft. We were mistakenly trying to navigate to ENY VOR which caused us to be confused and task saturated. Once we got the correct identifier for HIA and entered into the FMS; we proceeded on course as cleared. However this delay resulted in us temporarily deviating from the charted procedure. This caused the controller to get a low altitude alert although we did not receive any terrain avoidance warnings from our aircraft. In retrospect; the controller admitted on our phone conversation afterwards that this situation is very common. There are a lot of risks and stressors associated with this MZULA.MSO RWY12 and I feel like our outside resources did not help the flight crew. We accept full responsibility for allowing this situation to develop; but changes to this procedure will greatly increase safety in the future.First our Jeppesen charts had no verbal narrative and left us needing FAA charts to understand the procedure. It would be a great help if the procedure could be simplified or the controller's expectations that are outside the procedure could be communicated before we hit significant turbulence. The controller was clearly frustrated with us which only made our situation worse by trying to give us a phone number during a high stress and high workload phase of flight in significant turbulence. This shows the controllers are not fully aware of what human factors the flight crews have and how to help safety of flight. Having poor radio transmission quality was also a significant factor.
Report_2_Narrative: I was the First Officer and Pilot Flying departing MSO. I received and read back the clearance from ATC which included the MZULA5 Departure MSO transition. I programmed this into our FMS and the Captain and I thoroughly reviewed the route in the FMS and on our Jeppesen plates. We also briefed the procedure for engine failure departure. In my takeoff briefing I again described the departure we were assigned. After being cleared for takeoff and reaching 400 AGL; I turned to heading 189 as charted. Immediately we began to encounter moderate turbulence. Just before reaching 1;000 ft AGL; Tower said switch to Departure. Upon contacting Departure we were told 'upon reaching 8;000; proceed direct to ________ ' but it was very turbulent and we did not clearly hear the name of the fix. I immediately realized the flight director was not commanding a left turn in accordance with the charted procedure. The Captain asked to say again the fix; and the controller said the name but not the identifier or phonetic spelling. At 1;000 ft AGL; I called out 'flight level change'; followed by the normal flap retraction callouts and told him we needed a left turn since I knew from the charted SID that's what we were supposed to do. Meanwhile the Captain was trying to get clarification on the fix to which we were to proceed. We knew it would be a left turn so he asked for a left turn and we were given heading 120; to which we turned. During this entire time it was very turbulent and took a lot of effort and concentration to focus on safe flying. It took several radio calls between the Captain and ATC to get clarification on the identifier to which we were cleared. This was due to scratchy radios; the turbulence; and some possible confusion on the part of the controller who was talking with us and another company aircraft. Once we got the correct identifier and entered it into the FMS; we proceeded on course as cleared. However; this delay resulted in us temporarily deviating from the charted procedure. During this time; we had terrain radar displayed on our MFD and were well aware of our surroundings and the location of rising terrain; and at no time did we ever get a terrain caution or warning.Although the SID was correctly programmed into the FMS; for unknown reasons the Flight Director did not command the left turn to intercept the MSO 159 radial. The extreme turbulence and difficulty understanding ATC instructions caused a delay in turning to the assigned fix; resulting in a course deviation. First of all; given our route of flight; it would have made much more sense if; in the pre-departure clearance; we would have been given the Salmon transition. This fact was even stated by the ATC supervisor on the phone when I called him after we landed. He further stated they have had frequent problems with pilots and aircraft not following the procedure we were assigned. Second; I could have anticipated a potential issue; and tuned the MSO VOR in NAV 1 and 2; along with the radial to intercept prior to takeoff. If I return to MSO in the future; I will definitely do that as a backup in case this kind of thing should happen again. I realized we were supposed to be turning left; which is why we asked for a left turn in the interim while trying to get the identifier for the fix and get that loaded in the FMS. The significant turbulence and the communication difficulties with ATC were definitely contributing factors in this temporary deviation. Nonetheless; as a pilot it is my responsibility to safely and correctly fly the plane; so I will ensure I have a backup navigation source programmed and ready in the future. And if I go back there again and get assigned that same SID; I will ask for the Salmon transition rather than the MSO transition; as suggested by the ATC supervisor. Finally I will brief my Captain on what happened previously so we can be vigilant and prepared.

Report_1_Narrative: While approaching ITH from the south; we had a visual on the airport and were cleared for the visual. I turned away from the FAF which was our last point of direct navigation as assigned by ATC. I slowed the airplane and configured for flaps 9. As we got closer I was heading for a 4 to 5 mile intercept of the final approach course and began a slow descent from 4000 ft to 3000 ft; the final approach [fix] altitude. I set 3000 ft and began a 500 fpm descent. As we approached 3200 ft we got an EGPWS warning 'Terrain'. I disconnected the autopilot then added max thrust and began a climb; I climbed to MSA for the area; 3700 ft. The First Officer and I felt that we were still in a position to continue the visual into ITH and did so. All precautions were taken; the flight operations quality alert and terrain were discussed both on the departure briefing and the arrival briefing. We briefed the tallest obstacles; their location and the rising terrain. We were slowed to 180 kts flaps 9 and used a slow descent rate to avoid such alerts. Performed the escape maneuver and leveled at MSA. Next time I will ask to just begin the approach from OCCIE fix rather than attempting the visual.
Report_2_Narrative: During visual approach on RWY 32 Pilot Flying (PF) turned away from FAF .The airplane [was] slowed and configured for flaps 9. The airplane was on left base about 5 miles before intercepting the final approach segment. [Captain] began slow descent from 4000 ft to 3000 ft with VS 500 fpm. At 3200 ft we gotten EGPWS warning 'Terrain '. PF immediately followed escape maneuver for EGPWS and climbed to 3700 ft. After that we continued visual for landing. During departure briefing and approach briefing we following all SOP's. During approach we briefed for tallest obstacles. We used slow descent rate to avoid such alert.

Report_1_Narrative: We had just switched on to approach frequency and were given a heading and altitude for the visual approach. We both heard the altitude clearance as 5;000 ft. and the PM read back 5;000 ft. A few min after we had leveled off at 5;000 ft. ATC asked us to confirm altitude and gave us the altimeter. We responded with 5;000 ft. and verified we had the correct altimeter. ATC then responded the minimum vectoring altitude was 5;300 ft. for that area. As the PM (Pilot Monitoring) attempted to clarify we read back 5000 ft. in the initial clearance; I started a climb to 5;300 ft. ATC then asked if we had the field in sight which we did and then we were cleared for the visual approach using the GPS 14 approach plate as guidance. We landed normally.The cause of this event was a miscommunication with ATC. We both heard 5;000 ft. in the initial clearance not 5;300 ft. The PM read back 5;000 ft. and we didn't hear a correction from ATC. A contributing factor in my opinion was the fact that we were experiencing light to occasional moderate turbulence for most of the flight including the descent and that could have caused us to miss hear the original clearance and potentially the read back was hard for ATC to hear due to the PM's mic being jostled.In cases such as this we could have requested clarification if the altitude didn't sound correct but in my estimation the clearance seemed to make sense based on our position and the airport elevation and the location of obstacles and terrain which were not a factor from the direction we were arriving and they also seemed to be similar to the altitudes we would expect if on the GPS approach we were using as a backup. But it would be prudent in the future especially in turbulence to verify clearances considering the effect turbulence could have on radio transmissions.
Report_2_Narrative: We had just checked in with approach and they gave use a heading to turn to for vectors and an altitude to descend to. I thought I had remembered reading backing 5;000 ft. as the captain who was the PF put 5;000 ft. in the FMA and we both confirmed the 5;000 ft. once set. After reaching 5;000 ft. the controller asked us to verify our altitude and I read back 5;000 ft. The controlled returned with 'the MDA is 5;300 in that sector do you have the field in sight?' We had the field in sight but the question led us to believe we descended to the wrong altitude. The controller didn't clarify the altitude at this point and said were cleared for the visual using the RNAV GPS 14 approach plate for guidance.The cause of the event was a miscommunication with ATC and I could have possibly read back a different altitude and thought that 5;000 ft. was what he said instead; after we had set 5000 ft. Also; we were in light to moderate turbulence during the entire flight and it might have caused me to mishear the controller or misread the altitude.I should have asked for clarification from the controller after we had set the altitude in the FMA. From where we were; the altitude of 5000 ft. made sense so it did not cue me to ask for clarification. We had briefed the approach plate as well and the higher rising terrain was on the other side of the field and we determined it would not be a factor for the altitude of 5000 ft. Where we were seemed to coincide with the altitudes on the approach plate as well.

Report_1_Narrative: After passing waypoint GILLL on the LINKK1 arrival into IAH; we were cleared runway 27 ILS approach. The next waypoint on the flight was RDFSH then GEYGR. GEYGR was to be passed at 3000' while we were around 3600'. Being cleared the ILS approach; we were to comply with crossing restrictions that were still in place on the approach. The restriction was noted on the approach as a small note; and we announced it too late. The approach controller alerted us high on the crossing restriction and we were then able to comply with the rest of the crossing restrictions. After being swapped runways late in an arrival; we should still review all notes and restrictions applicable to the approach. The note indications are easy to miss; but I am well aware now of specific crossing restrictions instead of waypoint floors on an approach. I believe it would be beneficial to have these notes and restrictions in different colors or noted right below the waypoint name to make it more obvious to the pilots. The constant change in runway assignments late in arrival makes it easy to miss small annotations on approach plates that are important for traffic separation.

I arrived at the aircraft as relief pilot an hour before takeoff for a cargo flight to ZZZZ. The FO was at the aircraft and had completed much of the cockpit preflight. Because it was a cargo flight I went through the cabin and secured the overhead bins and many of the storage /catering carts. The Captain arrived and we went through the standard cockpit flight preparation prior to departure. After we pushed from the gate and initiated the engine start; I realized I had not done the Pre-Fight Walk Around Inspection. I asked the FO if he had completed it and he said he had not. He thought I was doing that when I was actually securing the cabin for departure. I told the Captain I forgot to do the walk around. The Captain got clearance and we turned around on the ramp and went back into the gate. The Agent came back; hooked up the jet bridge and I went out and completed the walk around inspection. We re-accomplished all the pre-departure checks and ended up departing to ZZZZ about XX minutes late. Most of the delay was a result of getting personnel back to park us and recalling another push crew.I think that because it was a cargo flight; I was possibly distracted by some non-standard items in the cabin and got out of my habit pattern. Like most of us; I haven't been flying too much but the bottom line is that I simply forgot to do the walk around preflight and realized it after the push.In the future I will report to the FO that the walk around has been completed and as the FO I will verify with the relief pilot that it has been complete. I have been flying airplanes for XX years and have never forgotten to do a walk around inspection.
Report_2_Narrative: During the pushback from the gate; the flight crew realized a communication error occurred resulting in the non completion of the pre-flight walk around duties by a pilot. A return to a gate commenced to complete the required tasks. Contributing factors may have been one crew member being newly experienced to three-four pilot crew tasks combined with a multi month period of not working. Making the crew cross information in this specific area a mandatory portion of any preflight operations area briefing; crew text preflight contact; or aircraft boarding process.

Report_1_Narrative: Let me say to begin I am not familiar with Flight Check. By this I mean I never came across it in any of my FAA materials or non-FAA reading; online; or video materials. It was not on any of my private; instrument; or commercial FAA exams. It was never brought up to me on any flight checks or check rides. I have never seen anything written about it in any online materials. No CFI or CFII ever mentioned it to me. I have never seen Flight Check aircraft at my home airport or any other airport I've ever flown into. Yesterday I experienced a conflict with a Flight Check aircraft. Just after I made my routine call for my pattern entry '...6 miles to the northeast inbound for a left downwind...'; a Flight Check aircraft made the call they were on an approach to the same runway as me and that they would need to drive inbound on the final until just short of the approach end for on an obstacle check. We're looking for inbound traffic. Other calls by various aircraft as well as Flight Check and my aircraft were made. One of the flight check calls was stepped on however and was therefore not audible. What is important to note too; is that there was an aircraft 2 to 3 miles behind me following me in. Because Flight Check and I were on an entry for the same runway in opposite directions I felt the situation was beginning to become dangerous; I considered doing a 360 turn at 6 miles out just to give spacing to the Flight Check aircraft coming in from the wrong direction. However; I did not do this because there was an aircraft directly behind me and I felt a 360 might put me into him. Therefore; for safety reasons I decided against it. I felt the safest course of action was to proceed on because I felt a King Air at 6 miles out was going to arrive much sooner than I was at 6 miles out because of his faster speed and because he was doing a straight in and I was doing a standard approach. Additionally; he said he was not going to land but instead 'break off just short of the approach end...'. Lastly; he said he was 'looking for inbound aircraft' which I felt meant he was aware of me and would divert if needed. I also briefly considered the option of extending my downwind but felt that to be unsafe because other aircraft were in the area and I had lost track of exactly where everybody was due to the constant traffic chatter both for ZZZ and other nearby airports. It was becoming stressful and my goal was to fly the aircraft; look out the window; and either land or see and avoid this other aircraft. As our approaches proceeded and I turned final I could see an oncoming aircraft above me ascending and turning south as I was descending to land. As I was about 50 feet off the runway I received a tongue lashing from the Flight Check aircraft and my landing was hard and it took several moments for me to calm myself after parking. Only after going into the Fixed Base Operator and asking about what a Flight Check aircraft was did I understand what had just happened. I now understand; after I did my own investigation; what Flight Check is and what I should do if I encounter a Flight Check aircraft in the future. I will now avoid Flight Check if I ever encounter one. I will advise other pilots of what Flight Check is and to avoid them. What caused the problem and what can be done to prevent a recurrence or correct the problem.The problem was caused by me not knowing what Flight Check is and that they have the authority to fly wherever they want and however they want. I will stay away from all Flight Check aircraft in the future. Flight Checks should be included in NOTAMS. I checked NOTAMS prior to my departure and there existed no such NOTAM regarding Flight Check aircraft that day. ATC; such as Approach did not advise me of any Flight Check aircraft. Flight Check pilots should include such wording as; 'This is the Federal Aviation Administration advising all aircraft to avoid (airport name) until (time).' This should be repeated over the appropriate frequency every 1 to 2 minutes until Flight Check is clear of the area. Include Flight Check information in FAA teaching materials. Include Flight Check information in FAA testing materials. Flight Check pilots should avoid berating pilots on frequency especially just prior to them landing as this can cause an unsafe distraction.

I am a flight instructor I was on the ground observing the traffic pattern. I had just sent my student on his first solo flight. There were three Citabrias in the pattern; all same company. My solo student was Aircraft X. Aircraft Z was on ½ mile final. Aircraft Y was on an extended downwind. My student; Aircraft X; was abeam midfield in the downwind. All traffic was in a left pattern. Tower called and told my student 'traffic to follow ahead in the downwind'. My student replied with 'traffic in sight'. My student had mistaken Aircraft Z on final as the traffic he was following; instead and of seeing and following Aircraft Y in the downwind. Tower proceeded to clear my student 'follow that traffic; clear to land Runway XX'. Because my student was following the wrong aircraft; he turned base (about a half mile) in front of Aircraft Y and cut him off. Aircraft Y was on a 1 mile final at the time. Aircraft Y saw the error and made appropriate maneuvers. Tower noticed when Aircraft X had turned final in front of Aircraft Y and proceeded to tell both aircraft to go around. No further events took place. The pilot in Aircraft X did not see or follow the correct traffic and turned base accordingly. He unintentionally deviated from the ATC clearance. The plot of Aircraft Y noticed and took evasive actions as necessary. I was on the ground and all distances are highly estimated.
Report_2_Narrative: I was given traffic information by ATC while on downwind leg of pattern; a Aircraft X in the pattern ahead and to my left. I saw an aircraft matching ATC description on short final approach and responded that I had the traffic in sight. ATC gave landing clearance with instructions to follow that aircraft; so when I had sufficient distance behind I began my turn to the base leg of the pattern. Upon turning I spotted another aircraft ahead and to my right at about 500 feet clearance on final approach. I took a hard left turn to evade and was told by ATC to go around and that the aircraft I flew in front of was the traffic I was originally advised of and that I had cut them off. I continued in the traffic pattern and landed without further incident.

Report_1_Narrative: My student and I were cleared for the RNAV 18 from Tower we were told to report the FAF. At this point we were roughly on a 13 or so mile final. I hear Tower talking to another aircraft that was VFR wanting to practice approaches and also shoot the RNAV 18. Tower then told this aircraft to also report the FAF and advised them there is a Warrior on a 9 or 10 mile final and to look for us. The pilot responded that he does not have us in sight yet. I advised Tower that we are still in fact farther away than 9 miles. We had a strong headwind so our ground speed was pretty slow. Tower said we were still cleared for the option and to still report the FAF at this point we were at the fix half way between the FAF and the IAF. Tower again advised the other traffic that we were an 8 or less mile final and to report and he has us in sight. The Cessna still responded he didn't have us in sight so Tower told them to do a right 360. I then reported that we are .5 away from the FAF. At this point I got a traffic notification and I looked up [to see] the Cessna is at the same altitude off my right nose and passed right in front of me at the same altitude. I then took the controls; banked right and pushed the nose forward. The other pilot [said] 'holy crap I see them now' and I let Tower know we were going off course and low. Tower didn't seem like they were paying attention. Or seem to ask about it. We then continued on with the approach and went missed and Tower didn't ask us about it. When I reported going missed we didn't hear back from Tower till after several calls.

After completing a flight with a passenger and entering the flight into my logbook; I realized I had only 2 takeoffs and landings within the last 90 days and was not compliant with FAR 61.57(a).  Prior to the flight that included 2 landings on [date]; I had not been flying since March 2020.  I think lack of recency led to the mistake.  I had not thought of the requirement until after the flight was completed and I was making the logbook entry.  As a corrective action for myself; I made a checklist to use before every flight that includes currency requirements to carry passengers in VFR day and VFR night.

My instructor and I were on downwind approach for landing. Meanwhile a C-172 had just performed a go-around. We were told they came around at a faster velocity than us; but at nearly our same altitude/path. We later were told (I believe the C-172 instructor told my instructor) that the C-172 was only 100 feet away from us; I believe from above. We had no concrete idea about any of this at the time. We proceeded to land. Afterward; my instructor asked me if I was aware that we were almost in a mid air collision. He had just spoken with the other pilot near the fuel pump. We then looked at radar tracking website to see the activity but we only saw our airplane and not theirs. Pilot from C-172 came into the office to get water (Instructor I believe) and saw us looking at the radar map but didn't seem too concerned about the incident. That's all the information I have.

We have a family ranch which I normally patrol every other day; usually in the evenings; checking for things like bulls; cows; baby calves; horses; stray animals; escaped animals; distressed animals; fences; gates; water; feed; weeds; any vehicles; tractors or implements; and trespassers.  This evening; I was checking fences along the freeway; when I was shocked to see a powered parachute go by below me off to the right.  I do not know how close; it was far enough away that collision was not possible; nor was my wake turbulence likely to hit it; but it seemed big.  That is why I estimated 100 ft. over and 100 ft. down as being the closest it could have been; it could have been further but not closer.  I had not noticed it before because it was below me; with terrain as the background as shadows had fallen with the sun going behind the ridge to the west; and it had no lights and had made no radio announcements.  Its speed was likely no more than 20 MPH; and mine was 60 MPH; so closing speed was 40 MPH.  It is likely that due to the parallel positions of the two aircraft; my aircraft's nose covered it as I got closer; until I passed it.  I was not able to observe it long enough to determine his course before or after the miss; since as soon as it came into view it passed backward (relatively) on my right at a likely relative speed of 40 MPH; and I turned left.

While flying with a student on an IFR flight plan; we departed Camarillo on an assigned heading of 270 and told to expect vectors to the VNY VOR.Whle climbing; Camarillo Tower handed us off to Mugu Approach. I looked and changed the frequency. Before being able to transmit; I saw a very close aircraft on the G-530 traffic awareness warning; which I announced and told my student to turn right. I could not see traffic and told the student to continue his turn.After nearly 180 degrees of turn; the traffic (a Bonanza) descended past us on the right. I instructed my student to maintain his heading and contacted Point Mugu. The controller asked us if we were on an IFR flight plan; which we confirmed. He told us to fly a (Heading) 070 vector for VNY without further discussion; and we completed our flight.

In non-tower airport PRB; when exiting Runway 31 to Taxiway B; announced on CTAF intentions to exit runway; taxi on (Taxiway) B; cross Runway 1-19; then turn to Taxiway A. To do so; I had to turn my right hindquarters to the approach path of Runway 19; not realizing that a small plane was actually landing on Runway 19. I was unable to see the landing traffic from behind the right wing; and I never heard this plane on radio or made a radio contact. Thanks to prompt warning from a passenger; I was able to stop on Taxiway B short of the Runway 19. It was daytime; clear sky; and unrestricted visibility.

On an IFR flight plan; departing CMA; we were instructed to fly heading 270 for vectors to the VNY VOR. On climb out; we were instructed to contact Mugu Approach. I looked inside and changed frequencies; and noticed a traffic alert on the G530 that was close. I announced it to the student; and he started a right turn.I could not see the traffic; and told him to continue his turn. I instructed him to to roll out on about a 050 heading. The aircraft passed off of our right side in a descent. We contacted Mugu Approach; who asked if we were on an IFR flight plan; which we confirmed. He instructed us to fly a 070 heading. We continued with flight to ZZZ without further incident.The incident occurred very quickly; and we were unable to query Tower due to the early frequency change. In retrospect; I would not have looked inside the cockpit at such a low altitude with a student.

I was training on the X Scope and traffic volume was fairly high compared to the previous several weeks.  ZZZ was landing Runway X and ZZZ1 Runway Y Right; making for a complex approach pattern.  Aircraft Y was NW bound toward ZZZZZ; the South fix for RNAV X approach at ZZZ level at 030.  Aircraft X was on the Localizer Y Right approach at ZZZ1 east of ZZZZZ1; descending out of 030 for 020; not cleared for the approach.  The close proximity was recognized as Aircraft X (now at 025; still descending) was established on the localizer and Aircraft Y (still level at 030) was approximately 1 NM away from ZZZZZ; the situation was recognized just as 3 mile separation was lost.  I turned Aircraft X to a 180 HDG as they were out of 022 for 020 to avoid a further loss of separation.Aircraft X was slow to descend and I did not recognize the altitude in sufficient time.  Training has been intermittent for a long time due to the COVID situation.  Also; unusually frequent storms and bad weather had kept traffic volume low for the past few weeks; keeping training complexity low as well.  Today presented lots of training and volume; I was getting tired due to not being conditioned for constant traffic and lost focus.  I would [recommend that I] recognize my own fatigue and ask for training or to work a slower or less complex position for a mid day break or to change things up.

In an effort to depart the gate early; I released the brakes and gave the ground crew permission to push the aircraft off the gate. Immediately after; I realized that we had not yet received our final Dangerous Goods report. After the pushback was completed; a request was sent to the station operations to send the report. The final Dangerous Goods report was sent within minutes.
Report_2_Narrative: Both Captain and First Officer took note of needing the final Dangerous Goods summary prior to push back; xxx pushed back without the document. The error was noted immediately as the push commenced. An additional written item (Final Dangerous Goods summary) will be added to the list of other items I currently look at before departure.

Report_1_Narrative: We exceeded 250 knots below 10;000 and were on speed by 9;600. I (PM) was looking at weather and the STAR and when I went to monitor the aircraft displays I noticed we were doing 300 knots at about 9;760 feet. I said check speed and the PF disengaged the autopilot; reduced power; deployed speed brakes; and leveled off. He had the speed window open and we both failed to monitor the speed window. He is very good about VVM (Verbalize; Verify; Monitor) and not doing something without informing the PM; so I feel I'm just as much responsible for the incident. I believe the speed window was open from a previous instruction from a Controller.
Report_2_Narrative: On the ZZZZZX south of ZZZZZ [Intersection]; we exceeded 250 knots below 10;000 feet.ATC had originally asked us to cross ZZZZZ at 15;000 feet leaving us high for the next restriction (ZZZZZ1 at 8;000 feet and 250 knots). I was using speed intervene (300 knots) and flight spoilers to make the anticipated restrictions at ZZZZZ1 while descending from 15;000 feet. The FMA switched from VNAV SPD to VNAV PATH at approximately 12;500 feet. At this point; I began focusing more attention on small build-ups and ensuring the flight attendants were seated. Upon crossing 10;000 feet; the PM called out 'speed' and we quickly corrected to 250 knots by 9;650 feet. ATC passed us off to Approach without commenting about the speed deviation.The FO did a great job as the PM. However; we did debrief that; despite verbalizing the use of speed intervene; we ended up below 10;000 feet above 250 knots. The error is mine and I own that; but recency of experience may have been contributory as well.

Report_1_Narrative: On an early evening flight to IAH we were descending via the DRLLR 5 RNAV arrival being set up and briefed for the RNAV Y 26R with our altitude set to 6000'. When checking on with the approach controller we were told to expect the runway 26L transition. We selected the new runway in the FMS but failed to reset our altitude selector to 7;000' at that time. As we neared ZOEEE Drag Required appeared on the FMS scratchpad and the Captain said; 'Careful not to be too hot at ZOEEE'. I deployed the speed brakes and slowed us to 210kts and checked the FMA and we were still in VNAV PATH. Shortly after passing ZOEEE; I noticed the FMA showed VNAV SPEED as we were leveling at 6;000' and I no longer showed SKLER as a waypoint on the screen. I then looked more closely at the DRLLR 5 chart and noticed the point we'd briefed as the lowest altitude on the arrival had (Runway 26R) beside it and thought we should've stopped at 7;000'. I brought it to the attention of the captain who then queried ATC. The ATC response was that we were ok and to now descend to 5;000'. We started the descent and shortly after were told by what sounded like a different voice on the radio to maintain 6;000'. We reversed the approximately 200' descent and returned to 6000'.The rest of the approach and landing were uneventful and the flight ended safely at the destination. I believe we should've stopped at 7;000' but am unsure since the controller didn't have a problem with us being at 6;000' so I listed the event as a possible altitude deviation.

Upon arrival to Gate X in ZZZ we were asked to call OPS. The Captain called them and we were reminded that we should call ramp before entering the ramp next time. I couldn't believe that I had made such a mistake after over XXXXX hours on the fleet and almost XX years of flying at the company with many many ZZZ arrivals. I looked at the Captain and I said; 'I am sorry I have no excuse;' which is how I felt. I then called the ramp and apologized. I called them on the frequency that I had set on stby at Comm 2 prior to the top of descend when I was setting up for our arrival to ZZZ. As we debriefed I started questioning the why of my mistake and I realize that I am taking for granted my lack of situational awareness as a result of my 7 month absence from flying.When I went to ZZZ1 I was very serious about it; during my OE trip I was still very conscious of my weaknesses because of my lack of recency but now as I am finishing my second trip after OE I feel that 'I am back' which obviously I am not. There were a few other threats that contributed to my mistake. We had thoroughly briefed a [Runway] XXL arrival and taxi in but had spent way less time discussing [Runway] XYR. Our set up for XYR and execution was great but created a distraction and increased the workload. We cleared XYR on Taxiway 1; were given instruction to cross XYL on 1 and call Ground. Both myself and the Captain were anticipating taxi via 1; but when I called Ground we were told 'Taxi via 2; 3 hold short of 4 and at the half way point on November call Ground at XXX.YY.' A lot of information to digest especially if you aren't very familiar with the airport; you have been out for a long time (this is the Captain's first trip after RQ (Recurrent Qualification) training and OE). We were both expecting to go straight on Taxiway 1.I told the Captain to stop. Which he very nicely did as he was also figuring out that we were not suppose to continue on 1. The Captain taxied as instructed; I called Ground Control and was told to taxi via 3; 4 to the gate. After making the turn on 4 we were instructed to give way to opposite direction traffic on 4 that was turning on 5. We then were told to turn right on 5 and continue via 6 to the gate. As I am debriefing this I realized that ZZZ unlike ZZZ1 or ZZZ2 doesn't instruct you to contact ramp. They simply say taxi to the gate/ramp. Finally as we were approaching the gate area I was trying to time my APU start so it is running when we come to a full stop; and I also noticed that there was a lot of vehicle traffic traveling parallel to us and I was a little concerned on whether or not they will stop at X7 for our turn. There was no traffic in the alley and we taxied to Gate X uneventfully.

We were assigned the ZZZZZX RNAV departure off Runway X at ZZZ.  The clearance was to climb via the SID; top altitude of FL230 was set in the MCP. All the fixes and altitude restrictions were correct in the FMC; I was the PM; the airplane was light; so we had a good climb rate. The PF was hand flying as we approached the first fix which has a crossing restriction of at or below 10;000. LNAV/VNAV and VNAV PTH was selected. Close to the first fix the PF called for the autopilot as the airplane started its turn to cross the fix that placed the sun directly into our face which affected our visibility. As I turned my head to get my sunshade; ATC asked if we were going to stop at 10;000 feet; we got as high as 10;300 the PF turned the autopilot off and went down to 10;000.  In this situation I think turning the autopilot on earlier would have helped.
