As the PIC and PF; I set the Altitude Alerter to 2;000 and vertical speed to 700 FPM descent; with autopilot engaged. During the descent to 2;000 MSL; I briefly looked up at the Pressurization Control Panel to monitor the PNF progress in controlling the cabin pressure; I noted the cabin pressure was finally close to the aircraft's actual altitude. I directed the PNF to select 'RAM AIR' ON.  That action stabilized the cabin for landing. ATC called to confirm our 2;000 feet clearance limit and I realized that the aircraft did not level off at 2;000 feet and the autopilot was not engaged. I acknowledged our clearance to ATC; manually flew the plane from 1;700 feet to 2;000 feet and reengaged to Autopilot. Since the cabin pressure was finally under control; an uneventful approach and landing were completed. Due to the distraction of the cabin pressure control issues; as PF I briefly failed to monitor the aircraft flight profile. Since I had tasked the PNF to attempt to stabilize the cabin pressure; I take full responsibility for failing to continuously monitor the aircraft flight profile.No matter what cockpit distractions occur; it is incumbent upon the PF to continuously monitor the aircraft flight profile. And if using the Autopilot; PF should continuously monitor that it functions as programed and does not inadvertently disconnect.
Report_2_Narrative: The flight went well up to the time of descent when the cabin pressure started to malfunction. All attempts were made to control the pressurization using ADC 1 & 2 and Manual control. As preparations were made for landing; air traffic control issued further descent to 2;000 feet and the flying pilot was in control of the aircraft with the auto pilot engaged. The aircraft did not capture the 2;000 foot altitude because the autopilot was inadvertently disconnected and the aircraft continued descent to 1;600 feet at which time the deviation was noted and immediately corrected.  The aircraft was subsequently vectored onto the final course and the rest of the approach and landing was uneventful.  The pilot monitoring should have noticed the altitude deviation as a result of the auto pilot not being engaged while manually controlling the pressurization and the pilot flying should have been aware that the auto pilot was not engaged. It is imperative that the pilot flying concentrates on flying the aircraft while the other attends to the malfunction and refers to the checklists.

Report_1_Narrative: On a visual approach to Runway X; the FO was flying; he started the approach a little high but managed to get back on a 3 degree glide to the runway. By the time I noticed we were at 500 feet and his airspeed was still about 20 knots above Vref. The airspeed managed to get down to Vapp with the engines spooled before touchdown. I hadn't flown in a month so my situational awareness was not on a level it should have been and I should have called a go-around.

When loading Cargo Compartment the Loading Supervisor noticed that there was no DG (Dangerous Goods) info for this ULD (Unit Load Devise) in the Load Platform Program.He contacted the Load Controller who then contacted Cargo for the information and the DG info was then updated in Load Platform Program.

Training on combined sectors; moderate traffic; two practice approach aircraft in satellite airspace all other traffic in departure sectors.Aircraft X came off ZZZ on the published missed approach for the RNAV RWY XX approach; he checked in and was radar identified while in the published left turn towards the missed approach fix; ZZZZZ.  The trainee subsequently verified the pilot's previous request to return to ZZZ1 on an IFR clearance.  The trainee cleared Aircraft X direct ZZZ and instructed the aircraft to maintain 2;000'.  At the time; the aircraft was within a 2;000' MVA [Minimum Vectoring Altitude].  I believe both the trainee and I anticipated that the pilot would make a right turn direct to ZZZ1; not recognizing that a continued left turn may be more appropriate because the aircraft was already in the published left turn on the missed approach.  With the anticipation of a right turn in mind and the aircraft in a 2;000' MVA my attention as the OJTI turned to the other departure traffic; which was going to require the trainee to merge and weave multiple departures off multiple airports (ZZZ2/ZZZ3/ZZZ4) to get them on course and through our airspace.  This was an area the trainee had shown struggles in in previous sessions as well as earlier in this training session; so I was attempting to stay ahead and have a plan of my own if needed.  Upon reviewing the incident; I became aware that in the moments following Aircraft X's clearance I was suggesting an alternative course of action for one of the trainee's departures in an effort to illustrate a way to expedite getting a departure on course which also got them out of the way of succeeding departures quicker.  Unfortunately; this conversation kept my focus on the departure traffic as opposed to the continued turn that Aircraft X made into the higher MVA.  Aircraft X subsequently continued a left turn towards ZZZ1 and entered a 2;500' MVA at his assigned altitude of 2;000'.  The trainee was aware of the incident almost immediately.  The trainee issued a climb to 2;500' to Aircraft X when the aircraft was only a third or a half a mile inside the 2;000' MVA.  I was not aware of the situation until I heard the trainee issue the climb to Aircraft X.  I subsequently requested that we be relieved.  The trainee and I debriefed; after which I informed management of the incident when I requested time for myself and the trainee to review the incident and fill out our report.  To my knowledge; Aircraft X climbed to the MVA and subsequently landed at ZZZ1 without further incident.Numerous things could have prevented this occurrence.  Had the trainee climbed Aircraft X to 3;000' instead of descending them to 2;000' they would have been above the MVA.  Had the trainee left the aircraft on the published missed approach for even a few miles they would have climbed above the MVA and a subsequent clearance may have included a more appropriate altitude.  Had the aircraft made the anticipated right turn instead of continuing in a left turn they would have remained in the 2;000' MVA.  Had the trainee issued the clearance to ZZZ1 via radar vectors; I believe they would have assigned a vector that would have remained within the 2;000' MVA and avoided the situation.
Report_2_Narrative: Recertification time on [positions] (departures) and Article 67 training on[positions](south satellite). Aircraft X departed ZZZ on the published missed from the RNV runway XX  approach. Requested clearance to ZZZ1 airport. I observed Aircraft X in a 2;000ft MVA [Minimum Vectoring Altitude] and told pilot to maintain 2;000' and proceed direct ZZZ1; which is north east of ZZZ. A direct flight path would have kept the aircraft away from the 2500ft MVA east of their position. Pilot was in a left hand turn though; and continued a left hand turn direct ZZZ1. I was expecting a right hand turn direct. The left hand turn put the aircraft 2-3 miles further east than planned and inside the 2;500ft MVA. I climbed the aircraft to 2;500ft and let them continue on course. I; as the controller should have been more aware of the chance the aircraft could have entered higher MVA. I should have climbed Aircraft X to 2;500ft before sending direct ZZZ1 to account for a left or right turn.

Report_1_Narrative: During the boarding process of Aircraft X at Gate; a passenger in her assigned seat was holding her carry-on bag on her lap. The carry-on had a vape charger inside the bag and the batteries started to smoke and meltdown. There was smoke and fumes in the cabin. An unknown passenger ran towards the front of the aircraft yelling that there was a fire in the back of the aircraft and for everyone to get off. The flight attendants made an announcement and commenced the Emergency Deplaning at the Gate procedure (which we had discussed in my pre-bridging brief to the FA's (Flight Attendants)). The Purser asked what to do and I told him to continue the process they had started; which was going very well with passengers calmly deplaning up the jet way. The First Officer alerted emergency response and got up from his seat to assist. I was then told by an unknown person; that the smoking bag had been removed from the aircraft by a dead-heading pilot. I ran the Emergency Evacuation checklist to secure the aircraft; grabbed the fire extinguisher (not needed) and stood at the door directing the passengers to continue up the jetway; 'do not take bags or stand in the jetway; and to proceed to the terminal.' I directed the First Officer to go to terminal. There were a few passengers slowing the process trying to grab their carry-on bag; but were directed to stop and continue to the terminal. Once all passengers were off; I directed the FA's to go to the terminal while I swept the aircraft to be sure all crew and passengers were off. I then proceeded to the terminal. At the terminal I found the crew and deadheading crew members at the door; several passengers and agents and the smoking bag lying on the floor next to the ticket counter. I then directed all persons to leave the area; and requested Law enforcement with/dogs and fire and rescue to the bag. Asked the gate agent to call medical assistance and found the affected passenger sitting in the terminal; which I directed Medical to her location. At that point I was alarmed that the threat had followed us to the terminal and was concerned as to its contents. I then briefed fire and rescue; Maintenance as well as corporate security and law enforcement. I directed the deadheading crewmember who had carried the bag to seek medical attention. We coordinated for the FA's to stay nearby to facilitate the eventual allowing of 5 passengers at a time to go back to the secured aircraft to get their belongings. The First Officer was having chest pain and went to the hospital. I declined medical treatment. And met the ZZZ Chief Pilot who assisted me in the process remaining. There were burns on the owner of the smoking bag; and she also went to the hospital. There were no other injuries or incidents associated with the emergency deplaning procedure...but there was some uncertainty among the many constituents affected by the procedure; gate; ground; maintenance. etc. I was relieved from duty with 'p' for the remaining duration of the trip. I received follow up calls from ZZZ Chief Pilot on multiple occasions and was grateful for his support. I also contacted directly Union Safety and spoke with [Representative] who provided assistance and guidance during and after the event. Overall the procedure works; but should be expanded to a joint training scenario. I was most alarmed to learn. That the (brave) deadheading crew member had carried the smoking bag with him during the deplaning and it eventually ended up in the terminal with no oversight. The direction was to deplane away from the threat; not take it with us.The cause was allowing of the charging device and vape apparatus onboard the aircraft as a carry-on. The positive outcome was due to emergency deplaning at the gate procedure and pre-briefing. Well trained and professional back-end crew was outstanding. First Officer reverted to training and promptly called fire and rescue. However once passengers were off; there was confusion and potential danger which needs to be mitigated for future events. Communication and full understanding and procedures associated are necessary.I suggest the need to continue the use of and to training of the 'Emergency Deplaning at the Gate Procedure'. Would recommend suggested briefings for the crew. Also believe joint training would be very beneficial to role play these scenarios...there is a lot going on and many potential pitfalls. This affects all constituents of the normal process; but also many others. Being released from duty by the Chief Pilot was appreciated; however as a reserve pilot; working to get my time for the month; Pay - No Credit does not help in any way. In fact it prevented me from flying another 4 day trip I could have held; and subsequently puts me at zero(0) hours going into the middle of the month.
Report_2_Narrative: It was the first leg of the start of the sequence. I had just met the CA (Captain); for the first time; that afternoon. Along with the first time working with the FA's (Flight Attendants). Boarding started as normal; the CA and myself went about our preflight duties. We had finished all our checklists and were just waiting for the Gate Agent to inform us of boarding being complete. As we sat waiting to close up FA-1 shouts up to us 'We have a fire in the cabin'. In the time that it took the CA and I to get over the initial shock factor; the FA's immediately began an 'Emergency Deplaning' of the aircraft. From my advantage point I had no clue how bad the fire was in the cabin. I did not hear any call for an 'Evacuation'; or see any doors and slides deploying; so I assumed that the FA's were just doing the 'Emergency Deplaning'.The CA went to the QRH immediately. I got on the radio to ops and told them of our situation. That we had a fire onboard the aircraft; we were 'Emergency Deplaning' into the terminal; and we needed ARF (Airport Rescue and Fire Fighting).The CA and I had completed everything we could think of to help the FA's with the situation in the back. Still not knowing how bad things were in the cabin the CA instructed me to exit the airplane. I did so and then followed my 'evacuation' duties when outside the aircraft. As I exited the flight deck I found a clog of passengers not moving out the door. I made my way through them to find a group of passengers have congregated on the jetway. I instructed them to move and get in the terminal building. This helped the flow of passengers off the aircraft. I found one passenger in visible distress on the jetway. I did a quick assessment of the passenger and then asked if they were OK. The passenger complained of burns on their arm. It did not look bad so I asked if they could walk. The passenger said yes. So I got the assistance of another passenger to help them into the terminal. I also instructed them to take the bag that the hurt passenger said was theirs and was standing next to. Later I would find out this was the bag that was smoking. It was not smoking when I was on the jetway and I was unaware this was the threat on the aircraft. I never would have thought that the threat would be off the aircraft in the direction the passengers were going. Or taken off and left unattended.After the passengers were off the aircraft the FA's were coming off and then asked where the bag was. I asked 'What bag? The one on the ground in the jetway?' They responded Yes; that was the one that was smoking. At that point I realized that I sent it inside the terminal. I remembered what passenger had it and told the supervisor; that was on the jetway; that I could identify them to find that bag.The CA completed his final check of the aircraft and we proceeded into the terminal. The bag has been cordoned off at the gate and people were being kept away. The CA remained at the gate until ARF arrived. I took the FA's and proceeded to an unused gate to keep us together and away from the situation.The incident started with a passenger's bag there was no control over the problem. There was some confusion over what procedures were started and what roles the flight deck crew had in an 'Emergency Deplaning'.It was also confusing how and why the fire; smoking bag; was put onto the jetway. The use of the 'Emergency Deplaning' was very appropriate to this situation and did exactly what it was intended to do. However there is not a clear signal; that we knew of; that signals to the flight deck that an 'Emergency Deplaning' is in progress. There was also not an immediate action item; QRC; or QRH procedure to reference for an event like this for us on the flight deck to help. It felt as if we were making it up as we went along. Merging our evacuation checklist and duties into the 'Emergency Deplaning'.There is also no defined plan for fighting an onboard fire at the gate; that at least theflight deck is aware of. Just so we know what to expect. Example; that if we are to expect the fire or threat to be taken off the aircraft mid-deplaning.The event was a success; it was extremely helpful that the flight deck knows about 'Emergency Deplaning'. However there seems to still be confusion on roles; how to help with and execute an 'Emergency Deplaning'.

Report_1_Narrative: While we were on base leg on the RNP Y Runway 2 approach to MGGT [airport]; someone on the ground pointed a green laser and it flashed in the cockpit a few times; twice hitting me on the right eye. We were in between GT504 and GT503 with the aircraft fully configured; I was PM and since it was my first time flying into MGGT; I was looking outside to get a mental picture for future reference and situational awareness; trying to identify the runway;  when I saw a green laser beam being pointed at the aircraft; I called it out to the Captain and he kept his head down but I was hit twice on the right eye before I could go head down. I reported it to the Tower (in Spanish). The Captain asked me if I was OK; and I just mentioned that my eye felt drier than normal. (I've had the misfortune of being hit by a laser while flying before; so I knew the sensation). We continued to approach and landing without any further incident.Unfortunately; these types of events are almost impossible to stop; unless we wear protective eye gear; there is nothing we can do but lower our heads if a beam is being pointed to the aircraft.
Report_2_Narrative: While on approach; circling left arc to final; RNV Y 02; MGGT; about 145 knots and 2;000 feet AGL; FO (Non-Flying Pilot) observed a green laser coming from the low 10 o'clock position.  We continued approach to landing. FO reported it to ATC; giving them location; and Controller said it's been happening more often now and that they'll report it to authorities. I asked FO if he was OK and he said his eyes were drier than normal.Suggestions - Patrol the terminal area in vicinity of airport more often. Make area more sterile. Prosecute those who attempt to lase airplanes as they land at airports.

Report_1_Narrative: I was PF; hand-flying.  On departure; we were cleared to 10;000 feet.  Northbound on the SID; climbing through approximately 7;500; I increased pitch to avoid a small build-up off the nose.  Climbing thru approximately 8;800 feet; I realized that I was going to have a difficult time leveling off by 10;000 feet.  I asked the CA to advise ATC of the potential altitude excursion and aggressively; but smoothly; reduced pitch to level off.  I exceeded our assigned altitude by 450 feet; and promptly descended back to 10;000 feet.  ATC did not seem concerned by our deviation and cleared us to 16;000 feet.This was one of my first sequences after a 6-month leave of absence.  The CA is new to the airplane and new to the seat.  These factors contributed to our poor altitude SA and slow cross-check.Our threat-forward briefing focused on our lack of recent flying.  We needed to be even more vigilant to trap decision and execution errors.

We were given a recovery trip from PBI to ZZZ1. Upon arriving at the aircraft we had to defer the Nav database and the Jeppessen charts. We did just that which made us unable to do RNAV STARS; SIDS; routes etc. When the First Officer tried to get the clearance they gave us a RNAV SID and an AR route as that is what had been filed. The First Officer advised them we were unable to accept the route as we weren't able RNAV SIDS; STARS; routes etc. they informed us that our suffix indicated we were RNAV capable so we would have to refile correctly for us to go without using an RNAV SID. I called dispatch and explained it to the dispatcher and asked for it to be refiled with an appropriate route and suffix. It was refiled with a different route but the suffix still showed us as RNAV capable so clearance still wouldn't issue it. I called dispatch again and asked for it to be corrected and filed again. I made sure the third flight plan did come through with a different suffix before turning my attention to the passengers as they had now shown up. I greeted the owners and got them loaded and made sure the bags were loaded. During the baggage loading it became apparent I would have to increase the weight to match the weight of bags for the trip. I adjusted the weight in the waypoint app and waited for a valid release. The First Officer had briefed the passengers and we got into the cockpit and got ready to go while we waited for the release. We waited quite a while for the release as we began making phone calls and sending messages to try and see if the release was coming. Once I finally got through to the dispatcher on the phone and asked if the new release was coming soon. He responded 'what do you mean? You're in the air already.' I informed him that no we weren't we were still waiting on a valid release due to the weight change. Shortly after that we received a release from a different dispatcher which we verified the suffix was still the same as the last release and made our way to depart. A short time into the flight they gave us a reroute to a couple RNAV fixes and different route. We advised them we weren't RNAV and would like to remain on our route navigating by ground based navigation. It was at this point that I started to wonder if the suffix still wasn't correct for the airplanes condition. We continued without further issues. The entire process was truly exhausting having to review and keep straight in my head four releases and make sure they were done correctly; even if it wasn't already a new airplane as well as a recovery at the end of what was now becoming a long day. The last two releases being issued after the passengers were already onboard the airplane.

While flying a VFR flight to LOM; I may have entered the Philadelphia Bravo airspace without clearance. I was communicating with PHL ATC on 124.35 for flight following. I was told to climb from 3;000 to 3;500 to avoid a TFR but don't remember specifically being cleared into the bravo airspace. Normally I would verify with ATC before entering the bravo if I was unsure. However; on this flight; my GPS equipment lost the GPS signal near Wilmington and I got distracted troubleshooting it. I was a few miles into the Bravo airspace when I realized I had not double checked the clearance. I was still talking with PHL ATC and they did not express any concerns with my location.

Tuned radio to frequency while approaching ZZZ airport from the west.  I listened for traffic at ZZZ and announced intentions to land on Runway XY after crossing midfield Runway XY-YX.  These announcements were 10 miles and again 4 miles west of the airport.  Crossed midfield from the west; made right turn to the south to enter Runway XY from 45 degrees. Announced again on frequency entering left downwind Runway XY; announced base; then final for Runway XY.  Saw that I was high and went around.  At midfield; while departing and at about 100-200 feet AGL; I saw another aircraft approaching from straight ahead and about 75 feet above me and to the left.  The opposing aircraft banked right; as did I. The other aircraft must have been landing on Runway YX and was also going around; because I did not see an aircraft on the runway or taxiway as I approached.  I checked the airport traffic CTAF against my entered radio frequency and saw the correct frequency was frequency.  I had been announcing on frequency and did not realize I was on the wrong frequency. I had made an error in tuning radio and did not hear the airport traffic; nor did they hear me and my announcements.

To keep current; I try to fly at least one hour a month. I confirmed that there was 15 gallons in each tank which would allow for 3 hours for an anticipated flight of less than 2. I did 8 landings at 3 airports and was on my last 10 mile leg to home base. About 1.5 hours. Because of failure to change the fuel selector to both; the engine died during low level cruise. I [selected and checked] carburetor heat; mags and gas but the engine did not start for unknown reasons. I landed in a farm field with no injuries; property damage and no damage to the airplane as determined by the club mechanic.  Lack of experience in type. This the only plane that requires leaving the fuel on right only that I have flown. I was interrupted during the preflight; I should have stated over again. I should practice emergency procedures more often.

In descent while returning to land at ZZZ from west of the airport and north of the Denver Class B airspace; I received a TCAS warning very close to our aircraft. I made a rapid and aggressive descending turn away from the threat aircraft in the direction of the Class B airspace (the only way that I could turn). We were near; but below the ceiling of the Class B segment and did not enter it. We never did see the other aircraft. The remainder of approach and landing were normal.

While in the cruise portion of my flight on an IFR flight plan I was alerted by ATC of traffic converging same altitude. I was on a NE (northeast) heading but was instructed by ATC to fly at 6;000 ft. The traffic was on a SE (southeast) heading also at 6;000 ft. descending for ZZZ. Due to me being on an IFR flight plan I did not deviate altitude or heading. The traffic descending was descending apparently at a very slow rate. ATC provided traffic alert but did not suggest new heading or altitude. When I had the traffic in sight about 200 ft. below me and very close I elected to climb based on my traffic message from my G1000 avionics with TIS-B (Traffic Information Service - Broadcast). At my altitude of 6;200 ft. the traffic passed below me by 500 ft. ATC was very busy but I should [have] queried them for vectors [or] altitude change earlier so I would not have to take action on my own. ATC did not complain about my momentary altitude deviation.

I inadvertently pushed back from the gate without a final Dangerous Goods summary because I mistook a secondary planned summary to indicate that no Hazmat was loaded. Another reason that I hastily made my assumption was because we don't ever receive secondary planned DG summaries. We only get a final summary after the initial planned summary. Thankfully; due to ramp congestion; we did receive a final Dangerous Goods summary prior to taxi.There are a couple of key notes added into the software log stating that a ramp scanner deleted the Hazmat at close out.

Gate agent was ready for early push. Planned Dangerous Goods was printed and noted. Cabin was secured and ground crew ready to push 10 mins early. Checklist completed and push back clearance received; as the IRO I was checking all the documents as many were being printed as we began push. I noticed that we had not received the final DG. The Captain stopped the push and we called operations. Final DG showed up right away and continued push with out further issues.
Report_2_Narrative: Gate agent came up early said they were ready for push. I think that we were caught off guard by the agent saying they were ready that early. We pushed early. The IRO was cleaning up the paperwork and noticed we did not have the final DG summary. We had only pushed a few yards; the Captain stopped the push. We notified ground and called ops to ask about the final DG Summary. As we were talking to ops; the final DG Summary printed out. We called ground and continued the push without incident.

Report_1_Narrative: I take full responsibility for this altitude deviation.  We were in the chocks at ZZZ and there was a slight reroute to ZZZZ.  The FO X said it would not load in the FMC; but FO loaded manually.  FO said the same thing happened on the last flight also into ZZZZ.   I had flown down to ZZZ1 in Month X and had the same reroute.   The FO had over 40 hours total time with XX landings.  I have not flown in over 60 days.  So in the chocks the Departure Briefing I briefed CA designates PF PM states concerns and threats PF states concerns and threats taxi; takeoff/reject departure.  The FO said low time and I said my over 60 days off.  FO is rock solid; what an excellent stick.  You know when you meet someone for the first time; it's going to be a fun flight.  Every time I see FO X's name on the crew list; it will be a fun flight.  For over 40 hours and XX landings FO flew real well.  Talking to ZZZ Center of frequencies XYZ.Z and XYA.B and we're climbing to FL230 and they ask Aircraft X we just want to confirm the route.  FO asks this is a reroute you want me to put on the autopilot?  If I had said yes; I would not be writing the report.  The fault is all mine.  My thinking was this is the same reroute we got in the chocks easy-peasy.  And it was the same route but took three or four minutes to get everything done. ZZZ Center then cleared us ZZZZZ JXXX ZZZ1 as filed.  I spelt ZZZZZ wrong and it was not accepted in the FMC.  Right then the Altitude Deviation warning came on.  ZZZ Center told us switch over to XYZ.Z and I reported FL240 we were at FL238 and our clearance was FL230.  Center than cleared me to climb to FL400.Lots of areas to hit on.  Even though I haven't flown in over 60 days do Company work on most days; I read all the changes; FD Pro X; Airline Pilots; WSI Pilotbrief; and Outlook.  On any day to read all this it takes 45 minutes to and hour and a half.  For this ZZZZ flight; I checked it three days in a row.  What was the routing; did it go over ZZZZ1 Country; and the weather?  FO is an excellent pilot.  Low time like FO is the autopilot should have been on.  The mistake is on me; no excuse; I messed up.It pains me to have made this mistake; I can't remember my last report.
Report_2_Narrative: New on the aircraft; I had briefed the Captain I wanted to hand fly the aircraft; which he agreed was a good idea. We departed ZZZ on XXL and was cleared on the ZZZZZ1 4 RNAV SID.  In our turn towards ZZZZZ1 we received clearance to ZZZZZ2 on frequency XYA.B. When we checked in to XYZ.Z we received a clearance to FL230 and to fly direct to ZZZZZ. ZZZZZ is not on the SID or depicted anywhere on the SID. The Captain was unfamiliar but I was because I flew the same Flight the day before and we received the same clearance. I believe I spelt the fix out to the Captain and asked if I should I put the Autopilot on; which he responded 'no it's okay'. Just as he was typing it in and he executed it we heard the altitude alert caution and we were at 23;580 feet. I quickly reacted and proceeded back towards FL230 at the same time the Captain called ATC and stated 'Aircraft X at FL240'. ATC then cleared us to FL310 or FL330. The deviation occurred at XA:22Z.I should not have asked the Captain if I should put the autopilot on; I should have just done it. We were obviously both needed to navigate. I should have put the autopilot on while the Captain was trying to figure out and insert a fix that was not on the SID. Having the autopilot on would have prevented us from flying through our assigned altitude while we were navigating the new clearance.

Report_1_Narrative: While on the RNAVM XX in ZZZ1 we encountered a GPWS event. At the beginning of the approach we had a tailwind and the descent angle of the approach caused us to be a little fast starting the approach. As the pilot monitoring I should have caught this issue sooner and brought it to the attention of the PF (Pilot Flying). Because of the speed the PF disconnected the autopilot/auto throttles and slowed the plane to the proper approach speed. During the turn onto final the PF was visual with the runway and let the nose drop which caused a momentary don't sink. He corrected and the warning went away. Still with the airport visual he tried to correct on the VASI and received another momentary don't sink call at around 700 ft. We continued the approach and the landing was normal. I don't have anything to add for [this report].I have learned many lessons from this very short flight. I haven't had much ZZZ experience and my FO (First Officer) who was PF has had very little. We tried to mitigate the possible issues by having a slow cruise speed of 210 kts. and trying to get ready for the approach early. We briefed the 3.4 GP and felt we were in a good position for the approach. With the unfamiliarity of the situation and the weather in ZZZ this day I missed the strong tailwind that was making our situation worse. This put us behind and we had to play catch up. It was turbulent the whole flight and when he disconnected the AP/AT my [situational awareness] went down because I was worried about his flight path at that time. As I look back at the event and if I could do this again I would have us discontinue the approach early and come around to try again. Once we continued and got out of the [weather] and had the runway visually climbing back into it in that terrain did not seem the safest option. But if we had bailed early it would have made the situation easier the second time.

While doing my countdown/brief down; there was a passenger in [seat] XX1 complaining about the passengers in [seat] YY3 and [seat] YY4 that were not being compliant about the mask being over their nose and mouth. I immediately told the passengers in YY3 and YY4 to place their mask over their nose and mouth; which they complied. While I was waiting for the pilots to finish up the last of the paperwork; so I can close the main cabin door; the passenger from XX1 came up to me to complain about the passengers again in YY3 and YY4 that were not being compliant about wearing their masks. The passenger from XX1 proceeded to ask me what the consequences were for people who don't comply with wearing their masks so I told him that I would speak to the Captain. I told the Captain to make an announcement about wearing the masks over their nose and mouth for the duration of the flight and in the next few seconds the Captain made the announcement and everyone was in compliance. It was then time to close the main cabin door and begin the safety announcement. I started the beginning of the safety announcement and the passenger from XX1 walked right up to me to complain yet again about the passengers in YY3 and YY4. I told the passenger that I am doing the safety announcement and I have to do my Flight Attendant duties but I will come to address the issue. The passenger was not pleased with my response because he wanted me to address the issue right at that moment; but I only have a finite amount of time to do the safety announcement and do the last of my Flight Attendant duties before takeoff. After the safety announcement was complete; I went to the passengers in YY3 and YY4 and told them to keep their masks over their nose and mouth. One of the passengers said that he was drinking his beverage so he forgot to put the mask back on. I reminded both passengers to keep their masks on and comply with the policy in order to avoid any issues in the future. Both passengers made some remarks and was upset that the passenger from XX1 was complaining about them so much. I even offered to give them additional masks because the face covering that they had on was sliding off their faces. The passengers refused to receive the additional masks and said that they would comply. In the end; both passengers from YY3 and YY4 complied so that we could depart safely. While inflight; I made several announcements about all passengers keeping their masks on for the duration of the flight and that everyone had to comply with this policy. The passenger from XX1 did not come to me to complain at all during flight or after taxi. I assumed that he was satisfied with the results of what happened and the passengers in YY3 and YY4 remained compliant with the mask policy.

I was the PNF and the First Officer on this flight to ZZZ. On our approach into ZZZ we were slightly high on vectors and then cleared for the visual approach. The Captain PF was at a speed of 230 knots indicated airspeed (KIAS) and was trying to descend and slow to land. He was unable to slow to 200 KIAS as we were entering the Class C airspace and we determined the approach was unstable and executed a go-around. We entered the traffic pattern and landed successfully. I believe the cause is a decrease in proficiency due to reduced flying during the pandemic. We were doing 220-230 KIAS while entering the Class C airspace.

Aircraft X was the only aircraft on frequency due to issues with the normal frequency. They attempted to check in on the missed approach off of a satellite airport but were stepped on by multiple other aircraft. Including Aircraft Y that sounded like a similar call sign at the time. Due to the frequency congestion; I answered who I could hear and asked all aircraft to standby until I called on them. When I did hear Aircraft X check on; they told me they responded to an RA and I saw a target near them squawking 1200 at a similar altitude. I identified Aircraft X and issued instructions to approve their further requests. Upon watching the replay; I saw that the 1200 code had departed the satellite airport ahead of Aircraft X but do not know if the Tower issued the traffic. I did have another controller working my assist during the frequency congestion which lasted about 5 minutes.Continue scanning airspace and keep more aircraft on the same frequency so they can hear each other.

The Ops Agent arrived in the cockpit just prior to push time and provided me with a NOTAC (Notice to Captain) to sign. I looked over the NOTAC. Everything looked appropriate; the correct flight number and city pair and date. What I failed to do was to call Dispatch and amend/add HAZMAT (hazardous material)/ COMAT (company material)-ONBOARD to the release. I realized my error upon arrival in ZZZ1; at which point I contacted my Dispatcher.

I took over this flight at shift change and outgoing Dispatcher advised they had just received information about undeclared HAZMAT (hazardous material) being on the aircraft.  Service recovery advised that there were 98 packages of lithium batteries in position 2R; which was located too close to ADG (accessible dangerous goods) loaded in position 1L.  At this point; flight was about 3 hours out of ZZZ; notified crew of issue via ACARS; and after service recovery consulted with Duty Officer; determined no immediate safety issue existed; so flight continued to ZZZ1. I do not know what caused the issue; aircraft was already in the air when I found out about it and the error was with the loading of the dangerous goods and lithium batteries; anyway.  You would need to contact hub to find out the cause.

Turned in closer to final than anticipated after receiving vectors to final.  Cleared for the approach and wasn't quite to ZZZZZ [Intersection] before descending to the final altitude.  Received a low altitude alert from ATC.  Called up ATC and discussed the situation; didn't lose any separation or any other problems.Turned in closer to final than anticipated.  Cleared for the approach and wasn't quite to ZZZZZ [Intersection] before descending to the final altitude. Create a clear path for the descent and verify the DME.

I was complying with a functional check of the aileron cable tension in the forward cargo area. With this task you must remove the forward bulk head right hand panel and cargo area floor boards. After the aileron cable tension checks; I completed the job card signing off all the tasks including the completion of the bulk head blow out panel and floor boards. I failed to secure the floor boards and the bulk head panel resulting in the aircraft flying in an unsafe condition in which could of resulted in a catastrophic result. As a maintainer it is my responsibility to make the sure any and all aircraft is it a 100% airworthy. In the future; any task that is not completed will not be signed to make sure it is completed to ensure the aircraft is airworthy.

C Flight Attendant positioned behind the exit row on Aircraft X. Family boarded with 2 small children; one of whom would occupy a car seat. I believe car seat may have had blankets over it during boarding so it was difficult to tell that it was a car seat. It was also difficult to see what was going on in front of the exit row when I was behind it due to multiple passengers; seats; and distance in the way. We also became distracted at the end of boarding by a high maintenance passenger traveling with 2 dogs that attempted to sit in the exit row that took some time to relocate. It was getting close to our departure time; so C needed to brief the exit row passengers and get to the front. Due to the above factors; it was missed that the family with the car seat was one row in front of the exit row. This was not noticed until A flight attendant did the final walkthrough shortly prior to being cleared for departure. At that point; the plane was in motion; the seatbelt sign was on; and every row was occupied. There was not somewhere we could realistically move a family of 4.We could have more specific instructions for the D Flight Attendant during boarding; to position on an opposing part of the exit rows from the C on the 800 to be able to see what is going on all around the exit row. The 737-800's exit row is 3 entire rows; and it is difficult to see what is going on all around it with just one person located there. Additionally; the D positioned in the exit row during boarding would keep the exit row monitored during potential distractions; like having to relocate a passenger from the exit row.

Passengers bag was too large to fit in overhead bin; I advised bag needed to be checked. I noticed the face mask was down and asked her to cover her nose and mouth. She said that her mask just fell down when she sat in her seat. I advised her that her mask had been down since she tried to stow the bag. She started yelling at me and ask if I was the mask police? I said no; but if your mask doesn't fit; I can provide you another mask; she declined. I told the Captain and forward Flight Attendant because the passenger would not comply. Forward Flight Attendant asked passenger if she needed another mask; she complied wearing her own mask. I had to ask the passenger to pull up her mask a second time.  When deplaning; passenger had mask down again.

Three passengers boarded with their masks down. I asked them to pull them up. When collecting trash; before door closure; I noticed passenger in XX4 mask down again.  I asked her again to pull up mask and advised of policy; she laughed at me; then mocked what I had said. I asked again if she would pull up mask. Then advised the Captain of the interaction since I had to speak to her twice while on ground. Passenger in YY3 had mask down for second time in cruise when I collected trash after service.  He claimed he was forgetful. XX4 passenger played the peek aboo game where she had mask down but each time I approached; before I reached her; would pulled it up. ZZ4 passenger asked me how many times I was going to ask him to pull up his mask. When deplaning; had mask down.

I experienced a loss of full engine power during initial climbout from RWY 32 at ZZZ. I had the student notify Tower we wanted to return for landing. We were still able to climb and engine was running smoothly so I wanted to keep as low key as possible. When asked by tower we did not request assistance and did not require priority handling. We climbed to approximately 1;000 feet AGL as we entered left downwind. Just past abeam approach end of RWY 32 while on downwind engine experienced further reduction in power without moving throttle. I told Tower we were turning base at that time; added 10 degrees flaps and aimed for the approach end of RWY 32. As we rolled onto final there was a business jet in position for takeoff on the runway. I heard the Tower clear him for an immediate takeoff; then tell him to abort and hold position. The Controller then advised me they could not issue a landing clearance. I responded that I had to land and Controller told me about the jet in position. I replied that I observed the traffic and would maneuver to pass to the right so I would not overfly the jet; and come in around and land in front of him. I flew a few hundred feet to the right at approximately 300 feet AGL; did not fly over the jet and passed well clear of him holding in position. I then slipped the aircraft into the left crosswind; lined up and landed on Runway 32 about 1;500 feet down the runway. The engine was generating enough power to taxi and I utilized Taxiway XX and called ground control when instructed for taxi back to ramp. When I reviewed the event; I believe I incorrectly assumed that the tower controller was providing me priority handling; when in fact the Controller was not fully aware of my situation. I should have just requested priority handling either prior to or as I turned downwind so Controller knew not to have the jet line up and wait on the runway before me.

Practicing landings and flight pattern. As practicing the landing I was told to pull quickly for the flare process of the plane and caused a miscommunication as to pull on the yolk quicker. With the miscommunication caused the airplane to balloon and land on the nose wheel hard and causing internal damage to the nose wheel internals; ripping almost all the screws off the nose wheel screws keeping it attached to the plane. After the incident the instructor took control of the plane and went on a go-around and landed the plane with the nose being as high off ground as possible.
Report_2_Narrative: I was instructing my student on landing; focusing on the timing of flaring. He flared too much; which made him balloon. And to correct for it; he pushed down on the control to bring the nose down and landed nose first. The process was too quick for me to intervene. I heard a cracking sound as the nose wheel touched down on the runway. I executed a go around and made a soft field landing; wheelbarrowing the plane all the way down to the taxiway connected to our flight school's entrance. When I exited the plane; I visibly verified that the nose strut was sitting abnormally. I alerted our maintenance tech; who has verified that the nose strut was damaged.

Report_1_Narrative: Briefed the ILS 26L IAH prior to top of descent (DRLLR 5). Center delayed our descent due to traffic. When descent clearance was given; told to maintain 310 kts. or greater. We were already over 6;000 ft. above descent profile. We were able to get back on profile just prior to approach handoff. When switched to Approach Control; they told us to expect RNAV Y 26R. After positive aircraft transfer control; I loaded the RNAV 26R and briefed the approach. We were expecting vectors to final RNAV Y 26R. I became PF (Pilot Flying) again. We were still descending on the arrival passing through approximately 8;000 ft. and 6 miles from SKLER when approach cleared us the RNAV Y 26R approach. We then realized the RNAV approach started at SKLER and included an arc segment to final. The Captain reloaded the approach with the SKLER transition. As we approached SKLER; everything looked good as we accomplished approach procedures. Autopilot was still engaged. Approaching DRKAR; we were expecting the aircraft to start a right hand turn on the arc. It didn't turn as we continued straight ahead. Captain said we need to turn as I selected HDG SEL (Heading Select) to start a right hand turn on the arc. We were a couple seconds late to start the turn. At this time ATC said we were late on the turn and gave us vectors to intercept visual [Runway] 26R. Visual approach and landing were uneventful. We debriefed after block in and recreated load the FMC. Although the arc portion showed on the route; there was also a vectors line after DRKAR. We think this is why the aircraft didn't turn on the arc and maybe we needed to sequence the FMC.

We were cleared for takeoff. We set takeoff power and started our takeoff roll. At around 80 KTS the center runway red warning lights came on indicating another plane was on the runway. We queried ATC and they aborted our takeoff.

Please thank the Customer Service helpers for arriving immediately. 10 minutes prior to departure; the lead Flight Attendant (FA) informed us there was a female passenger who was dizzy and had vomited. The FA was concerned about the passenger's fitness to fly and requested we call for the Customer Service helpers. They arrived and immediately removed the passenger. I suggested to the Gate Agents that they consider calling for Medical Assistance. I discussed the situation with the lead FA and First Officer. The lead FA said the passenger had described a history of kidney illness. We all agreed we had not received any information that would cause us to suspect COVID-19 illness. We departed on time. I described the event to our Dispatcher via ACARS. After arrival; I called the Duty Officer and the Chief Pilot on Duty and reported the event to them.

After landing and during rollout on RW (Runway) XXR; we were instructed to 'make left turn if able and hold short of [Runway] XXL; then notify if gate was open'. As PF (Pilot Flying); I had already briefed during approach briefing that we would likely make left turn on [Taxiway] XX then hold short of [Runway] XXL to await further taxi instructions. As we decelerated and approached [Taxiway] XX; we noticed another airplane on Taxiway XY (around [Taxiway] XZ) facing east. We were initially uncertain if it was holding or continuing east so we were going to skip [Taxiway] XX and make next left. ATC stated that they were holding for us; so we made the turn on [Taxiway] XX. As we exited the RW (Runway); the CA (Captain) continued taxiing to cross RW (Runway) XXL even though we had been told to hold short. We were across the hold short lines when I queried him about holding short; but I was late in warning him to avoid the confusion and mistake of going so far. At same time; an airplane had rotated for takeoff on RW (Runway) XXL just prior to our position making it a more serious event. ATC never said anything to us as the event happened or immediately after; so I asked them if we should continue across RW (Runway) XXL. They replied in the affirmative and to contact Ground Control after exiting on other side. At no time did ATC comment on the event. We continued to the gate without further incident.Contributing factors were the confusion about the intention of the aircraft on Taxiway XY near the [Taxiway] XX exit we were instructed to use; the short distance between the parallel runways; and lack of coordinated and deliberate attention to the immediate situation we were in. I failed to give the CA a reminder we had been instructed to hold short of RW (Runway) XXL prior to him crossing the hold short lines. Neither of us confirmed both directions were clear prior to crossing even though it is SOP to do so and we had consistently been doing that up to that point. He said during our debrief he had momentarily looked down at his taxi diagram to confirm location of the potential crossing taxiways we might use before realizing too late we had taxied over the lines. I had also momentarily diverted my attention to try to contact ramp (per ATC's request about our gate availability); while assuming the CA had heard the hold short instructions and would comply. Again; we had already briefed this likelihood of taxi route and necessity of holding short of and eventual crossing of RW (Runway) XXL during the arrival/approach briefing during descent into ZZZ area. I had never received a query from ATC about gate availability in ZZZ; even though it's common procedure at other airports. Therefore; it affected my normal after-landing expectations and flow. That's not an excuse; but was definitely a factor.Weather was not a factor; nor was the lighting due to being midday. I don't believe fatigue or other physiological factors contributed either.
Report_2_Narrative: CAVU. RNAV [Runway] XXR. FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying). Upon landing standard transfer of controls on roll out. I planned on exiting [Taxiway] XZ to [Taxiway] XY to get behind a Airbus on [Taxiway] XY. ATC states 'exit [Taxiway] XX hold short of [Runway] XXL on [Taxiway] XX' (and gate status I think). The Airbus on [Taxiway] XY held short of [Taxiway] XX as directed by ATC. As we were in front of the Airbus as directed (on [Taxiway] XX) I was looking at chart and outside to see which way [Taxiway] XX veered (it looks like it veers left or right and is a wide taxiway). I was quickly comparing real life view to the chart diagram. As I was at that point we had already passed the hold short line. It then realized it and stopped. My FO asked 'if it's okay to cross [Runway] XXL'; ATC cleared us across [Runway] XXL at [Taxiway] XX left on [Taxiway] XA. All the while ATC communication was busy with the changing of our exiting directions to switching to ground. I have been to ZZZ multiple times before although I was unclear of how [Taxiway] XX is setup and viewed in real life. I was shocked what I had accidentally allowed to happen. I deviated from my exiting plan and slightly rushed thus missed seeing the HS (Hold Short) line and my situational awareness. I am at 100% fault in not continuing to [Taxiway] XZ as planned; taking on the slightly rushed exit; and most certainly not stopping as directed. I am still criticizing myself as I write this. I can guarantee that I will be more diligent and aware of actual versus chart of my location at all times.

Report_1_Narrative: While unloading Aircraft X a BE-1900C in ZZZ1; I discovered HAZMAT that had not been declared to the crew. The HAZMAT was a 'Battery; wet filled with acid' UN2794; class 8. The HAZMAT had the appropriate paperwork; but the pilot notification paperwork was not completed; nor was the crew notified. Appropriate company management personnel were notified and a SMS was completed.

While in the downwind leg of the pattern working with a student; Tower cleared our aircraft number one for touch and go on. They also directed an RV9 to enter left base. The RV9 was asked to report us in sight. After several moments; they reported us in sight and Tower cleared them number two to land. We then turned base and the RV9 turned base inside of us. When we rolled our wings level; tower called the RV9 to tell them they had turned inside of our turn. The RV9 was then cleared to land number one; however; he had already entered an evasive 180 degree turn to his right and when I gained visual contact again; he was directly above us with less than 200 feet separation. We were unable to maneuver evasively at our low altitude but he was able to pass over us with no further conflict.

Volume during this session was not particularly high; but complexity and workload changed quickly; and a sequence of events took my attention away from aircraft that didn't need much of it; including the aircraft in question. Aircraft X descending into ZZZ. My initial descent clearance was to 15;000 ft. An eastbound departure was potentially in conflict with 3 other aircraft; including Aircraft X. Deconflicting this aircraft took much of my attention during this time. Aircraft X ended up being no factor. Once clear of all other aircraft; I gave Aircraft X descent clearance to 11;000 ft. Read back was not as clear as I thought; but I had other things to worry about; including two aircraft simultaneously calling for VFR flight following and a conflict alert between VFR aircraft I was providing flight following to and a Mode-C intruder which took much of my attention because they were tracking to pass about 1 mile apart and the Mode-C intruder was climbing into the other VFR aircraft. Once that situation cleared up and as Aircraft X was descending through 12;000 ft. I received a call from FSS (Flight Service Station) which I decided to answer on the assumption that it would be a faster phone call than it turned out to be.Their request was for a Special VFR departure (SVFR). I had to take time to verify that SVFR was possible from this airport and since SVFR is a very rare request in my area of specialization I then had to look up the phraseology to give the clearance. By the time the clearance was delivered and read back; at least 2 minutes had passed. As I disconnected the FSS call; Low Altitude Alert showed Aircraft X at 7;900 ft.; descending below the MIA (Minimum IFR Altitude) of 8;000 ft. I immediately issued a low altitude alert with a clearance to climb to 10;000 ft. immediately. After the pilot responded I advised the pilot that his descent clearance had been to 11;000 ft. and asked if he had ZZZ in sight. Pilot responded that he had the field in sight. I cleared Aircraft X for a visual approach and gave instructions to contact Tower. It did not appear that he observed the climb clearance that accompanied the Low Altitude Alert; and the aircraft was at 7;300 ft. when I cleared Aircraft X for a visual approach.It is difficult to recommend any actions or changes to prevent re occurrence. This particular sector is quite complex; with high terrain; military airspace; two approach control facilities; arrivals to and departures from ZZZ1 in all directions. Convergence between ZZZ arrivals/departures to the south of ZZZ and ZZZ1 arrivals/departures to the southeast of ZZZ1. Also ZZZ2 arrivals and departures to the northwest of ZZZ2 all come together in the vicinity of ZZZ3. Also several satellite airports where pilots cannot reach us on the ground resulting in FSS relays. 11 adjacent sectors adjusted for typical sector configuration with multiple combined but the actual number of individual adjacent sectors is much higher and a relatively heavy VFR workload. It can unexpectedly become quite busy; even with a small number of aircraft in the sector. When this happens; it usually entails coordination calls from one or both of the approach control facilities; the military controlling agency; FSS; and one of the 11 adjacent sectors; and often includes aircraft that require a higher-than-normal workload. The timing of these factors is not controllable; so when several things happen at once; it usually can't be predicted; it can become difficult to manage; and it's often over before a D-side (Radar Assist) can make it to the sector to help.

Aircraft X entered a terrain area at 7;200 ft. with a Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA) of 7;400 ft. Aircraft X was cleared for the RNAV [Runway] XX approach. Aircraft X executed the missed approach. Aircraft X requested vectors for the RNAV [Runway] XX Approach. I issued a turn 30 degrees left when able direct to the Initial Approach Fix (IAF) and notify me when he was direct. Aircraft X reported they were ready for the approach clearance. I issued '[Aircraft X] maintain seven thousand until established. Cleared RNAV approach Runway XX at ZZZ.' Aircraft X read back the correct clearance.Aircraft X continued on their heading past the IAF without beginning a procedure turn for the approach. I was busy with multiple requests from other aircraft and fighters departing a military base for active Warning Areas. And didn't notice Aircraft X missed the fix. I was giving a relief briefing and noticed Aircraft X was not on his cleared route. I asked him where he was heading. Aircraft X stated he was waiting for vectors to the approach. I pulled up the MIA's and noticed he was about to enter a 7;400 ft. MIA block. I issued a safety alert for the terrain and climbed Aircraft X to 8;000 ft.In my scan I knew that Aircraft X was out of position but assumed he was unfamiliar with how to execute the procedure turn at the IAF and was going to get back on a correct course soon. Every time I checked back in my scan he was a bit further off course. The workload was high. Frequency congestion was causing a lot of missed and stepped on clearances further increasing workload. I had requested help from the Controller-in-Charge twice prior to this happening. I needed assistance with a flight break up flight plan and to notify the surrounding facilities the Special Use Airspace went cold. A dedicated D-side (Radar Assist) would have helped notice the aircraft off course.

ATC assigned a vector '[Heading] 140 and maintain 3;000 ft. until established on localizer....' As Pilot Monitoring I heard the vector and maintain 2;000 ft. until established. Captain heard 2;000 ft. as well and entered 2;000 ft. I read back 2;000 ft. to the controller. The Captain briefed and all checklist items were briefed prior to this event. The Captain flew through the localizer due to his failure to move the localizer frequency from Standby to Primary. He quickly recognized it and visually maneuvered aircraft back toward course. We received a Terrain Alert and we discussed to shallow our intercept angle. The course was intercepted safely. The controller transmitted that the altitude was 3;000 ft. until established. We were at approximately 2;000 ft. at that time. I transmitted that we had heard '2;000 ft. until established' and read that back to him earlier. We maintained 2;000 ft. until glideslope intercept. We landed uneventfully.
Report_2_Narrative: Beautiful day; 30 mile visibility; clear skies; airport in sight 30 miles away. Routine departure from ZZZ. At altitude; we were given direct to a fix and descend to 3;000 ft. Approaching the fix estimating 5;000 ft. altitude ATC gave us a clearance: 'Left turn of (EST) about 30 degrees and intercept localizer' which was inside of the fix 'and descend to 2;000 ft.' The First Officer clearly repeated to ATC '2;000 ft.' and no ATC correction was said. I set 2;000 ft. in the altitude alerter and pointed at it until the First Officer repeated '2;000 ft. A slight north wind caused my track just slightly south of center line of final; well within reason; estimate one dot right. As I was correcting and back on centerline the GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning) sounded; when back on center line the GPWS stopped. The hill was always in sight and we were never left of center line; never was there any danger. At that moment ATC asked if we had the airport in sight and we said yes. ATC cleared us for a visual approach to the runway and said our clearance was only to 3;000 ft. Uneventful landing. I clearly heard and after discussion; the First Officer also clearly heard '2;000 ft.""

Report_1_Narrative: The timing of the situation was ripe for a mistake; as a similar-sounding call sign received the exact type of clearance we would have expected as we approached the end of the runway while I was task saturated with completing the required checklist. Ideally; I would not have misheard the call sign; but ultimately I should have realized my attention had been entirely focused on the checklist; not the radio; and asked Tower for a short delay; clarification; or repeated clearance. We were operating the second leg of the day as Aircraft X from ZZZ to ZZZ1. We were given a short taxi from the terminal to Runway XXR. Approaching the departure end of XXR I switched to Tower frequency while still completing the Before Takeoff Checklist. As we made the turn from B onto B5 and while still finishing the last few steps of the checklist; Tower cleared another aircraft with a similar-sounding call sign; to line up and wait on XXC. I mistakenly thought the line up and wait clearance was for us and read back a clearance to line up and wait on Runway] XXR. Tower did not acknowledge my call; and while the Captain and I both saw an aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR; it appeared there was adequate spacing for the line up and wait clearance to be reasonable. As our aircraft passed the hold short line; Tower cleared the similar call sign for takeoff on XXC. The Captain and I simultaneously realized there had been a possible mix up of call signs. We advised tower we had read back the line up and wait clearance and asked for confirmation that it had been for us. Tower advised that it had not been for us; but cleared us for an immediate takeoff to accommodate the aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR. We immediately transitioned to a rolling takeoff and were able to get airborne before any go-around or other action was required by the aircraft on final.
Report_2_Narrative: It did not sound right to me when the Tower said to line up and wait; but after the First Officer read back the instructions and the Tower did not correct us we pressed on. Next time I am in a situation that does not feel right; I will stop what I am doing and verify the instructions before taking the runway.We were taxiing to Runway XXR in ZZZ. As we were approaching the hold short line we heard the instruction to line up and wait. The First Officer read back 'Aircraft X line up and wait XXR.' Both of us looked at final approach and saw an  aircraft on approximately a 5 mile final; I verified this with my MFD. As we turned on to the runway the Tower cleared an aircraft for takeoff that had a very similar call sign as ours. At this point; I verified our instructions to lineup and wait. Tower said that he did not clear us to line up and wait. The Controller immediately cleared us for takeoff and so we took off.

Report_1_Narrative: During taxi out for takeoff at ZZZ we received an ACARS message stating that 'the load close out was invalid' and that we must wait for a valid load close. I got distracted and forgot about getting a valid load closeout and we departed and flew uneventfully to ZZZ1. The Captain and I had had a successful trip. We worked well together. This was our last leg home on our last day. We had confidence in each other and perhaps that led to complacency. We had just been delayed at the gate for a COVID concern. I think we had a runway change and I got distracted.Perhaps; in the future; I will Scribble 'void' over the invalid load close out so that we do not mistakenly use it.

ZZZZ hub failed to notify us (Dispatch ZZZ) of Dry Ice being loaded in aft belly of aircraft. Required fuel over burn not included on release as a result.Failure of the ZZZZ hub to notify Dispatch ZZZ of Dry Ice being carried in forward or aft belly of a 777.

While flying the ZZZZZ RNAV departure this morning; ATC asked us if we were assigned a heading from the last Controller. The PM replied stating we were direct ZZZZZ1; with ZZZZZ behind us on the FMS legs page. This made us look closely at our legs page and moving map on our iPads; which showed us off course. ATC replied with 'roger.' Shortly following that; we were handed off to the next controller. When we checked in we mentioned our box input error and he said; 'No problem; proceed direct ZZZ1. How's that?' No further discussion with ATC. I immediately knew what happened and why. The FMS would not download our flight plan (not uncommon) so I built in manually. As I looked at the clearance I noticed the departure and selected it; but failed to assign the ZZZZZ1 transition. ZZZZZ1 appeared on our route packet as a different fix; not part of the departure and I fell for it. I had a lot of fixes and airways to type in so I focused on getting them correct and failed to notice how I had loaded the departure; without the transition. Ultimately we were missing 4 fixes in the legs page. I checked the first 5 fixes in the legs page to confirm the altitude restrictions since we were cleared to climb Via. Normally I check all the fixes but the other contributing factors today were no coffee or breakfast at this point. I wanted to get setup and ready so I could get coffee. Due to COVID; food and coffee was not available at the hotel so we catered it for our morning departure. Additionally; the pax showed up XX minutes early which squashed my plans to eat before we took off. Additionally; the lead pax birth date was incorrect on our brief so we were further distracted.Human error. I caused this event during the FMS setup. The pre-flight distractions becoming more and more common place with passenger related issues of catering and name details; today it got me. Another potential cause could be how the flight plan route is displayed on our packets. I'm used to seeing something like 'ZZZZZ.ZZZZZ1 ZZZ2; etc.;' as the FMS shows; but the filed route on our flight plan shows 'ZZZZZ ZZZZZ1 ZZZ2; etc.' Seeing it with the period would have drawn my attention to the ZZZZZ1 transition. Suggestions - For me; all things being the same again; if the filed route on our flight plan packet showed 'ZZZZZ.ZZZZZ1' this deviation wouldn't have happened today. Regardless of this and the distractions; I need to be more careful.
Report_2_Narrative: Clearance was ZZZZZ departure ZZZZZ1 transition; data link would not download flight plan. Flight plan was manually entered by PF loaded ZZZZZ departure to ZZZZZ then direct ZZZZZ1.  When verifying flight plan off filed route page neither of us caught this manual entry error.  When asked if on heading by ATC we started wondering why so checked departure and GPS position and found our error.  Asked ATC if we needed to correct error and they said no and cleared us farther down line on clearance.  Possible contributing factor were passengers showed XX minutes early; the way ARINC flight plan filed route shows on first page.  Things could do to correct this issue on future.  Verify and check each fix on the departure and also check mark each fix on lat long pages in flight plan compared to what is in the box.The way filed route showed on ARINC page; not loading transition properly when manually entering flight plan.Cross check each leg of the departure versus the plate.  Also check mark each way point matches the lag/long pages in the ARINC flight plan.  Any time having to manually enter information verify everything a couple times.

Report_1_Narrative: I as Pilot Flying; was initially on a stable approach descending to 3;500 ft. on base for [landing]. I had briefed a Flaps 22 landing. As I was turning base to final (a left turn) and starting further descent; the winds shifted and resulted in a loss of tailwind; and my airspeed increased in the turn. At that same time Tower told us to turn 10 degrees right I believe due to traffic. Through all of this; my approach became unstable with too steep a descent; and got [we] a GPWS sink rate alert. I immediately executed a go around. ATC issued a new clearance and we came back and landed without incident. Suggest some more insight; and different scenarios taught on Flaps 22 landings possibly during training. Because of the higher airspeed vs. Flaps 45; it seems there is an increased chance of unstable approaches; especially if the winds shift as in my case. I carried too much power on this approach and also got distracted with the instruction from Tower to turn opposite of my base to final turn.

I went into the cockpit for a bathroom break at XA:30pm.   Neither pilot was wearing a mask.   I asked; 'please put on a mask;' and pointed to mine as I entered and saw they weren't wearing masks.   The First Officer reached for his mask and put it on as I paused upon entering and pointed to my mask.   I again asked the Captain to put his mask on; once again pointing to my mask with both hands this time.   The Captain just turned and looked forward out the window in front of him.  He would not verbally reply nor put on a mask.   He refused to follow company policy.   We did not speak the entire time I was up there for their break - he looked out the window; I stood silently by the door.     I had to stand there in the cockpit and potentially be exposed to COVID-19 and the serious consequences that could ensue.   It is my understanding that COVID policies apply to everyone!    When the First Officer swapped out; I said something to him about the captain refusing to comply with policies and procedures and being disrespectful.   The First Officer chose not to comment on the Captain's inconsiderate and dangerous behavior; although the First Officer was wearing a mask at that time and he kept it on while I was in the cockpit.I chose not to go up again; so a colleague said he'd help with the meals in 20 minutes.   At the time the colleague handed the meals up; the person taking them from him didn't have a mask on.   The Captain did not wear a mask when others went into the cockpit to help.  Several crew members chose not to go up there at all; several were offended and uncomfortable but chose not to say anything; and one said they didn't care that much.  Fortunately; we only flew one leg with the Captain.The Captain's behavior was inappropriate; disrespectful; non-compliant with company policy; and potentially quite dangerous.  His behavior was unsafe and unacceptable on all fronts.

Captain reported that the CPDLC [Controller-Pilot Data Link Communications] accept and reject buttons were switched; this caused 2 occasions of erroneous reject responses being sent to ATC. On arrival switches were confirmed in the wrong place (IPC (Illustrated Parts Catalog) 31-10-51-02) and switched back (SWPM (Standard Wiring Practices Manual) 20-84-13). This report is to raise awareness that these switches can be inadvertently transposed.

A near midair collision occurred while in the traffic pattern. I was cleared to take off after [a] Cessna and was told to follow traffic. I took off and turned to John Wayne's standard 15 degree offset heading while climbing to TPA. Around 500 feet AGL I realized I no longer had traffic to follow in sight. I heard the Cessna request a short approach and ATC approved [him] for a short approach and clearance to land [Runway] 20L; after this I assumed that the traffic to follow was downwind and that I would have visual contact if I turned towards crosswind and downwind. As I was in the turn ATC asked me if I had traffic to follow in sight still. I reported back that I no longer had traffic in sight. ATC then told me to just fly a wide downwind for spacing. At this point the Cessna reported that [they] needed to make a right turn to avoid me. This whole time the Cessna was not in my visual; so they must have been beneath us and out of line-of-sight. I can only speculate on how exactly we converged as I had zero visual reference at this point with the Cessna. The Cessna and I continued flying the pattern together with no further incident but I did notice this Cessna was not flying the standard 15 degree offset on upwind and seemingly a little lower than TPA. I am unsure if this was an attributing factor to the loss of separation we encountered.

During a tailwheel transition training flight; the aircraft departed the runway surface on landing. The winds upon time of landing favored Runway XX and a right-crosswind component of about 10 knots was present at the time of the occurrence. No other traffic was in the pattern and the approach was stable throughout. The touchdown was in a three point attitude and did not bounce. On the rollout; the student improperly managed rudder control and the aircraft rapidly developed a significant turn to the left. As Instructor; I took control of the aircraft and stopped the ground loop from further developing. The aircraft departed the hard surface of the runway at roughly a 10 degree angle at a fairly slow speed and came to rest in the grass about 20 feet from the edge of the runway. Upon shutting the airplane down; both the student and I exited the airplane to assess the situation. No signage or lighting was hit nor was there any damage present on the airplane. The grass was smooth and no holes were present.The situation developed from a normal crosswind landing to a runway excursion quickly. The cause was an improper control input by the student that was not caught in time by me; the instructor. Despite not reacting in time to prevent the excursion; the aircraft did not ground loop and no damage was sustained by the airplane. Any corrective action would be on my part as the Instructor; to take control sooner before such a significant turn could develop.

I was entering the traffic pattern at 45 degrees for a downwind entry for RWY14 at FIT. I had previously announced my intentions and location on the 122.7 CTAF. I did not see or hear the conflicting traffic during its go around and climb out from RWY14.  I was alerted to the traffic by an ADS B traffic alert on my iPad. At this point I could not see the traffic in my field of view. I turned 45 to downwind and added power to get away from the traffic.  Earlier I had heard the conflicting aircraft on the CTAF advising it was on the RNAV20 approach and would be breaking off the approach for a straight in to RWY14. Calls to the conflicting traffic before and after ADS B alert we're not answered.It all happened very quickly and I only glanced at the ADS B display; figuring my attention should be outside the airplane looking for the traffic. But the combination of repeated ADS B traffic warnings and not being able to visually see and avoid the traffic was quite stressful.  Today; I was able to get the conflicting traffic's flight path from flight aware and compare it to my own from Garmin Pilot. It appears the conflicting traffic did a low approach to RWY14 and a go around; making a left climbing turn close to my own flight path. From the flight paths; the time stamps; and the fact that I never made visual contact with the conflicting aircraft; I think it passed behind me; and estimate it may have gotten as close as 500 ft.Being more vigilant to visually identify and track what I thought was landing traffic; would likely have avoided this dangerous conflict. The fact that the conflicting traffic never responded to my radio calls should also have raised a red flag.

Flying the published missed with autopilot in NAV and VS ALT to capture 4;000 [feet] as assigned by ATC. I was instructed to fly heading 070. After setting the heading bug to 070 I forgot to switch AP from NAV to HDG mode. The autopilot then started into the hold turning right to 161. [ATC] asked where I was going and at that point lost control trying to turn too steeply back to 070. In an unusual attitude I was trying to recover. Eventually; [I] hit the straight and level button on the autopilot and climbed above the cloud deck; got reoriented and rejoined the approach and landed without further problems.

During a Flight Attendant crew change and passenger boarding; our Flight Attendant B advised Flight Attendant A and myself; Captain; that she received a call from her personal Physician that she had a positive result for COVID-19 test resulting from a pre-surgery screening. A Flight Attendant immediately advised B Flight Attendant to contact Crew Scheduling and request to be removed and replaced for the flight. The Captain immediately advised Operations Agent to discontinue boarding and have passengers in jet-bridge return to boarding area in terminal building; while B Flight Attendant gathered her belongings and removed herself from the aircraft.I then requested the Operations Agent to call Flight Attendant crew lounge to see if a Reserve Flight Attendant was available to assume vacant position. The Flight was originally advised of a 30 minute delay for inbound Flight Attendant from another flight. After approximately 10 minutes; we were advised that a Reserve Flight Attendant was enroute from the crew lounge to cover flight. The Flight took a 17 minute delay due to the Flight Attendant exchange.
