After getting clearance to push from both Towers to the 'Y' I initiated the pushback. As soon as I started my turn into the taxiway the pilot notified me of a jet speeding in our direction. I immediately braked and pulled back toward the gate. The pilot stated again that we had the clearance and the jet should have stopped. It did not stop and sped right by.

While driving the bridge to the aircraft the 737-900 door was opened prior to the bridge docking or the required two knocks by myself. While driving I don't expect to see the door get popped which is extremely distracting while I am moving heavy equipment towards an aircraft. If the slide had been deployed it could have caused me serious harm or even death. I file an average of 3 or 4 [safety reports] on this exact issue every single week. This has got to stop.

Aircraft X was showing out of the gate when the ramp tried to final. Once I finished working another flight I saw I had 5 Weight and Balance Check messages showing Hazmat not secure w/15 bags for flight Aircraft X. I called ZZZ Operations at first the zone controller did not want to be bothered said ramp will call me. After the ramp tried to final another 5 times the Lead [Ramp Supervisor] then decided to call the Load Planner. When he called he at first stated the Hazmat was below the line; which I corrected him and said nothing was ever below the line because the flight was not weight restricted. That line isn't what got my blood boiling; the lead then stated on a recorded called all of the Hazmat was no show and wanted me to hold it off. I refused because I couldn't understand why if it were a no show he would attempt to finalize the Manifest 10 times before calling me. When I asked him that question he hung up the phone. The lead then attempted another 6 time to finalize the Manifest with no bags in the pit; afterwards he called back to say the plane is going to have to come back because the Hazmat is onboard. If I would have been one of those push over load planners I would have simply held off the Hazmat taking the lead at his word and we would have had a plane takeoff with Hazmat onboard; not secured; and no pilot paperwork. That really irks me the fact that he lied on a recorded line to have me hold off Hazmat that he knew he loaded on the aircraft.
We had to bring back [taxing] flight because of [load configuration error] requirement of the15 bags [missing] around the Dangerous Good cargo.

There was a break before the next flight to ZZZ.  Flight attendants left the aircraft.  First Officer (FO) left the aircraft.  Captain left the aircraft as one of the FAs came back to the aircraft.  Captain came back to the aircraft.  Both FAs and FO were on board.  Also a Ramp Agent (RA) was on board sitting in the back of the plane resting.  One of the flight Attendants told the Captain she was concerned that the RA did not complete the security search.  From her perspective it seemed as if the security check was not complete.  Captain went back and asked the RA if he had completed the security search.  He said yes.  Captain asked what was involved with the search and he described that it was a visual search and where he looked.  Captain told the Flight Attendant that the Ramp Agent said he did it.  Flight Attendant was not sure because the RA hadn't lifted up the seat cushions and looked behind them or put the tray tables down as the FA had seen done in previous security searches.  Captain asked the RA if he could do the security check again and that there was a concern that it was not complete.  The RA started doing another security check lifting up the seat cushions in the first couple rows.  Captain went to the flight deck to prepare for the flight.  Captain assumed that the security check got completed the second round and all was good to go.  Flight departs for ZZZ; Captain and FO review the security section of Flight Ops Manual (FOM).  After arrival in ZZZ; the Captain goes to review the security section with FAs.  It is then that he hears that the Ramp Agent did not go completely through the cabin on the second time and check seat cushions and tray tables at ZZZ1.  The RA talked with another RA and then told the Flight Attendants that the search had happened and it was complete.  The Captain called the Duty Officer and had a discussion about the situation and the wording in the FOM.  Duty Officer told the Captain that he would call back after a few minutes while he made a call to find out what the Ramp manual says to do.  After the Duty officer called back to the Captain he referenced the Services Manual about aircraft search to and from ZZZZ; wording was not the same as what is in the FOM.  The Duty officer agreed that there is room for some confusion between the wording in the two manuals.  Captain decided to stop the operation and have ZZZ ramp agents do a Full Search before continuing on to ZZZ2.  A Big thank you to ZZZ RAs in knocking out a Full Search.         My thoughts (Captain) there is some confusion in how the FOM describes a cabin search and what a visual inspection actually is on the security form.Pay closer attention to details.  Don't assume anything; make sure everyone is on the same page in understanding of what's happening.  Ask questions; try and understand the full picture.  It's been awhile since our airplanes have made ZZZZ trips and it was the first time for our crew the other day since prior to COVID-19.  We need to show each other respect; and give grace in this super stressful time.  After talking with the Duty Officer; I more fully aware of the staffing issues with ramp; COVID-19 cases and just under staffing in different areas which has been extremely difficult.  I also see a value in debriefing the crew at the end of the trip or after abnormal situations as a learning time for all as we grow as professionals.

During boarding Family A was sent down to aircraft not wearing face covering. Passengers were asked the age of the child at the entry door and stated 3 years old. Passenger stated at the doorway she had the child's mask in her purse and would put it on at their seat location; I apologized and stated the mask would need to be on the child before boarding the aircraft. Family A then stepped out on the Jetway; pulled the mask out and said it would be difficult getting the child to wear and keep the mask on. I wanted to have the passengers go back up and speak with customer service; but thought they would comply. As other passengers were boarding; Family A followed behind; I did not notice they had not put the mask on the child.After we completed the safety briefing and doing the walk through; Family A were sitting with the child; had not placed the mask on the child; mother had placed the mask back in her purse. I apologized again and stated she would need to put the mask on child. Captain had signaled for takeoff. I also mentioned to the parents the Captain wanted to go back to the gate and I told them I did not want to go back to the gate. The mother tried to put the mask on the child; the father tried; then stated he wanted to give her some medicine that would make her go to sleep. I informed the Captain what was going on and of our progress; Captain made the call to go back to the gate. Several managers met the aircraft. Family A was asked to deplane.

On [Date 1]; I was reviewing paperwork on the current Aircraft X in the [Maintenance Visit] in ZZZ and noticed that a robbed transponder on Aircraft X was installed the previous night and did not get a signoff in the logbook releasing the aircraft for RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum) operation. This aircraft was still scheduled to be in the [Maintenance Visit] for another 5 days so I just had the nightshift avionics tech complete the entry the next day. But I knew that the avionics tech that forgot the RVSM signoff on the [Maintenance Visit] also replaced a transponder on Aircraft Y during the overnight on [Date 2]. I pulled the paperwork from the night of [Date 2] and found that the #2 Transponder was changed to clear a MEL (Minimum Equipment List); but a RVSM release was not done in the logbook. I called Maintenance Control and told them what happened and Aircraft Y was inbound to ZZZ1 at the time so when it landed in ZZZ1; Maintenance went out and deferred the RVSM. The ZZZ Avionics tech that replaced the transponder is [Name].Found during paperwork reviewShould of; but did not realize the transponder is RVSMCalled Maintenance Control and had the system deferred (RVSM)[Parts Document] lists the PN (Part Number) of the transponders that go on our aircraft that are post ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast) mod. This PN [XXX] is not listed in the aircraft IPC (Illustrated Parts Catalog); so there is no statement (RVSM Critical Item) like all the other transponder PN's listed in the IPC. This statement (RVSM Critical Item) should be added to the [Parts Document]; to bring attention to it like the PN's listed in the aircraft IPC.
Report_2_Narrative: On [Date 2] I was assigned to clear a Transponder #2 MEL (Minimum Equipment List) on Aircraft Y. I determined it needed a new transponder; however; we did not have the proper transponder in stock required for ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast) mod aircraft. I was directed to rob the transponder from Aircraft X which was the [Maintenance Visit]; after robbing (from Aircraft X) and installing (on Aircraft Y) the transponder; I went through the operation checks and everything was good. I went to sign off the paperwork and seen; from the beginning of Chapter 34 in the AMM (Aircraft Maintenance Manual); that it was not CAT II (Category 2). I forgot that RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum) isn't included in the beginning of ATA (Air Transport Association) chapters and since I picked the part number based on [Number] I didn't see the RVSM reminder note seen in the AIPC (Aircraft Illustrated Parts Catalog). I went to open the GMM (General Maintenance Manual) on the tech computer I was using and it wouldn't open. It was known within the base that PDF's can act up on the 2 provided tech computers. My ignorance of not remembering that RVSM wasn't included in the beginnings of ATA chapters; and not going into AIPC; and assuming that since RVSM wasn't downgraded it wasn't an RVSM component; along with my laziness of not confirming with a lead or Supervisor on their computers contributed to missing the RVSM release sign off. The plane made at least one flight with out having the RVSM release. The next night I installed new transponder in Aircraft X and Operations checked everything and since I assumed it wasn't RVSM the day before I repeated my mistake for the [Maintenance Visit on Aircraft X]; and didn't write up a RVSM release sign off. It was caught before the aircraft left and the other avionics technician went through the operation checks again and provided the RVSM release sign off.The day shift Supervisor caught the [Maintenance Visit] not having the release and knew that I did the aircraft the night before and seen that I didn't release that one either and took corrective action.I didn't put max effort into looking into seeing if the component was RVSM critical. I went off of a handful of assumptions and didn't double check with my leads or Supervisor. The [Number] didn't reiterate it was an RVSM critical item; the GMM PDF didn't want to load on the tech computers; RVSM isn't located at the beginning of ATA chapters like CAT II and RII (Required Inspection Item) are.Put RVSM in beginning ATA chapters like CAT II and RII. Add a reminder in [Number] that the transponder is RVSM critical component. Remove old transponders from stock that can't be used with ADS-B aircraft seeing that our whole fleet has ADS-B mod. Leadership verified wrong part number in stock the night before contributing to this issue being on 2 aircraft due to robbery. Update/upgrade/fix tech computers so we can open the files we need.

Report_1_Narrative: After doing 5 touch and goes on runway 28R; I received clearance to land. I did a wheel landing; let the tail come down; and when the aircraft was traveling slowly at about 15 kts or less; I started turning left to aim for the taxiway exit; and the aircraft kept turning to the left and did a complete 360 ground loop on the runway. The aircraft did not suffer any damage or touch any part of the aircraft to the runway; except the wheels of course; and I did not leave the runway. Due to this unexpected delay the aircraft landing behind me had to go around. I attribute this ground loop to unexpected strong crosswind; and the fact that we just put air in the tires and the aircraft was a lot more responsive to rudder control inputs that on my previous flight. She just got away from me for a moment.

After takeoff Runway X on the ZZZZZ3 departure ZZZZZ transition; the Pilot Flying called for clean wing configuration; Climb Power and VNAV.  Pilot Monitoring failed to properly select and verify the selected vertical command.  PF flying did not receive the correct level off climb guidance.  Human error focused on initially to cross ZZZZZ1 above 7;000 feet NOT at or below 7;000 feet. ATC immediately instructed aircraft to 'STOP CLIMB' and level off in which we complied with.Contributing factors:Pandemic and lack of flying time in last 6 months.Unfamiliar airport operations.
Report_2_Narrative: At departure called for clean wing; CLB; VNAV. PM cleaned wing and selected VNAV;CLB. I as PF failed to verify new VCLB mode on PFD. Contributing factor: - on initial brief i misread SID as cross ZZZZZ above 7;000 feet. The SID requires a cross ZZZZZ below 7;000 feet. - last flight was July 2020.

Report_1_Narrative: I was working two radar sectors combined with a trainee and trainer as my coordinator. The traffic volume was high with the normal arrivals; departures; overflights; and practice instrument approaches. The complexity was high due to a 10 minute notice of a flight check aircraft that needed to check an opposite direction approach. Once the flight check aircraft started his opposite direction approach I started briefing the relieving controller in training for a sector split. As the flight check aircraft had just finished with the opposite direction approach and continuing to brief the relieving controller for the split; I radar identified the flight check aircraft and turned him on course for the next approach. As all that was happening; I was descending and vectoring Aircraft X for the ILS approach to a different airport. Aircraft X was the second aircraft in the sequence of three for the ILS approach. As Aircraft X crossed 2 miles north of a satellite airport; Aircraft Y was departing VFR north bound climbing to 3;000 ft. without any communication with me. I called the traffic as soon as I saw the issue and Aircraft Y reported that he was maneuvering to avoid collision. A few seconds later Aircraft Y called VFR looking to pick up his IFR flight plan. Also the weather at each facility within the airspace was overcast or broken around 2;200 ft.

I signed Dangerous Goods Form for wet battery HAZMAT; only later did I realize that there needed to be a HAZMAT on board notation from Dispatch on the Dispatch Release notes section. This notation was missing. This notation is a recent change to the Dangerous Goods Form procedure.

During pushback a Flight Attendant called and informed me that a passenger was refusing to wear their face mask.  I asked if she wished to have the passenger removed for non-compliance to which she responded in the affirmative.  I had the push crew pull us backed to the gate and requested a Supervisor from operations.  Arriving at the gate; the Flight Attendant informed me that she made four attempts to have the customer replace his mask over his nose to which he refused or was otherwise non-compliant.  The Gate Agent boarded the plane and was unable to get the customer to wear the mask appropriately so he was removed.

While in cruise flight Flight Attendant (FA) A called the flight deck.  The First Officer answered the call.  He told me the FA had a non-compliant passenger with the mask.  He reported that she was tapping him trying to waken the passenger and he pushed her arm away.  We transferred control and I called her back and asked it everything was OK and to repeat what happened.  We ACARS Dispatch to have a Supervisor at gate.  We were in range when we received the call and elected to continue to ZZZ. The passenger was mask non-compliant.  He was told to cover his nose with the mask.  He was sleeping when mask slid down.  FA woke passenger with a tap; it may have startled him; but he pushed her hand away and swore.I believe the company has to make sure the passengers are aware of the seriousness of non-compliance.

During accomplishment of portable water system disinfect done by using ozone cart; as instructed by fellow mechanic assigned as support; (name unknown). I might have improperly connected the water supply line. This happened at ZZZ [Gate] around XA:00 [Date]. As soon as I noticed water coming out of the aft fuselage drain holes; I immediately stopped the re circulation process; notified crew chef; dumped over serviced wasted and requested for assistance form tech crew personnel.Lack of work experience with this specific task on my part; lack of assigned training; inability to complete work interdependently due to not having D1 endorsement and being able to drive on airport premises and move around with equipment and tools. Hostile work environment.Assigned training required to completed before being assigned to perform ozone cards; different diameter or tread pattern on water fill line attainment port preventing from improper installation; picture diagrams included in revised work card; hands on ozone unit classroom training; ZZZ ramp driving credentials; hangar access in below freezing conditions; better gate illumination; larger port markings allowing to differentiate between both ports housed only 3 ft. apart.
Report_2_Narrative: I was assigned to drive new transfer AMT's around since none of them had their DR1/3. One group required the 'blue cart' for water servicing; and the tech responsible for the job asked me questions about it. I gave him a basic description of the job; helped hooked the FILL line to the FILL nipple; and showed him the correct adapter for the job and threaded it in to show it was the right fit. After which I left to move other techs around. When I came back to the gate; that's when the results of the improper servicing occurred. The event occurred at [Gate] at roughly XA:00 on [Date] during the cycling phase of the disinfect.

Report_1_Narrative: My wife and I planned a round trip to visit some friends at a church in ZZZ and have lunch after the meeting. Our granddaughter stayed the night with us prior to our flight the next morning. She travels with us often and enjoys the trips; and has flown with us since she was born. The next morning;  we arrived at the airport and boarded the aircraft along with our friend and his wife. I did a weight and balance; and it came out more than 200 lbs under the gross weight limit. I verified and center of gravity and weight were well within limits. My wife had our granddaughter sit next to her in the seat; and I made sure they were buckled. Together they weighed less than 170 lbs and seemed to fit comfortably in the one seat. We departed mid morning. We had a good meeting and lunch in ZZZ; and after lunch we all got back in the aircraft and buckled up ready to go back. It occurred to me that our granddaughter is now 6 years old; and even though she is approximately the same height and weight as when she was 4 and 5; she should have her own seat and seat belt. In the future I will make sure each occupant of age has his or her own seat and seat belt; so they can be properly buckled and restrained.

[Aircraft X] was on an IFR flight to ZZZ1 on [Airway1] at 9;000 ft. There was another aircraft on [Airway2] also landing ZZZ1 just handed off to me also at 9;000 ft. The adjacent sector asked me if I would like [Aircraft X] at a different altitude and I gave them 8;000 ft.; which they complied with. Upon further review that was below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude of 8;600 ft. and [Airway2]'s Minimum IFR Altitude of 8;400 ft. I should have been more aware of the Minimum IFR Altitudes along [Airway2].

While working Radar West a C-130 came off of LRF requesting direct to HERCK intersection to hold. I misread the approach plate with the holding procedures and issued an altitude of 2000'. The hold at HERCK is depicted at 3000'. The aircraft entered a 2200' MVA while holding at 2000'. I noticed the MVA as they entered it and immediately climbed them to 2200'.

Flight Attendant was preparing for door closure checking bins and seat belts. Upon reaching Passenger X in XX2 noticed young man mid twenties wearing mask below his nose. She asked him to pull his mask up and got no response. She motioned to him to get his attention and repeated herself; but he just stared straight ahead in defiance. She had to repeat herself and asked if he understood English. He made an eye roll and told her; 'You can leave now;' dismissing her instructions.The Flight Attendant approached the cockpit and told me the situation. We were now past departure time and I stepped back to reiterate the mask policy verbally. When I approached his seat; he was staring straight ahead with the mask just barely over his nose. I explained Airline has a mask policy for everyone on the airplane except when actively eating or drinking; and that he was not being singled out and needed to follow the instructions of the flight attendants. His response in a raised voice was; 'I'm wearing the [expletive] mask!' and 'You're making me angry.'I asked him why he was angry and then what he thought he would do when he got angry. He just tried to stare me down and I decided there was no point in continuing this conversation with him. I made the decision to have Customer Service remove him from the full flight to prevent anymore altercations once airborne.

While boarding for our ZZZ-ZZZ1 flight; the Fwd FA came into the flight deck to tell us about a rude passenger. Passenger boarded the plane without wearing a mask and took a seat in X1. The Fwd FA approached and reminded passenger about the required mask policy. Passenger reluctantly complied and as the FA turned to walk away; the passenger called her an '[expletive]'. After the FA informed us about this; I first asked the FA if passenger is being disruptive. The FA stated that passenger was not. I then asked if she would like me to go speak to the passenger. The FA stated that was not necessary. Finally; I asked if she would like me to request a customer service representative. The FA again said no; the situation is under control and she would let us know if the problem continues. I messaged my Dispatcher to keep them in the loop of what was said. We pushed; de-iced; and while taxing to our assigned runway; I asked the FO to call and make sure there were no further issues in the back. The FA stated that the passenger had fallen asleep; so we elected to continue. About mid-way through the flight; the Fwd FA called up and stated that the passenger had woken up and was starting to cause some disturbances. The FO was already the PF and I asked him to take radios as well. The passenger asked for a cup of water so passenger didn't have to wear the mask because she was 'drinking'. The FAs let this go on for about '30 minutes' (quoting them) and the passenger had not drank any of the water. Passenger again was non-compliant with the mask and both FAs asked passenger three times to wear the mask. At this point; I sent another message asking for a customer service representative to meet us at the gate in ZZZ1 and explained to the Dispatcher everything the FAs had told me. About 5 minutes after that; the Fwd FA called up again. Again; I told the FO his controls; his radios. The FA then explained that the passenger was now starting to 'scream' on the plane and yelled very loudly; '#@%^! I'm not from [foreign country]!' While doing this; proceeded to flip off both FAs. I again messaged our Dispatcher with this information and now requested Security along with the customer service representative. The FAs and I talked back forth regarding passenger danger level. The passenger was remaining in the seat and making no other threatening gestures; so we did not see the need to declare an Emergency. We continued the flight to ZZZ1 with no further incidences. After landing; I had the FO call Ops just to make sure a customer service representative and security were waiting for us at the gate. Upon arrival into the gate; a customer service representative and security officer where indeed right there. The customer service representative immediately came onto the plane and removed the passenger.The FAs did not make any indication and no one told me they suspected the passenger was drunk. Seems as if passenger was just a genuinely disturbed person.We probably should have gotten security or customer service involved at ZZZ because of the name calling. Because the passenger had put on the mask and was making no further threatening behavior; we decided to make a note of it and continue. We all agreed though; that if passenger made one more derogatory comment; we would most likely be removing passenger from the flight. We had no further problems until mid-flight.

The mechanic who was assigned to work Aircraft X by chance found that the hydraulic reservoirs and accumulators for the green and yellow systems still had danger tags stating 'do not pressurize hydraulics' still hanging from them. These tags were dated XX/Sep/2020 and were from another company.

While enroute and at cruise altitude of 39;000 feet; with between 1:00-1:15 hrs of flight time remaining of a 3:40 hour flight: my stomach began to feel queasy; I started sweating; and in a short time felt the urgent need for a bowel movement. I advised the Captain of my need to use the lavatory and contacted the flight attendants. I quickly got out of my seat due to the urgency in my stomach and went to the door. When I stood up rapidly; I felt a rush of nausea as well as feeling light-headed. When the flight attendants knocked; I opened the door. The next thing I know I am sitting on the galley floor with a nurse sitting next taking care of me; with a doctor assisting. The doctor and nurse monitored my vital signs; which initially both blood pressure and oxygen levels were low. I began to feel normal shortly after they had me drink some water and ½ cup of orange juice; utilize the onboard oxygen and began to administer 2 bags of IV fluid. Blood pressure and oxygen levels returned to normal.I told them that I had been fasting and had not eaten for nearly 2 days. I also said I could tell I was dehydrated due to the limited amount of urine output on our first lavatory break 1-1/2 hours into the flight. Both agreed that this was probably a cause for what happened and called it a vagal response; which can cause a person to faint.Upon landing; we were met by paramedics at the gate. They took my vital signs to include a blood glucose test. After completing their assessment; they said I was fine and there was no need to take me to the hospital. After being released from the paramedics; the entire crew of went to the crew van and the overnight hotel.Medical Assistance provided a Hospital for me to be seen; to ensure I was medically able to fly (as a passenger) back to ZZZ. I went to the Hospital and received: initial screening; EKG; and blood tests to include Electrolytes; Hemoglobin; Triage Cardiac screen 'CUANTITATIVO; (INCLUYE TROPONINA I; BNP; MIOGLOBINA; CKMB Y DIMEROS D)'The doctor stated that I was in excellent shape and saw no problems or issues with any of my test results. I was released with no further action nor medications required.
Report_2_Narrative: On Aircraft X ZZZ to ZZZ1 my FO (First Officer) said he needed a lav break; and I said go ahead; I have the aircraft. He got out of his seat and waited for the (FA) Flight Attendant to call so we could open the door. As the door opened and the FA came into the cockpit; he said boy your FO is very sweaty. Not more than 30 seconds later; the back end called and said the FO had passed out in the forward galley or first class. At that point; the FA stayed with me on the flight deck for the duration of the flight as he was talking on the phone and giving me vitals so I could send too Dispatch. We had an ER nurse taking care of FO and she set up an IV and he began to get better and told us he wants to continue to ZZZ1. FO was checked at the gate as well as going to ZZZ1 hospital and everything was good with no further action.

Report_1_Narrative: During the crew brief we discussed the departure procedure and I selected LNAV on and confirmed it was armed via the PFD. After a maintenance issue at the gate and a rushed before takeoff checklist during a short taxi we took off. Shortly after takeoff; ZZZ tower asked why we didn't turn. Confused I looked at the PFD and LNAV was not operating. I told the Captain to turn in the correct direction and moved the heading bug to the correct heading. We don't know how or why LNAV disengaged after we both confirmed it was armed in the brief.Rushing the before takeoff checklist caused us not to verify everything in the MCP/ND check so we don't know if LNAV was still armed at takeoff. Other than training in the aircraft type simulator; I have not flown since early November. The Captain only has X00 hours in the aircraft.Suggestion - Don't rush the before takeoff checklist.

Flight was uneventful until cleared the ILSZ RWY 36 via direct QIT VOR at 18;000 feet assigned. A large build up was directly situated over QIT therefore ATC was asked for a left deviation due to the buildup. During the decent briefing the approach and contingencies were briefed; however; a storm disrupting the approach was not anticipated. A left deviation was started and I decided to proceed direct to point D111G as the best course of action. The FO had his terrain display in terrain mode allowing me to see any conflicts ahead. The FO correctly pointed out that going direct point D254I would be a better course of action and allow more time for the approach. I agreed and we asked for and was given direct to that point by ATC. At this point the situation was less than ideal. The weather was mostly cloudy with only brief glimpses of the terrain. I had the altitude of 10;500 feet in my mind thinking I was heading for my original point (D111G) when in reality we were required to be at 14;200 feet point D254I. I had started the descent when the FO pointed out the error and I immediately started to climb. At this point we were still not on the approach due to the weather. We were still proceeding direct to D254I. I was also slowing and configuring for the landing. I ended up disconnecting the autopilot in order to reestablish myself on to a stable approach. The terrain display never showed any conflicts and no verbal warnings of the system was encountered. I knew approximately where the aircraft was at all times. My error was in the decent altitude while forced off of the approach due to weather. We eventually did get on the approach; were stable when we broke out of the weather and had an uneventful landing. The large cell situated over the UIO VOR forced an unprepared diversion around it resulting in an off route decent in order to land. This in turn resulted in attaining a lower than desired altitude in heavily mountainous terrain. This was my first time into UIO. Although I reviewed the required airport familiar special qualification briefing; in this situation it was not adequate. I consider myself very experienced in mountainous terrain and I believe this particular field should require a Check Airman checkout. 'Picking the brain for gotchas' would have been invaluable.

While operating the flight controls as PF (Pilot Flying); and beginning the takeoff roll; after brake release; as power was applied and throttles passed through approximately 10;000 ft-lb torque; the Captain/Pilot Monitoring and I simultaneously heard and felt a 'thud' which we immediately interpreted as a possible load shift. The Captain called for a rejected takeoff which we conducted in accordance with company procedures. We taxied back to the ramp; parked and shut down all engines. All hands conducted an inspection of the load. The skid load consisting of approximately eight long steel drill pipes; approx 15 feet long by 18 inches in diameter appeared to have shifted approximately two linear feet aft from the original loaded position. (Caused by) inadequate securing of cargo skid load; suspected due to improperly affixed and/or inadequate number of aft securing straps and/or chains.
Report_2_Narrative: On takeoff roll a noise was heard and felt in the cockpit.  The takeoff was rejected and the aircraft returned to the ramp for evaluation.  Upon examination it was determined that a portion of the cargo had shifted aft. Insufficient aft restraint on the affected portion of the cargo.

Report_1_Narrative: During boarding procedures a passenger was advised of the mask wearing requirements; nose/face. Flight Attendant (FA) advised passenger nose exposure to FA B who also had to re-advise passenger.  Passenger appeared disgruntled about requiring nose coverage and Gate Agent advised she also had words with passenger with same response. FA B advised Captain of passenger's position against compliance.  Prior to door closure the Gate Agent was present in case passenger removal was necessary.An announcement was then made saying 'non-compliance passengers were noticed and removal option would be used for non-compliance.' Passengers were advised they had an agreement at ticket purchase this had been clear this was not an optional requirement for flight.Using this method passenger realized this wasn't a joke and removal would occur.  Captain advised FA B to do walk through for compliance and removal before door closer option.  This passenger had been advised over 3 times by 3 different individuals with non-compliance results and now aware airlines was serious about compliance.Entire plane was compliant during walking through and continued flight. During entire flight passenger continued to pull mask off nose and appeared disgruntled. Passengers around passenger kept saying 'FA caught you again each time' so passenger then laughed like it was a game.  We are to 'inform' at this point; which continued at every walk through; 'not enforce .' This was a constant occurrence during flight.After arriving and during departure this non-compliant passenger stopped and made rude remarks to FA A.  FA B went to Gate Agent during cleaning to advice of this passenger's attitude and non-compliance so Gate Agent could make a note for future travel. This passenger felt he was not going to wear mask as required. FA B advised gate agent row and seat area and agent opened up the location and verified passenger's information.  Due to constant resistance FA felt safer not to confront passenger directly. During 3 points this party was given clear instructions on mask covering requirements and possible travel denial.  They continued during every stage with 4 different advisory points pre-board at gate; boarding (flight attendants);  flight introduction (Captain's announcement) and inflight (flight attendants). With actions [during] deplaning this passenger let FA A know that they were above this rule.

On [a] flight to XNA we were cleared for the visual traffic pattern to land Runway 16. The Captain as the pilot flying had properly briefed the visual approach; to include the obstacles in the vicinity and highest obstacle on the approach plate. On right base; the Captain turned autopilot off with flaps 3 and gear down. I announced visual contact/no factor on the highest obstacles out the right window - a set of towers. We continued to slow and configure for landing and maintain an altitude of more than 1;000 ft. AGL. We received a caution from the GPWS; followed by a PULL UP verbal command. The Captain took the appropriate actions and immediately increased the airplane's altitude. We were in daytime visual conditions; and in accordance with company policy; determined we were still in a safe position to stabilize and conduct a safe landing after clearing the obstacle. We finished the turn to final; configured the aircraft for flaps 5 landing; completed the before landing checklist; were fully stable by 1;000 ft. and in a safe position to land the aircraft. Upon landing; we debriefed the situation; reviewing the approach plate for any obstacles. At more than 1;000 ft. AGL and no visual contact with an obstacle; we were unsure why the GPWS gave us the PULL UP command; but agreed that it was the most appropriate and safest action to respond to the command as we didGPWS PULL UP verbal command resulted in appropriate action of increasing aircraft altitude.A visual traffic pattern is one of the most difficult approaches we fly. I would suggest additional training during initial and recurrent training on distances and altitudes for different types of visual traffic patterns. I will personally work to improve my awareness as the pilot flying to ensure 1;500 ft. AGL on downwind and the 3:1 descent path ratio throughout the visual traffic pattern. I will review my flying skills to ensure I am flying this way as well. While I believe we took the appropriate action both during the event and after in the debrief; I will use this as a learning experience to ensure I am always a safe and skilled pilot.

On pushback from gate; ground crew asked me to set brake because they had an issue with the tow bar. It was difficult to understand what they were saying outside of normal communication procedures because the tug operator spoke poor English. The ground crew was in the process of switching tow bars when I selected the external horn and informed the crew to remove all equipment and provide me a wing walker for the turnout as I had plenty of room to accomplish the taxi out. I watched the upper ECAM and waited for the 'NW STRG DISC' message to extinguish as confirmation of pin being removed. I also received a 'wand up with pin flag in hand' signal/salute. Taxi out was normal. On climbout; Center called and said the ground crew asked them to inform me that the nose wheel steering disconnect pin was still in the nose gear. I immediately knew this was false as I had confirmed before taxi out that it was removed and also the aircraft would not be able to be taxied with the pin installed. I contacted ops on company frequency and informed them that no pin was in the nose wheel. Again; I had a hard time understanding them as their English was poor.  I then received a message from Dispatch and my understanding was they had lost the pin; assumed it was on the nose gear and then later found the pin. As a precaution; on arrival; I inspected the nose wheel and found no discrepancies. As a side note; it appeared that the tow bar being used was much smaller than normal. The tow bar they were going to swap it with was much larger.Cause - Poor English and communication.

Prior to taking off I checked the UAS FM (facility maps) and observed that I could not exceed 50 feet AGL as I was in ZZZ Class B airspace. In an error; I did not file a flight plan and instead flew the drone; keeping it at an altitude of 50 feet AGL or below. After ending my flight I realized my error and immediately contacted my company UAS coordinator. In the future I shall always remember to file a flight plan and receive authorization before flying in controlled airspace.

I was flying right seat. After landing; Runway XX and exiting at [Taxiway] XX1; Tower radioed; 'taxi to ramp; this frequency.' PF (Pilot flying) proceeded to taxi south on Taxiway XX; and then turned right onto Taxiway XX4; into the FBO's ramp.I noticed Aircraft Y; parked close to our taxiway (on our right side); and asked PF to slow down; which he did. I then asked PF to come left (away from the aircraft) which he did. This put us left of the taxiway centerline (away from the aircraft). In the dim light; it looked as if we would clear the aircraft wingtip; particularly since we had come so far left. Unfortunately; the wingtips made contact.We then taxied to parking to deplane our passengers. After our passengers departed; I informed the FBO. PF walked to Taxiway XX4 and measured the distance from taxiway centerline to the wingtip of Aircraft Y. The distance measured 32 feet 8 inches. There was a cone under the wingtip. The wingtip did not have a static wick cover on it.
Report_2_Narrative: Taxiing into ramp on center line we were turning into the FBO ramp and clipped the wingtip of Aircraft Y. The wingspan of our aircraft make/model is 69 feet 6 inches and from Center line to the edge of Aircraft Y was 32 feet. The PIC (Pilot in Command) who was in the right seat told me to adjust over some and I did. He then thought we were clear of Aircraft Y. While taxiing by the tips met by a few inches. It only scratched the winglet of [our aircraft]; but bent the wingtip of Aircraft Y.

Report_1_Narrative: While conducting RNAV Runway 15 approach into ESN; received a low altitude alert from Easton Tower while at our MDA of 440 ft. MSL. We immediately climbed to 550 ft. MSL and conducted a missed approach once at the MAP. My assumption is that the aircraft descended too rapidly; causing us to be below glide slope as indicated by the PAPI's.

While entering the downwind via the 45 for Runway XX; right traffic at ZZZ; we were following a Cessna. I was sitting in the passenger seat as the NFP (Non-Flying Pilot)/Safety Pilot observing my friends landing. We completed our landing checks and continued following the Cessna. As the Cessna reached our four o'clock position I told the PF (Pilot Flying) that he was clear to turn base leg behind the Cessna. We made our radio call; and turned to base leg. After we made another radio call to final; a young woman flying some sort of low wing canopy glass cockpit airplane turned directly in front of us and cut us off in the pattern. She did make a radio call announcing her turn to base. Approaching her turn to final she then queried 'how many airplanes are in the pattern and where are you'. I am sure she was completely unaware of our position. Out of the turn we were approximately less than a quarter of a mile at her seven o'clock position. Clearly she had no idea that she had just cut us off and was now on a simultaneous approach with another aircraft. The PF did not have the conflicting aircraft in sight until late after turning final. In the interest of safety; I took control of the airplane and elected to fly a parallel to her track. I passed by her left wing approximately 400 feet lateral distance and nearly level with her aircraft. Once I knew we were safely passed her aircraft I executed missed approach. We then came around for another successful landing to a full stop.
Report_2_Narrative: As background; ZZZ was heavily congested with training and weekend aviators. The approach to the downwind leg was reported via UNICOM as 4NM southeast to enter a 45 - downwind entry. We coordinated spacing with another crosswind aircraft to follow us for spacing in the pattern. Our aircraft was operating with LED strobe lights and all landing lights for visibility to other aircraft.Reported downwind; uneventfully. And recognized the aircraft we were to follow and mimicked their pattern to minimize spacing conflicts. Reported our base turn to UNICOM; uneventfully. Our track on base leg was approximately 2 NM from the end of the runway. At no time was our location questioned by the conflicting near miss aircraft.Reported a base-to-final turn; uneventfully and unquestioned. Upon the completion of the turn to final; I focused on vertical alignment to the runway and did not see the conflicting near miss aircraft. The PNF (Pilot Not Flying); in a radio call questioned the conflicting near miss aircraft to determine her position upon hearing the UNICOM base report. After it was determined the conflicting aircraft turned inside of our location; resulting in cutting us off placing our aircraft in a hazardous flight profile; PNF took control of the aircraft in a professionally coordinated manner. At this time; the conflicting near miss aircraft was well within 1/4 NM and somewhat above our altitude; which was approximately 300 ft. AGL. Because of her proximity to us; and her apparently being unaware of our location; the resolution was complex. Keep in mind in her base to final turn (Right Hand pattern) with a low wing aircraft she would have then been unable to locate us visually. In a UNICOM radio call; the conflicting near miss aircraft questioned 'how many aircraft are in the pattern and where are you'; indicating her further lack of awareness to traffic as she was a close in final; an inappropriate time to establish traffic.Suspecting her unpredictable actions; we were prevented from a turn to side step since it would have blocked our ability to monitor her activities. Our inter-cockpit discussion created the point; given our closure rate; that our only recourse; in the interest of safety; was to parallel her path; fly past her which would have given her awareness of our location; then when ahead of the conflicting aircraft; we could safely initiate a missed approach; which we did.We remained in the pattern for another approach and full stop landing; uneventfully.

Report_1_Narrative: After two right 360s we were told to extend downwind for runway X; and look for helicopter traffic (Aircraft Y) three miles west of the field at 700 feet. We called out traffic in sight; and continued. After Aircraft Y didn't respond to two radio calls; we were told to do another 360. Rolling out of the 360 we were told to look for Aircraft Y one mile ahead at 900 feet orbiting. We called traffic in sight; and began a climb to 1;300 feet. It appeared as though Aircraft Y was still climbing as they passed in front from our left to right. After a controller change; Tower called our position to Aircraft Y; they did not respond. Shortly after that call; Aircraft Y made a sharp climbing right turn directly into our flight path. To avoid a collision; I took the controls and made a climbing left turn as sharp as I could. Shortly after I started that turn; Aircraft Y banked sharply to their right to avoid us. I can't put an exact number on the distance between us; but it was close enough that I felt the need to bank sharply towards traffic on final; and Aircraft Y banked hard enough for us to see the bottom of their helicopter. Once a safe distance away; we turned to rejoin downwind; and began a descent back to pattern altitude. After that; Aircraft Y asked tower to keep aircraft away from that area while they followed a pursuit. Hearing other aircraft have difficulty with separation; we departed the pattern to ZZZ1 to avoid a similar situation.It is normal for us to fly over helicopter operations at 500-600 feet while in the pattern at 1;100 feet. It is not normal to fly the pattern with unpredictable traffic at the same altitude.

Prior to top of descent we briefed the NNCEE 1 RNAV Arrival into IAH.  The arrival says to plan on flying the RWY 8R transition.  So we briefed the ILS RWY 8R approach.  Houston Approach Control then told us to fly the RNAV (RNP) Y RWY 9 approach.  The CA was the PM and told the FO he would change the approach in the FMC.  After selecting the new approach the CA told the FO that there was a discontinuity at HOWLN.  By selecting the HOWLN transition two separate HOWLN fixes were created separated by a discontinuity.  The discontinuity appeared at the top of page two in the legs page of the FMC.  So the CA brought up the second HOWLN fix and entered it into the discontinuity boxes and assumed that this action closed the discontinuity.  But he never went back to page one to verify that the discontinuity was closed and he did not ask the FO to verify it as the FO was busy managing the descent.  The MCP displayed LNAV and VNAV were engaged and so did the FMAs.  Approach Control then said at HOWLN you are cleared the RNAV (RNP) Y RWY 9 approach.  So the CA set FAF altitude of 2;000 in the MCP.  The FO went through LAVSFT and at HOWLN (IAF) had me set TDZE of 100.  Then we both said why isn't the airplane turning?  Tower immediately cancelled our approach clearance and vectored us for the ILS RWY 9 approach.  The CA was on a larger scale than the FO and noticed that the magenta line showed both the RF leg turn inbound but also extended beyond HOWLN as well and said I think I did not clean up the discontinuity correctly.  The contributing factors were both pilots did not verify the legs page after the Captain cleaned up the discontinuity and the high workload environment in IMC.  Additionally it was the FO's first trip in over a month.
Report_2_Narrative: I briefed the ILS 8R per listed as the runway for flying the NNCEE1 Arrival.  During our descent when checking in with Houston approach we were given a new runaway RNAV Y runway 9.  The captain put the new runway into the FMS and Verbalized he was cleaning it for the RF.  I was reviewing the approach plate and didn't verify his FMS inputs.  The CA noticed right away that the plane should be starting to turn for the RF; which ATC cancelled our approach and told to fly a heading.

Report_1_Narrative: We were descending via the ZZZZZX arrival with the Autopilot off - on profile and speed. FO decided to hand fly the remainder of the approach and landing and disconnected both the Autopilot and Autothrust near ZZZZZ1 intersection. A minute later I looked away and when I looked again; I noticed the FO was high on profile and I mentioned that we needed to cross ZZZZZ1 level at 6;000 feet. The FO increased the rate of descent and crossed ZZZZZ1 level at 6;000 but then continued to descend to approximately 5;900 feet and around 250 knots - with accelerating trend. I verbalized both altitude and speed; and the FO quickly recovered both. We were soon level at 6;000 feet - 240 knots...and soon to slow to 210 knots by ZZZZZ2. There was very light traffic in the terminal area and ATC made no mention of conflicts of any kind. We then proceeded to land uneventfully on Runway XX. The main contributing factor was the FO being new as well as not having flown much for the last several months. We fully debriefed everything; including what to improve on; once at the gate when duties permitted.

Leaving ZZZ; we turned left on Taxiway XX out of FBO to taxi to [Runway] XXR. When we turned left; ground told us to expect 15 minute delay due to departures on the center runway. We therefore proceeded to wait to do the taxi checklist until we were holding short. Approaching [Runway] XXR; I switched to Tower; and within a matter of seconds we hear Company Traffic is on a 4 mile final; and from there I do not recall what I heard. I do not recall because we were then doing the taxi and before takeoff checklist. I remember going up and hitting all of the lights on; which in my mind I must have thought that we were cleared for takeoff; and not line up and wait. We both verified the runway; did all the checklists; took the runway and took off. We never got corrected by ATC to abort takeoff because we weren't cleared. Everything seemed normal; we then got handed off to Departure; and a few minutes later we got asked to copy down a phone number for a possible pilot deviation. At this point both my partner and I looked at each other confused; not knowing what we did. We arrive in ZZZ1; we call the number; they said if they needed us they would call us; they then called us on the Monday after the incident and told us that we took off without clearance. Apparently; I did read line up and wait correctly; though I do not remember what I said due to the last minute high work load. We were called the morning of Monday [Date] from the supervisor telling us what we did. I then told him our side of it. He told me he had to do a report; I told him we would do the same on our end.This occurred because of task saturation. We went from a very calm and slow environment; to a very fast; high workload and task saturated environment. Part of doing the checklists requires a lot of talking between the flight crew. Having to do two checklists; the end of a briefing; as well as listen to ATC all last minute; we clearly didn't hear/comprehend the full ATC instruction. Which; led to us taking off without a clearance. The fact that neither of us caught it; and still didn't know what happened until we got a call; just shows to me how fast we were working. That is a pure reminder to remember to take things slower to make sure this does not happen again.Both my partner and I immediately started doing a report from the incident; when we found out what we did. We made sure that we timely called the ATC facility on the day of the incident as instructed.This occurred because of task saturation. We were thrown off guard getting a clearance 10 minutes before we were expecting to; and were rushing to complete everything. Rushing is never a good thing. We should have stated that we need a minute to finish some checklists and not agreed to the clearance; and if there was ever a doubt in our mind that we didn't understand a clearance we should have double checked with the Tower.
Report_2_Narrative: We were given taxi instructions from FBO via [Taxiway] XX to Runway XXR and told to expect a 15 minute wait at the end of the runway. I was Pilot Flying and made the decision to hold the taxi checklist until we got to the runway due to the short taxi and long wait for takeoff. Once we arrived at [Runway] XXR we switched to Tower and immediately was given clearance onto the runway. Due to the rush to get through the taxi checklist and before takeoff checklist; remember hearing traffic on four mile final and all lights being turned on; I thought we were cleared for takeoff. Once all checklists were done I preceded to takeoff. I do not recall hearing the lineup and wait instructions as my mind was rushed to complete both checklists. Looking back I clearly should have denied instructions to take the runway and take my time to complete checklists.We were notified after takeoff of a possible deviation and even at that time we did not know what we did. We called ATC after landing in ZZZ1. They didn't tell us what the possible deviation was and that they would call us back if needed. They called morning of [Date].I allowed myself to be rushed and became task saturated at a critical phase of flight by trying to rush through two checklists before takeoff and knowing traffic was inside a four mile final behind us. This prevented me in clearly hearing our instructions from the tower which was line-up and wait; not cleared for takeoff.Completed report after speaking with ATC on the morning of [Date]I allowed myself to be rushed and task saturated; trying to run two check lists after ATC instructions at the runway hold short line. Even though we had a short taxi and an expected 15 minute wait; I should have still ran the taxi checklist on the taxiway enroute to [Runway] XXR. If we had to wait the entire 15 minutes; I could've reviewed the taxi checklist again if needed. At the hold short once we were given instructions by ATC; I should have denied instructions to allow sufficient time to run the taxi checklist.

Report_1_Narrative: We were given descent from 12;000 feet to 4;000 feet. As we approached 9;700 feet; ATC amended the altitude to 7;000 feet for traffic. A minute later they advised us of traffic climbing to 6;500 feet VFR. At this point we were still at about 9;000 feet and descending. The TCAS popped up and an unknown traffic was spotted approaching us; we could not locate it; and it was not the traffic ATC advised us of. Moments before the RA; ATC advised us of unknown traffic approaching us and to turn to a right heading of 310 as we were currently on a west heading of 270. As ATC was talking TCAS yelled 'traffic traffic' as we were in the right turn. The First Officer immediately disengaged autopilot and continued turning right; added max thrust and began climbing. I caught the traffic on TCAS as close as 300 feet and approaching and we were at about 8;500 feet and descending. After we were clear of conflict we were told to descend to 4;000 feet on heading 270 and continue for Visual [Runway] 13. At this point I refused any visual responsibility and requested the ILS. The aircraft was way; way; way too close! Way too close!

We were cleared to descend from FL180 to FL050. We ran the descent checklist and approach checklist as necessary. During descent ATC advised of a VFR target approaching our descent path at our roughly ten to eleven o' clock position. We scanned visually for traffic as well as verified the target on TCAS. ATC then advised us to descend to a higher altitude of FL070 to avoid the traffic. We amended altitude; visually scanned as the target got closer in vertical and horizontal path. As we began to approach the TCAS advised with a 'traffic; traffic' alert. Our altitude to the best of my memory was roughly between 10;000 feet and 9;000 feet. We scanned for traffic appearing to be around the 12 o' clock position of the aircraft. ATC issued a vector turning northeast bound which we complied with; however the TCAS RA went off immediately with a 'climb; climb' resolution. I disconnected the autopilot; put the thrust levers into the maximum position and adjusted the aircraft pitch to the vertical speed resolution. I believe we were about 9;000-8;700 feet before completing the RA TCAS. The separation showed about 300 feet. We climbed until clear of conflict to 9;500 feet and continued the ATC vector. After the 'clear of conflict' we leveled the aircraft; advised ATC of the TCAS RA and returned back to new given ATC clearance. We continued the flight to land safely at ZZZ. This was a huge learning experience to maintain separation and situational awareness of other aircraft and ATC instructions.

We were descending via the DRLLR5 arrival for Runway 26R transition and we had everything briefed and ready to go. At a point ATC changed the runway on us from [Runway] 26R to 26L so we reloaded correct runway and its respective ILS approach procedure to back up the approach. We rebriefed the approach and reloaded all frequencies again so we were ready once again for the approach. At the end of the arrival there's a fix named ZOEEE; right upon crossing that fixed; my First Officer selected HDG sync; immediately follow by HDG mode. And just like that; we were auto sequenced for the approach. ATC then came to us with right turn for a right base followed by another right turn turn intercept the localizer for Runway 26L. At that point the FO (First Officer) armed APP and we confirmed APP1 was armed. So we continue with the approach but the airplane didn't intercept the LOC but instead it continued. Before we reached the final approach course I told my First Officer 'Be careful I don't think it's going to intercept' (he didn't acknowledge) right when we reached the course I told my First Officer 'turn now...turn now!' But he didn't; so at that point I said 'I have the flight controls' and I hand flown the airplane back the final approach course. At that point ATC came to us to let us know we were showing left of course to what my First Officer responded 'We are correcting. This happened like at about 3000 ft. AGL. After I restablished the airplane on it's final approach course I asked my First Officer if he wanted the controls back and he said YES. So I transferred the controls back to him. Then I quickly checked the information and I verified that the FMS was loaded with the ILS26L; we had a good preview with right identifier IJYV and we were showing an inbound course of 267 and APP 1 was armed. So I had to reach the FMS and I activated vectors then I forced V/L on the Pilot Flying side after asking the flying pilot if he agreed with every single step. Then the airplane's automation 'woke up' and the First Officer followed the FD all the way. We were stable at 1000 ft. and we continued to landing. I think the new FMS program has a glitch or something. Apparently it doesn't auto sequence the approach the same way. This happened in another aircraft which has the latest FMS update. From now on I'm not going to trust the auto sequence on the new loads and I am going to manually sequence it from a fix on the final approach fix. This should prevent the same issue from happening again."

I was PF (Pilot Flying) and received an RA about 20 NM SW of ZZZ. Descending from 7;000-5;000 feet; I received a TA and immediately reduced my rate of descent. I received an RA while leveling off at 5;000 [feet] regarding traffic 500 feet below; I complied and cleared the conflict. The aircraft was landed safely without issue.

While turning from base to final on a visual approach to Runway 13; Tower informed us that we had triggered a terrain alert. Around the time of the transmission I noticed we were between on the PAPIs to slightly low on the PAPIs. We acknowledged the transmission and said we were visual with the runway. We proceeded to get established on final and land. On board EGPWS equipment was never activated.Flying a fairly tight base to final turn that necessitated a continuous descent in the final turn.Extending downwind would have allowed a higher altitude for the base to final turn and possibly a more level turn to final that would not have triggered the alert.
Report_2_Narrative: While flying a visual approach on base to Runway 13 at DSM; ATC Tower gave us a low altitude alert; and said to 'check altitude.' We confirmed that we were above visual glide slope in reference to the PAPI; and continued onto final; where we intercepted and maintained ILS glideslope indications to an uneventful landing.Base turn to final approach inside FAF for ILS approach.

Report_1_Narrative: Typical peak afternoon departure.  Estimated 1hr late with 2 MELs; one unfamiliar. Flap system inoperative. About 25 minutes after show time finally got notice and it showed aircraft nearly fully loaded with 1 can to go. Went to aircraft and still pushed late.  After engine start got ECAM flaps extended.  Since flaps should have been fixed in the closed position I believed it was a problem.  Maintenance believed it was an expected spurious ECAM due to the MEL.  It took over 40 minutes to taxi to maintenance area; make calls to duty officer; and attempt calls to 2 tech pilots to finally have Maintenance on ground visually confirm that the notch flap on the left wing was not retracted and the ECAM warning was real.  Returned to gate; tail swapped and blocked out in just under 2 hours.  Nice job by everyone involved.  Flight to ZZZ was uneventful until approach control informed us the Tower was closed due to COVID.  We flew a box pattern while simultaneously looking at ZZZ1 as alternate (we could get there with 10;000 pounds of fuel due to Captain added fuel) and FO working ACARS to get non-Tower approval.  We briefed the details and flew an instrument approach in VFR conditions to an uneventful landing and block in at destination airport.  After block in I studied the events and our crew response and believed we did an excellent job under the conditions but noted the newest FOM revision required an alternate to operate to a non-towered airport; which we did not have.  The following morning; further study showed that the normally 24 hour Tower may have been closed due to COVID; but there was indeed a NOTAM for the closure which both I and the Dispatcher missed.The confusing MEL language refers to 'appropriate AFM performance restrictions are applied' led to longer than necessary hunt for those in the AFM when the only restrictions I could find are in the QRH.  The time I spent trying to make sure I didn't miss any restrictions could have been better spent studying the NOTAMs.  The closure NOTAM was found using the iPad app. which sorted the airport closure first; while company's provided NOTAMs sorted the closure near the end next to the airport inop lighting.  The requirement for the alternate seems to be a change in this week's FOM.The missing NOTAM closure is high workload human error sorting through pages of NOTAMs.  Lowering the workload by changing the MEL to point to the restrictions rather than creating a lengthy search would have helped. Company NOTAMs should be sorted more like the iPad app.
Report_2_Narrative: Flight was scheduled out and conducted a return to gate. An aircraft swap ensued and then departed. I took the flight over from Dispatcher #1 at shift change. I am unsure if the crew was given any NON-TOWER OPS info before departure. The following NOTAM came into play upon arrival: Air Traffic Control and Air Traffic Control Procedures------------------------------------------------------ZZZ APTZZZ SVC TWR CLOSED CTC CTAF XXY.Z DLYZZZ APTZZZ SVC TWR CLOSED CLASS D SERVICE NOT AVBL CTC ZZZ3ARTCC .4 PRACTICE INSTR APCH NOT AVBL DLYDid not know if the crew or the releasing Dispatcher #1 saw the NOTAM for the Tower closure due to the aircraft swap and delay that occurred.  Which delayed the flight where the NOTAM became an issue.Double check NOTAM closures when flights are delayed for an extended period of time.Checked NOTAMs to verify Tower was closed. ACARSed the crew a non-tower operations worksheet and informed the crew to contact ZZZ Center for arrival procedures.

Report_1_Narrative: While descending on the DRLLR5 into IAH; I asked the Captain a question regarding the flight and we began to converse about the topic. I continued to monitor the descent and we were on speed and on altitude all the way through MPORT. The Captain had the bottom altitude of MPORT (16;000 ft.) in the altitude selector and once past MPORT it was not changed to the bottom altitude of DRLLR (13;000 ft.). Since the altitude selector was still at 16;000 ft.; it looked as if we would be fine with the restriction at DRLLR according to the vertical guidance. 5 miles from DRLLR I noticed we were actually high and told the Captain we needed to increase the vertical descent. He increased the vertical descent while maintaining the speed restriction but we passed DRLLR 400-500 feet above the restriction. The flight continued normally and we continued on the arrival into the visual on 26R. This event was caused by a discussion about the flight leading to a monitoring breakdown. As Pilot Monitoring; I lost focus of the descent path and did not realize the bottom altitude of DRLLR was not set. By the time I realized and queried the Captain to increase the descent rate it was too late. I should have waited to ask a question until we were on the ground considering we were already on the arrival. This would have allowed me to monitor the descent rate more accurately and avoid being high at DRLLR. I also probably would have caught the altitude still being set to MPORT.

There was a family in exit row. They were not compliant with mask policy. We asked for a Supervisor to come on board to talk with them because it is an X hour flight.  We were close to push when the Sup came on.  I forgot to brief the exit row.I realized it when we were going up in the air.  The Captain dinged us at 10;000 [feet].  I made sure it was safe and A made her announcement.  I got up and briefed the exit row.

Due to the COVID pandemic; I have not been able to fly as much but had ample time in full motion jet simulators within the preceding month. That day I was working at the airport when I was contacted to help move planes around in the area for a business because their main pilot did not show up. I was told I was to be flown to an airport and to pick up a plane from a local airport and to fly to another airport. I got to the airplane and was able to fly the airplane to ZZZ. Upon arrival at the airport; the person I was following then asked for a ride back to the origin airport on the plane I was in. I accepted and flew without incident to the airport and made a safe landing. Upon landing; I realized that I had not been passenger current for single-engine aircraft. I would not say there was a lack of proficiency; just a lack of currency. I have been in and out of simulators for jets and had no worries that I could not fly and land the aircraft safely. I was just not able to fly as often as I'd like. I think some of the main causes of this was the fact that this was sprung on me with little warning and no clear plan and I had not flown with this group of people before. It was a major complacency incident on my part and lack of proper flight planning.

After takeoff [I] started after takeoff checklist. ATC instructed me to contact North Tower. I changed frequency before completing after takeoff checklist. During that time TCAS voice message [announced] 'traffic.' I got confused because TCAS is inoperative and placarded. I started right turn 2;150 feet MSL. ATC called me and gave me instruction for upwind. At that time; I was at 2;400 feet MSL; saw the aircraft left and below me. I told ATC 'aircraft in sight; climbing to avoid traffic.' ATC gave instructions to enter right downwind which I acknowledged. To avoid traffic I had climbed to 2;750 feet MSL then descended to TPA and landed safely.

I was conducting my first training flight after completing training as of Date. The plan was to fly to the southeast/southwest training area of ZZZ1 bravo airspace and show effects of controls. The flight went extraordinarily well and got through the lesson. I received information D winds were 180 @ 8 altimeter was 30.08'' hg. Originally I was flying from the west and advised to set up for Left downwind for XXL. Once coming into 3-4 nm; I was then advised by tower to switch to right pattern for XXR. I set up and we looked and felt good; I was flying my usually pattern I would do in a rental I do out of ZZZ. I set up for my standard 45 degree angle; and believed I was setting up for XXR; but all I could see was XX over my shoulder. I then continued to reduce power and begin my descent. I started my turn and per training they require a 20 [degree] angle of bank and no more. I did go thirty to make sure I would not overshoot. Unfortunately to my knowledge I checked runway XX but not left or right. Upon touch down I was advised to pull off on taxiway A; which is when I noticed the runway XXR and was even advise to hold short of XXR. I then took blame and was expecting a phone number. When I landed; I shook it off and knew I had to continue my teaching with my students and advised them of my mistake.

We landed on Runway 4 and due to the snow removal operations that were occurring at the time as the front had just cleared the city; with numerous taxiways closed or impassable; we were instructed to exit onto Runway 13/31 and taxi to hold short of Lima. We were then cleared onto Lima; Alpha to the gate; but it turned out that there was on outbound Company coming out of the alley. We then were told to hold short of Bravo on Lima. We did and then I heard Ground give the outbound Company aircraft instructions to taxi Lima; Bravo.  I said to my FO (First Officer) that I didn't think we had room for him to clear us. As he came past Spot X I was concerned. As he started onto Lima I said 'I think this is too tight.' As he started his turn onto Lima I knew it was unsafe and I instructed him three times on Ground frequency to stop. He did. We were staring at his right winglet very close to us. I then coordinated with Company and Ground to get a tug out to us. Because we were inbound; I had the Ground Crew push us back towards Runway 13/31 on Lima to allow [the Company aircraft] to continue his taxi. We then continued to the gate. I have since talked to and debriefed the other Captain to get his view of what occurred and to talk about the safety concerns that I had. No metal was bent; but it would have if we hadn't both realized his close proximity to our aircraft. There were parallax concerns from Ground's view and the other crew's view. Speaking up is critical when things don't look right. The substantial numbers of snow removal trucks and plows along with closed taxiways that were constantly changing made it even more challenging.

Before push checklist and Ramp tower clearance to push were completed / obtained per procedures. Following standard pushback verbiage from the ground crew; I released the brakes. Pushback and No 2 engine start proceeded without incident. Ground crew instructed to 'Set Brakes' - at this time I read back 'Parking brake set; pressure normal' while the FO (First Officer) and I visually checked the red parking brake indicator for illumination and Brake Press gauge for 2800 PSI. Ground crew then announced 'Aircraft disconnected by-pass pin removed' to which I replied 'Disconnect headset.'Shortly after this statement the aircraft appeared to move forward unexpectedly. The ramp agent came back on headset with 'Set brakes set brakes' to which I applied brakes.Apparently the parking brake was not set; as we had intended and thought; and an uncommanded aircraft movement had occurred. The ramp agent came on the interphone and indicated that all was fine on the ground; and standard signals were given for ramp departure. Upon crew debrief; one of the threats that we identified was the sun shining into the cockpit; making the red parking brake light less apparent to the crew. Another threat was the general distraction of normalcy and not blocking out the sunlight's glare to verify aircraft condition.We are glad no one on the ground was injured and will learn from this experience.
Report_2_Narrative: Before push checklist was completed. Received our push back clearance and the Captain communicated with the ground crew standard tow bar less pushback clearance and released the brakes appropriately. Pushback was completed uneventfully and during so we were cleared for engine start after which the Captain had me start the No. 2 engine. The ground crew instructed to 'Set Brakes.' I saw good brake pressure - 2800 psi; and what I believed to be the red Parking Brake light illuminated so I continued my duties of setting the packs appropriately.The ground crew announced 'aircraft disconnected - bypass pin removed' and I heard the Captain respond with 'Disconnect Headset.' Shortly after this the aircraft began an uncommanded movement forward; ground crew came on headset Immediately saying 'Set Brakes Set Brakes' to which brakes were applied and the aircraft stopped the slow roll and the Parking Brake was appropriately verified engaged at this time. The ramp crew came back on the inter phone and indicated all was fine on the ground; and standard signals were given for departure. Apparently our parking brake was not set after all when we initially thought; and upon debrief we both agreed the threats encountered were the glare of the sun on the parking brake light illuminating it more red than usual; as well as the general distraction associated with starting engines and push back tasks in general. We did not properly block the sun's glare and we should have been more vigilant to ensure it was set properly. We are both thankful nobody on the ground was injured and we will learn from this experience in order to prevent it from happening again.

Report_1_Narrative: Aircraft was out of balance at start of my shift. It required the last row to be blocked. I sent a message to ZZZZ to do so and they did. At about 10 minutes before departure aircraft went out of balance again and I determined that the last 3 rows now needed to be blocked. ZZZZ called to find out why weights weren't generating and I advised that last 3 rows now needed to be blocked. He advised that aircraft was already out so I said I would handle it with Captain. I sent Captain that info via ACARS to block last 3 rows; but when I went back to double check the Load Program (LP) to verify that in fact the additional 2 rows needed to be moved and blocked; this time LP indicated that no rows needed to be blocked. I checked it 1 or 2 more times because it didn't seem right to me. LP again indicated no rows now needed to be blocked. So now I quickly sent Captain an ACARS to disregard moving the additional 2 rows of people as that would now be an unnecessary waste of time since no longer needed. I sent the disregard message and then released weights. Departure [load] control now did not indicate to block any rows-although row XX had been blocked from the beginning. As I thought about why LP would show 3 rows needed to be blocked; and then it indicated that we now didn't need to LP any rows; I checked the seat map (aircraft was airborne by then) and sure enough; Operations had gone in and shown last 3 rows blocked but never spoke with the crew. They should not have touched the seat map since they did not physically move anyone. And since they are not supposed to do that; it didn't occur to me that they had. But that is why LP did not show I needed to block rows; it was reading that the rows were already empty. I called Dispatch and asked them to find out from crew exactly which rows were blocked on take-off. The answer was just row 34-the last row. I verified with ZZZ; and they advised that they had not told crew to move anyone; they just showed passengers moved out of last 3 rows. I advised ZZZ never to indicate passengers moved seats unless they have physically accomplished the move. They understand now.

I was working Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZZ1 at gate and had planned the flight according to the [Manifest]. One of my agents connected a cart of freight going to ZZZ2 to a string of carts that was going to ZZZZ1 unbeknownst to me. Since all of the carts were together; I scanned all of the carts for the flight going to ZZZZ1. The last cart of freight was a pallet going to ZZZ2. At first I had no idea that it was going to ZZZ2 so I scanned it just like I scanned the other cart before it since it was DG for ZZZZ1. When I scanned the cart for ZZZ2 the barcode wouldn't scan so I had to enter it manually. I did and confirmed the weight. I did not get an error message stating this was freight for ZZZ2 the scanner allowed me to add it to my flight for ZZZZ1 and I continued on my work.  I was moving from AFT to FWD of the aircraft to ensure the operation runs smoothly on my gate. I am the Lead. I instructed my agent in the back to load up a cart of bags and a cart of DG (freight) and to surround the DG with 15 bags. I went to the front of the plane to check on the crew working there. When I returned to the rear of the plane all three carts were loaded including the freight for ZZZ2. When it became time to close the flight on the scanner the scanner asked me to confirm if everything was loaded and again no error message was displayed and since everything was in fact loaded; I confirmed that everything was loaded. Still nothing on the scanner told me that I had freight for another destination; in this case ZZZ2 going to ZZZZ1. I didn't realize I had loaded the ZZZ2 freight to ZZZZ1 until the plane was already gone and I was already working the next flight on my gate and the cargo coordinator questioned me about the freight that it was missing from the gate. I notified my supervisor and spoke with safety to file a Report and the Cargo Dispatcher regarding the incident.
