The cargo shipment 016-62127472 for Aircraft X was found with one box out of ninety-nine (slac count) containing Lithium Ion Battery with UN3481 sticker attached. The shipment was not declared on the MAWB (Master Airway Bill) as per PI-967 with the language 'Lithium ion batteries in compliance with section II of PI 967' in the 'Nature and Quantity of Goods' section of the MAWB (Master Airway Bill). As per the packing list attached to the outside of the package; this shipment is a wireless Bluetooth headset from; model RLN6490B. I tried looking for the owner manual to get the battery type and voltage; but could not fine it on line.The entire shipment has been placed on hold until this is resolved.

Descending on the DSNEE 4 arrival from EMLLD; we became aware of congested airport environment with two different TCAS traffic alerts very close by. Approach had us at 190 knots departing DSNEE; and we were expecting the visual to runway 20R. We had gone to flaps 2 configuration. We were handed off late to the final controller; and when we switched over we heard a company call sign with nearly identical numeric as ours; cleared for the RNAV (RNP) Z runway 20R. We heard the other aircraft read back the clearance; we knew a VERY similar call sign was on frequency. We thought we might be cleared for the same approach. We checked in and asked if we were going to get the RNAV approach; and the controller said no; we were going to get a visual unless we asked for the approach. Shortly after; the controller cleared an aircraft to descend to 3000'; but neither of us were sure that it was our call sign. The First Officer (FO) attempted to clarify; but the controller was very busy and talking nonstop. We flew a few more miles; and before we could clarify he repeated the clearance to descend to 3000' and asked if we had the airport in sight. We called the airport; and said we were high. He cleared us to fly through final approach course and intercept from the right. FO read back the clearance; and we both agreed it was going to be tight. I immediately called for gear down and got the flaps to full; I also used the speed brakes as long as safely practical. We switched over to tower control; and I made the glideslope at approx. 1400' AGL; but we were still 40kts fast. I told the FO we were going to do a 'Soft Go'. At 1000' AGL I called for 'Go Around; Takeoff/around (TOGA)' and advanced the thrust levers to TOGA. I then called 'climb' as I brought the thrust levers back to what I thought was the climb position; but was actually the Maximum Continuous Thrust (MCT) detent. The tower initially held us to 2000'; so I reduced our climb rate - we were accelerating rapidly. As we cleaned up and were cleared to 3000'; the FO recognized that we were in MCT detent and brought it to my attention. By then we were clean and rapidly approaching 250 KIAS. I brought the thrust levers back to idle as we passed 260; and we saw a maximum speed of about 280 before we began to slow down again. I selected speed mode and set 220 KIAS; positioned the thrust levers in the climb detent and re-engaged auto thrust. We slowed and turned back into a right downwind for an uneventful visual approach and landing at SNA.Causes(1)	Late clearance to descend. SNA is challenging even when on a normal profile; and a few miles made all the difference.(2)	Task saturation. The L.A basin is one of the busiest airspaces in the world. Two Traffic Alerts and nonstop chatter from the approach controller indicated that he was task saturated; as well.(3)	Call sign confusion. The scheduling shop should never have had two call signs separated by one digit arriving at the same airport at the same time.(4)	Approach expectations. We were going to do a visual; and changed our setup and expectations when we heard preceding traffic approach clearance.(5)	Fatigue. Our FAR 117 duty day limit was 10 hours. We had been awake since 3.5 hours earlier to make sign-in; and were at 9:00 of our 10:00 limit when we arrived at SNA. When we knew we were too high to likely make a successful approach; we could have asked for a vector and more time/distance to descend. Flight scheduling needs to de-conflict call signs so very similar ones are not scheduled to arrive at the same airport at the same time. The ATC controllers seemed very busy; perhaps a few more controllers on-shift would help divide the workload better. Crew scheduling could alleviate fatigue factors by avoiding sequence builds that have crews flying into airports at the back end of their duty periods that are known to present unusual challenges (even if they aren't officially 'special qualification' airports).

Arrived at destination and load crew advised us that DG can door was not secured to ULD (Unit Load Devise); can was resting on main cargo deck door. Load crew noted that all DG was still secure to floor of ULD (Unit Load Device) and intact. The cause was DG agent in ZZZ failed to secure door to DG ULD (Unit Load Device) after presentation of DG to flight crew. The reporter recommends load crew not leave cargo area until DG door is visually confirmed closed. DG Inspection checklist listed in FOM does not require flight crew to verify DG door reattached after presentation although common sense should be applied. Positioning of this particular DG container would not have allowed 'double checking' of secured door through smoke barrier windows. Reliance on other parties to perform job correctly is inherent in our operation. This was just a mistake by the ZZZ DG agent.

During the baggage loading phase I loaded a small box containing approximately 6 small bottles of recreational Oxygen. We departed ZZZ with a final destination of ZZZ1 and the flight proceeded uneventfully. Upon arrival all baggage was unloaded and the First Officer questioned me after the passengers left regarding the contents of the box. At no time up to this point did I consider the possibility of this being HAZMAT or anything not authorized to be carried by us. Upon further research I felt compelled to reach out to the Chief Pilot for guidance. I suggest a more thorough training of company policies; procedures; and specifically HAZMAT information. Access to more detailed information regarding Hazmat material.
Report_2_Narrative: The owner and family arrived to the airplane ramp side. I went to open the exterior baggage compartment; to load the luggage. While loading bags the line personal were bringing me; I saw the Captain was also loading bags/backpacks etc. inside the cabin of the airplane. After the the baggage was loaded in the exterior baggage compartment of the airplane; I walked into the airplane greeted the owner and family and sat down in my flight deck seat to preform the rest of the pre-takeoff duties for the flight to intermediate stop to pick up two passengers and then finally on to final ZZZ1; our final destination. All flights were uneventful. When we landed at ZZZ1 while unloading the baggage; I noticed the box contained portable Oxygen containers which the Captain had loaded at our initial departure airport. After the passengers were on their way; I mentioned to the Captain about the box and it's contents. I said I don't think that was something we could carry; as it may be considered Hazmat. We further discussed this and performed research of company FOM and HAZMAT. The Chief Pilot was contacted to get clarification and guidance on this issue. I suggest more specific HAZMAT guidance and possibly more comprehensive HAZMAT training. Also a more detailed description of carry and non carry items would be helpful.

Report_1_Narrative: On Aircraft X from ZZZ to ZZZ1 while boarding passenger seated in seat XX4 came on that aircraft with his mask below his nose and between his lips. I told that passenger his mask must come over his nose and mouth. He responded 'He will try.' I then offered him a company approved surgical mask that were provided by the company. He responded 'No thanks.' That was the first time of me asking him to put his mask on properly. During my compliant check before doing the safety demo; the mask was down again. I told him once again about the mask. That was the second time. During cabin service the mask was down again and I had to remind him once more. Once cabin service was over and passengers were given time to eat and drink; I then went around to collect trash and he was asleep with his mask down. I called out to him and he opened his eyes and I told him for the third time about his mask. Once we got to the final descent his mask was down and I reminded him once again for the fourth time. Once I told him this time he just looked at me and shook his head. This passenger and his wife; were both sick. They were both coughing without covering their cough. The couple kept coughing during takeoff; throughout the flight and landing. The coughing drew the attention of other passengers to look. People are very scared and when they hear coughing and other passengers not wearing mask properly they worry.  Many verbal warnings were given.

Employee was seen multiple times on gates X and Y around other employees not wearing a mask; violating the company's mask policy. On multiple occasions; he was well within 6 feet in distance from other employees talking to them. Management has briefed everyone of said company policy. Employee continues to violate said mask policy. Multiple employees have tested positive for COVID; and he has previously had to quarantine for being in contact with those employees; yet continues to violate said policy.

A vibration / rumble was noticed during the No.2 engine start.   During taxi the Captain performed his flight controls check so fast that I was unable to keep up with the call outs. It had been this way the whole trip; in fact I had insisted that it be repeated on the 1st leg so I could correctly check the control position.  Whilst holding short of Runway XXL the Captain indicated he would turn the anti-ice off on taking the runway. I pointed out that the published ATIS reported runway conditions of 3;3;3 with 50% compacted snow; 50% compacted snow; 75% compacted snow. He was still inclined to turn off the anti-ice so I tried again suggesting should anything happen any investigation would focus on this. Reluctantly the anti-ice remained on and having lined up it was obvious that the runway was still significantly impacted by snow.  On departure I chose to call for autopilot 2 at approx 100 ft in case the controller was busy. This allowed me to notice during the initial turn towards / at ZZZZZ that on the E / WD the title Engine" was flashing as well as "N1 VIB" was flashing green 9.9. An increase in noise and vibration was perceived from the right engine. Anti-ice was reapplied and after a short while the indication reduced to 8.2. The indication returned to 9.9 after the anti-ice was turned off during climb and it remained this way until the engines were at idle during the descent. During cruise I suggested the possibility of a bad sensor; the Captain's opinion was that we had a vibration. Later during the leg I commented on the vibration and now the Captain's opinion was that we may have a bad sensor. We were initially cleared to FL360. As we approached level off the Captain requested a climb to FL380 earlier than the flight plan fix of ZZZZZ1. On reaching cruise I reduced then increased managed Mach by 0.01 to investigate the potential of a harmonic vibration. Once established at the new speeds there was no difference in the indicated vibration so I returned to the managed speed with the intent to divert should the situation deteriorate. The Captain took out his QRH and referenced the High Engine Vibration procedure. It was given to read and after a discussion the Captain elected not to follow it as it would require descending from cruise altitude. For the remainder of cruise the Captain returned to reading his magazine. I monitored the indications; changed assigned radio frequencies and from time to time closed my eyes to concentrate on feeling whether the vibration had changed or the ambient sound had changed. We began flying the arrival. During the descent; Approach instructed us to increase speed to 310 kts or better. I felt that this was a bad idea and expressed this by saying ' I don't want to do that ' and 'I am uncomfortable with that'.  'So what it's only 20 kts' was the reply and seeing no intent to inform Approach of our situation; I felt that I had pushed as far as I could and increased speed. The Captain gleefully declared that the vibration had dropped to 2.2. I countered stating that the engines were now at idle as the aircraft attempted to stay on the profile at a higher speed. It was at this point that I decided to reach out  and have myself removed from the trip. ZZZ1 was landing north with reported winds of 160/6 so a landing approach calculation was performed for the tail wind landing. On short final the Captain elected to increase Vapp unilaterally leading to a long float during landing. A new landing approach calculation for the new numbers was not performed. On landing I briefly deployed reverse thrust and quickly closed down the right engine before it could spool up and exacerbate the vibration problem. I moderated the thrust from the left engine and relied mainly on wheel braking to slow the aircraft. Control was passed back to the Captain at approx 60 kts for the taxi to the gate."

While working Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZ1; I became concerned for the health and well-being of my flying partner.  She displayed numerous symptoms of severe illness including: persistent coughing; sneezing; shortness of breath; weakness; and sweating profusely.  Prior to starting the service; I witnessed [flying partner] remove her tote bag from the galley cart and take several pills.  [Flying partner] then had difficulty pushing and pulling the beverage cart with assistance.  It strained her and seemed very difficult for her to speak with customers; so I had prepared a sign in blue marker that read; 'What do you want to drink?' and listed beverages.  After picking up trash; [flying partner] repeatedly placed her used gloves on top of the galley counter.  Upon descent into ZZZ1; I noticed the orange strap at door 2R was not attached and reminded her to affix it.  At that time; she was coughing and was no longer wearing a mask at all.  I expressed genuine concern for her well-being and was uncomfortable flying with someone who appeared so unwell.

Today I taxied to the ZZZ strip with ATIS weather to ZZZ Runway XX. I noticed 4 or 3 maybe snow removal vehicles working around but not yet on the runway. I called in to the Tower and I introduced my airplane numbers and request for takeoff. The Tower asked me to repeat my call sign and I did.  The Tower told me to wait. I heard quite a bit (of half) the Tower radio traffic communication.  The Tower then told me I was cleared for takeoff and I confirmed.  I noticed that the snow removal vehicles all had backed off and so I took off successfully; without incident.  One of the snow removal drivers called into the Tower and told them I took off the strip.  I verified this and was told to call into the Tower later.  I called in and told the Tower how it happen from my prospective after my flight. They say they are passing the incident on. There was a southeast wind and in reflection; the Tower may have told me I was clear to take off southeast; which is from the lake; not the strip.  I should have reiterated my position on the runway when he asked me to repeat my tail numbers.  I do not believe I called for a southeast take off; but there was a southeast wind that I was in my mind.  I was also watching the snow removal equipment but believed he must have told them to back off on a different frequency. There was no other radio traffic until I was 8 to 10 miles away. The lake was empty and the Southeast water lane; frozen; is right in front of the Tower. He could have helped the situation if he said he could not see me. He did not see me take off at all. It was the snow worker that saw me. When I returned about an hour later the ATIS changed to MIKE and the strip was closed.  I had no issue landing on the lake to the southeast. I have flown out of ZZZ since I was a training pilot. I use the lake in the summer and occasionally in the winter. I regret this happened today; regardless that all was safe and there was no chance of any collision with truck or another plane.  It was my intention to follow ATC control; I am sorry if I heard/misinterpreted; or even possibly asked for the incorrect runway. I will certainly talk more to the Tower when they are short and listen more acutely.  I will be more certain of the runway as well and add it to my checklist as I am with wheels and skis. I have caught miscommunication from the Tower in the past coming in on floats but was able to talk it out.

The incident occurred while on the initial climb from runway 25 at Orlando Executive Airport (ORL). The probable cause appears to be communication and pilot error. Our aircraft was a Aircraft X. The student and I were remaining in the pattern practicing touch and go landings. An Aircraft Y [faster aircraft] was following us throughout the pattern. Our call sign was occasionally [mis-stated] which contributed to the confusion. During our pattern practice we were told to extend our downwind and that Tower would call our base. While we were in the right downwind and extended right downwind for Runway 25; Aircraft Y was noticeably gaining on us as shown on our MFD's traffic feature. Our base was approved and we were number 1 cleared for a touch and go Runway 25. [Aircraft Y] was cleared for a touched and go number 2 behind us. While on short final; it was noticeable once again on the MFD that Aircraft Y was gaining on us significantly. Our approach speed in the Aircraft X is 65 kts. With the headwind component that day our ground speed was undoubtedly slower than 65kts. Aircraft Y was told by Tower to do an s-turn on final for spacing as it was apparent they were getting close. The problem arose on the initial climb out following the touch and go; Aircraft Y overtook our aircraft while on the departure leg at approximately 800 feet; resulting in a near miss.The traffic we were following was told to extend upwind and we were expecting to receive a similar instruction for ourselves. The confusion seems to have begun when Executive Tower radioed for Aircraft Y to continue in the upwind and that they would call their crosswind. We did not respond to this call; as we assumed it was intended for Aircraft Y.  Aircraft Y responded confirming to extend their upwind until their crosswind was called. This transmission must have stepped on the Tower's transmission because as soon as Aircraft Y was finished broadcasting; the end of tower's transmission came through with the only audible portion being '...end upwind.' (Most likely 'extend upwind.')  This stepped-on call was most likely a follow up to [us]; but Aircraft Y then responded to continue their upwind for a 2nd time. Tower then called for Aircraft Y to extend their upwind. At this point the instructor in Aircraft Y; clearly confused; read back the extend upwind instruction for the 3rd time. We realized that the extend upwind call to [callsign] was most likely for us and we continued the upwind as instructed with the traffic ahead of and behind us being on extended upwinds.During this time; we again noticed Aircraft Y gaining on us on the MFD traffic. We continued in our upwind climb as Tower called for Aircraft Y to follow traffic ahead to the right (us). They responded that they had us in sight as they continued climbing and closing in on our left hand side. This is the approximate time that [we] was overtaken by Aircraft Y at approximately 800 feet MSL. Aircraft Y passed by us at roughly 200 feet off of our left wing; while both of us were still climbing to pattern altitude in the upwind leg.

I was approaching F70 from the southeast after a personal flight while making radio calls every few miles as I noticed on my ADS-B in several targets circling around the airport due to intense student activity.Someone made a radio call a few minutes before I was in the vicinity of another aircraft cutting them off on downwind too and I prepared myself for heavy traffic in the pattern and trying to find an opening so I could join the downwind.Upon entering the 45 for downwind I noticed visually an aircraft on the upwind taking off and repeatedly asked his intentions and if he could see me.There was no answer and seeing him proceed on runway heading I continued on the 45 for downwind being confident he wouldn't start a turn to crosswind until he reached close to pattern altitude as is recommended for the noise abatement procedure out of F70 for departures from Runway 18.Then I saw him maneuver for the crosswind maybe 500 feet below my altitude which was at pattern and make a radio call in that regards. At this point I had contacted him several times regarding his intentions and whether he could see me but without any answer.Seeing him turn and put me on the direct collision course with me although 300 ft. below me I decided to execute a 360 degree turn saw him coming extremely near me. Finally he acknowledged my presence in a subsequent radio call.I continued the full turn and reentered downwind.F70 was extremely busy that day with several schools on the field and at one point I saw no less than 5 aircraft in the traffic pattern. Needless to say a Tower is badly needed in that field for the safety of flight as the indiscriminate addition of several aircraft from several schools without any sort of coordination between those schools makes this urgently needed.A bit of courtesy goes a long way too and someone was nice enough to extend their downwind for me earlier in the day so I could depart as I had to hold short of the active runway at least 10 minutes as one aircraft after another was landing with no end in sight.On my side I should have waited outside the pattern on a hold in a safe area until there was no aircraft on upwind and the downwind leg would be fully opened; with adequate radio communications ensuring everyone in the pattern has knowledge of any other traffic with acknowledgement for all.This was my first experience with this field with that many aircraft in the pattern and the lesson learned here is to stay outside an uncontrolled field until there is fewer than a set limit of aircraft that one can set as personal limit.Such a personal limit is not taught at all as part of any training curriculum nor in the AIM in my knowledge.Most uncontrolled field are uncontrolled because of very low traffic but some reach levels just below the threshold needed for a tower and F70 seems to be in that category.Also the schools on the field could coordinate some kind of schedule among themselves so not everyone is up in the air at the same time. Poisson's law dictates things happen in bursts unless there is coordination to avert it.

We were flying east at 3;500 VFR and Aircraft X was 3;500 Westbound. We noticed the Aircraft X at very close range and made a turn to the right to avoid the aircraft. We had ADS-B and did not see the traffic and believe there is no coverage in this area. No ATC services were requested at the time.

Post flight the aircraft was parked at the unmanned self-service fuel station to be refueled before returning it to the hanger. After the aircraft was refueled the parking brake was checked; the throttle was set; and the aircraft was hand prop started; this aircraft has no electrical system. The throttle setting was too high; over powered the brakes; and the aircraft began to roll quickly. An attempt was made to redirect the aircraft away from the building (FBO) and in doing so was looped back at the fuel station where it struck steel/concrete poles; and the fuel pay kiosk. The prop and engine directly struck one pole while the leading edge of the right wing tip stuck the other.I believe human factors were the cause. An additional check of the throttle setting; additional wheel chocking; or anchor the tail wheel would have likely prevented this.There were no injuries; also there were no passengers or crew aboard.

I departed Aircraft X for takeoff. Once approved separation was anticipated I cleared Aircraft Y for takeoff. Once airborne I noticed the departures in a closer proximity than anticipated but I was providing visual separation. I verified that I had appropriate separation on the radar display. In my judgment speed and spacing was adequate and I switched both aircraft [to] departure. Not long after I was called by the Departure Controller that they needed more space between departures because I had less than three miles between Aircraft X and Aircraft Y. This is not typical of my best practices to ensure separation. A possible factor could be aircraft performance. I wasn't anticipating the trailing aircraft to be so much faster or the lead aircraft to under perform like it did. Both aircraft were similar types. Another factor could be that I had been medically decertified due to a COVID-19 exposure at the facility. I was not sick but I was only notified the day before this incident that my medical had been reinstated and that I was to report to work the next day. [It] was quite a surprise to me and didn't give me any time to mentally prepare to conduct air traffic duties after [several] weeks away from work.The only recommendation would be a more advanced notification of a return to air traffic duties after a long time off. It allows controllers to be more mentally prepared to preform safety related duties.

Initially coming in on the IAH LINKK1 Arrival expecting 27 but then just before descent; we were switched to NNCEE arrival expecting 8R (parking north side). Once with 2nd approach controller we were given change to RNAV 9. We asked if they could please work out 8R; a few minutes later cleared RNAV 9. Started setting it up; reprogrammed the box and reconfigured the aircraft. We then received 'maintain 200 until advised; level 6;000MSL at HOWLN'. Came out of box to open MCP speed window for 200 (FMC VNAV speed would be 210 per the arrival). Looked back down and saw us going through TEXXN where the ARC was supposed to commence.I immediately clicked off autopilot and started 30 degree turn to get back on ARC. Halfway through the turn (past HHOOG) received call from ATC stating he saw that we went past TEXXN and to turn left to 300 but we were already 45 off from runway (base). ATC asked if we had runway in sight; we confirmed and were then cleared visual Runway 9. Visual approach was executed and landing was normal. We programmed FMS to perform RNAV 9 from HOWLN but don't think it closed the loop automatically like it does on LINKK1 to 27 approach and that's why the aircraft continued past ARC point.

Initial setup and brief was LINKK arrival; with visual ILS 27. Weather was clear; with excellent visibility. At TOD IAH changed to East flow; and we were given the NNCEE arrival. Approach control said expect RNP Y 9. We requested 8R since our parking gate was on the north side. Were told 'standby; we'll see'. Frequency change; and no reference to 8R request. Then cleared RNAVP Y 9 via HOWLN; maintain 200 knots. I observed Pilot Flying choose RNAV Y 9; with HOWLN transition. Other radio calls and congestion and nonstandard speed change prevented from looking into the LEGS page to see it tie into the HOWLN we were proceeding toward. Immediately after TEXXN we realized it did not turn and turned the aircraft with heading select.Approach queried about being outside the turn as our actual heading turned through approximately 330. Approach control said maintain current heading'. I immediately asked what heading do you want; we are currently in the turn. First response was 260 heading. We initiated a turn reversal; then approach said; I see you are in the turn; turn right 060; descend 3000; cleared the visual runway 9. I acknowledged. Soon thereafter were given 'maintain 6000; turn heading 100.' Responded with climbing back to 6000. Controller asked if we were still visual with 9. (It was perfectly clear; with unusual 30+ mile visibility). Responded yes; still visual. Re-cleared the visual runway 9. Flew the visual to an uneventful landing."

On the Visual [Runway] XXR approach into ZZZ. Cleared for the approach about 5 miles out; and given a heading 090 to join the localizer. We joined the localizer and got a TA and noticed another RJ (Regional Jet) headed around 150 degrees to join the localizer for [Runway] XXL; which wasn't going to be a factor. We were never left of the [Runway] XXR localizer at any point. We caught the glide slope and headed down while configuring. At this point we received a RA that the [Runway] XXL RJ had blown through the localizer and was right over the top of us; about 300 feet of vertical separation. Captain was flying; he disconnected the AP (Autopilot) and flew the breakout procedure. I told ZZZ Tower we had a RA and he was swamped today so he initially wanted us to go around; to which I said we had flown the breakout and were established on the approach - going around and flying his instructions would've led to a second RA since we were instructed to climb right into that jet that just flew over the top of us. Tower changed his mind and gave us [Runway] XXR landing clearance. ZZZ ATC workload was extremely high and the [Runway] XXL RJ didn't intercept the localizer at all; nor were they aware of the traffic around them (us).High ATC workload and neighboring jet on the other approach had low SA (Situational Awareness).ZZZ workload review and talk to the crew who blew through the [Runway] XXL localizer and crossed into us.

Assigned DRLLR 5 arrival into IAH. We were planning 26R RNAV Y. We were cleared to descend via the DRLLR 5. 6000 was set in altitude window and aircraft was in LNAV/VNAV. Runway was not assigned so approaching SKLER; Captain queried ATC. ATC advised ILS 26R. Crew discussed ensuring proceeding straight after SKLER. Captain as Pilot Monitoring began to change the approach in the FMC; in the process the aircraft leveled off at 7000; rather continuing the decent to 6000. We recognize this a couple miles from SKLER but before we were able to notify ATC that we might be high at SKLER; ATC assigned us a descent to 5000. We proceeded to descend to the new altitude and land uneventfully.

We were flying a parcel of contaminated blood. The box had the UN1845 on it as well as the Dangerous Goods Form. I thought about it and felt it was correct. Later that evening after signing off duty and the next day I was thinking that the parcel should have also had a UN3373 as well as a category B bio substance label. I only saw the Dangerous Goods Form and parcel that was labeled UN1845.I saw the parcel and inspected it and it's associated paperwork during preflight.Possible incomplete labeling of HAZMAT.At the time I looked the information up in the Dangerous Goods manual as well as online and determined in the short time we had that it only needed the UN1845 and nothing else. The more I thought about it however; I feel that it may should have had the UN3373 and cat B bio labels as well.[More correct procedure];verify with the manuals and if still not sure; discuss it with Dispatch or the Chief Pilot.

As I am greeting passengers and handing out wipes; Passenger X seated in X; boards the aircraft with mask barely covering his nose or mouth; him and his family. I gently as them to please cover nose and mouth to board; they comply. Keep in mind even kids barely have mask. They all boarded with mask on chin. When they get to their seats they still have it on. Now after pilots inform us that we are departing; Passenger X completely takes off his mask; passengers around him are looking uncomfortably anxious and he is looking to see if I am can see him.  Although the lights are dim in the cabin I can still see him and he sees me. I do a friendly gesture so he can put it on. He completely ignores me. Then when I get the 10;000 I get up to kindly ask him to place mask over nose and mouth; he complies and then as I walk away he takes it off. I let him know one more time announcements say. He doesn't comply and wife [has] her mask off too; to tell me why don't I leave him alone. At this point I head towards forward to inform pilots and they let me know to let him know one last time. When I informed him I spoke to the pilots and then finally after three gentle reminder; he and wife and kids comply.

We were delayed yesterday because of ATC issues. By the time we took off it was dark and we had been up for 12 hours. As we flew east off the coast of State X we encountered moderate turbulence and precipitation. The Captain got distracted and as we leveled off at 7;000 feet his speed began to increase. I called out 'Speed' and he did not acknowledge so again I said 'Watch your speed.' By this time we were doing around 275 knots and I don't why but he thought we should continue the climb so he used the autopilot to begin to climb the plane. I immediately told him to maintain 7;000 and reduce his speed. The initiated climb resulted in an altitude gain of 400 feet and the Controller told us to maintain 7;000 feet. We returned to 7;000 with no other issues and slowed. I was told our delay was a result of staffing issues at ZZZ center because of COVID-19. This is by far the longest ATC delay I have experienced and I definitely know fatigue was a factor hear. Do I think we were unsafe? No; I do not. That being said I don't think we were the tip of the spear that late in the day. ATC was super busy on the radios and we tried calling several times to request higher to get out of the weather we were in to no avail. Under staffing; busy airspace; delays; fatigue; and weather all combined would be the problem in my opinion. As well as pressure to get the job done.

Descending on LINKK 1 arrival ATC cleared us at some point (think above 10.000 feet) for the ILS 27 approach. Pilot Monitoring re-selected the ILS 27 connecting the transition from RDFSH (which was not done on initial FMC setup). Our altitude was set at 6000 passed RDFSH. We never set FAF altitude to keep the descent.Houston approach checked if we were cleared for the approach. After that; I realized we were supposed to descend via the transition. No further ATC communication was received however after the crew debrief and looking at the approach plate again; the altitudes were mandatory altitude restrictions and we don't think we made GEYGR and/ or SYYKO.
Report_2_Narrative: I was the First Officer and pilot monitoring on this flight IAH. We were cleared to descend via the LINKK1 arrival except maintain 300 knots. Then; 320 knots. Then; as fast as practical until LINKK. Then we were cleared for the approach from RDFSH and maintain 210 knots until FESTA. We had 6000 feet in the MCP window as this was the bottom altitude for the STAR. After being cleared the approach; I failed to set a lower altitude. Shortly after RDFSH (and level at 6000') ATC asked if we were descending on the approach. We were immediately aware of our mistake and the captain began descending. GEYGR and SYYKO (the next two fixes on the approach after RDFSH) had mandatory altitudes of 4000' and 3000'; respectively. Because of our delayed descent after RDFSH; we were high at both of those fixes. The multiple airspeed changes on the arrival caused a distraction and I failed to notice that I hadn't set a lower altitude to continue the approach after RDFSH. That being the case; it was still my job as pilot monitoring to set the lower altitude and I failed to do it.

Report_1_Narrative: On final; Runway XXL; ZZZ; Night; 1000 foot ceiling; Me (Captain) flying; gusty winds; very light load; FO low currency- only 150 hrs in type since hired 1 year ago; and has not been flying much. I was hand flying at 2;000 feet on normal ILS. Called for gear down; flaps 15 at approximately 2;000 feet and flaps 30 landing; check at about 1500 feet. FO ran check list; while I hand flew the approach. The winds were gusty; our target was +15 ref and it was night.  I saw flap refs change on airspeed tape; and glanced at gear lights when FO ran checklist; but I did not have full attention to FO duties since it was dark and gusty. We broke out at 1;000 feet; I called clear to land and set missed approach altitude. At 500 feet we simultaneously got the 500 auto call out and Too Low Flaps. I simultaneously; started a Go-Around (finger to toga button) and looked at flap gauge - which was at 25. But I hesitated on the go-around when I saw why we got the GPWS. I commanded flaps set to 30; which the FO immediately did. We were then about 350-400 feet and stable; and I continued to a normal landing. After landing I realized; even though there was adequate time to set the flaps to 30; I should have gone around. We debriefed after parking. The FO said he just missed the flaps in setting and checking on the checklist; most likely due to being low time in aircraft and rusty from time off. I missed his wrong setting; due to the dark and the gusty winds requiring my attention. I said I should have gone around; and that he as well should have told me to. I am confident if I had not immediately recognized why we got the too low flaps and immediately corrected it; I would not have continued.

Captain (CA) was PF and First Officer (FO) was PM. We had a crew alert action of AUTOFLT FCU1 failure not far from our initial descent for the STAR. CA was working the crew alert issue and Center called saying what the CA heard was 'Flight ABC'; Direct ZZZZZ descend via ZZZZZ1' (CA was in the QRH; reading about or looking for the crew alert issue).  After talking with the FO on the ground in ZZZ; after the event; the FO thought the read back something like 'Flight ABC direct ZZZZZ for the ZZZZZ1.'  Then; PM made sure PF was inserting the bottom altitude of STAR in the FCU (Meaning PM understood the clearance to descend via the ZZZZZ1).  PF did insert the bottom altitude. Then; shortly afterwards; we hit some moderate turbulence and PF decided to start descend early because he could not read the QRH; hoping to get into some clear air.  Then we were told we descended and there may have been a flight deviation.  I did not think so at the time because of the clearance I understood.I believe there were several causal factors on this event. 1) Pilot Inexperience.  The FO was on the Type X aircraft and in March was awarded the Type Y aircraft.  But from March through December; all the FO did was go to Type Y training; and IOE.  This was the FO's first flight since IOE. The FO was going very slow making sure no mistakes were made. The PF was also going very slow to ensure all FO tasks were completed.  2) Task saturation (crew alert event; pilot inexperience; turbulence and clearance understanding). Combined; this put both pilots in a 'caution situation.'  The PF; was trying for something like this would not happen from the moment the flight started by making sure he was slow and deliberate on all his thoughts and actions. 3) Turbulence event. We descended early because PF had a hard time reading the QRH. 4) PF flying and completing crew alert actions.  In this case; it may have made a difference; but; I do not believe it would have made a difference; because the PM understood the clearance from Center was a clearance to descend Via;   and when the turbulence started; the person working the crew alert action would have said descend to get out of the turbulence the QRH could be read. The same outcome would have happened.Suggestions - Verify the clearance; no matter the circumstances...In this particular event; the PF should have verified the clearance knowing the proficiency of the other pilot may not have been 100%.  A pilot may be 'Current' with regards to the official written documents approved by the FAA; but they may have not been proficient...(I do not know how this can be fixed...)
Report_2_Narrative: In preparation for descent; Status Page showed an inoperative system the CA/PF noticed. CA was in the process of investigating the crew alert message that caused it. We had a seasoned CA in the Jump Seat who was assisting in the lookup for the crew alert actions; if any; and the code was send to Maintenance. In that time; we received a call from Center to proceed 'direct to ZZZZZ for the ZZZZZ1.' I read the clearance back 'direct ZZZZZ for the ZZZZZ1 arrival'. I then conveyed to the CA/Pilot Flying 'direct to ZZZZZ; we can descend via the ZZZZZ1' and I backed CA up to set direct ZZZZZ in the FMS and dialed in the lowest altitude in the arrival for south landings. We verified the altitude. While inbound to ZZZZZ; we were at FL390 and encountered some occasions of moderate turbulence and continuous light chop. The CA/PF commenced a descent to avoid remaining in the turbulent conditions. I contacted the Center to let them know we were descending out of FL390. They called back to ask what was the reason for the descent. I stated we had encountered moderate to light turbulence and needed to descend to avoid it. They called back asking me to confirm the reason for descent was turbulence. I confirmed. Then; before we were handed off to the next frequency; the controller asked us to contact a number for possible altitude deviation. There was a misunderstanding; on my part; of the vertical clearance in the call with Center with the initial clearance for ZZZZZ1 and I passed that misinterpretation on to the CA/PF.It was my first flight in over a month; preceded only by sim events way back in September and just prior to OE. This was a threat up front. Hadn't flown in this environment in over 18 months. We had multiple distractions due to the crew alert and system loss being addressed; icing conditions and turbulence. The ATC frequency was covering high traffic volume so there was a significant amount of talking on the frequency. We had both been up very early in order to be ready at report time. My expectation bias led me to believe we were cleared to 'descend via' when we were cleared only laterally for the approach.We could have briefed the arrival prior to addressing the crew alert and done a transfer of control of the aircraft and radios. I could have had ATC repeat the clearance; once the PF was back with me; so that they could communicate directly what the intended clearance was. I could have stated the clearance exactly as it was read back to eliminate any 'single point' of interpretation; or misinterpretation.

Report_1_Narrative: I was the flying pilot; we were kept high the whole time. Then we were given [Runway] XXR GPS Y; as we turned; the turn was inside the approach fix; it was tight.  I turned to final and we looked high and I wasn't comfortable; so we asked for a go-around. I initiated TOGA; we were approximately 2;200 feet when we did the go-around; and began the go-around process and around 2;500 feet. ATC asked to maintain 3;000 feet. We got extremely busy and by the time we realized it we were at 3;500 feet.  We descended to 3;000 feet and we asked to extend the downwind.I had been out for a year and half; and since I gotten back; I did not fly that much; also was off last month and half of this month due to COVID-19; but also I should have asked for extended downwind in the airport; should not [have] allowed the Controller to rush us and I have learned a lesson.Don't accept any arrival that I am not comfortable with.

I was acting as pilot flying on the flight to ZZZ. While flying on our assigned course direct to the ZZZ airport; approximately 100 miles from our destination; while conducting the arrival and approach briefings; ATC Center assigned us to turn left 10 degrees; which I complied with by selecting heading mode on the FCP; and selecting the desired heading with the heading bug. Several minutes later; the Controller; again; assigned us to proceed direct to the ZZZ airport. I selected in the FMS direct to the ZZZ airport; confirmed the selection with my captain; and executed the flight plan modification. However; I had neglected to select NAV mode; which resulted in the aircraft continuing on its previously assigned heading for several minutes while I finished the arrival briefing; resulting in the course to show a full-scale deflection to the left (south). Upon finishing the briefing; I realized the missed step; and immediately turned back toward the course and armed NAV mode. Having recently switched over to ZZZ approach; within several minutes the controlled queried us about our position; informing us we were left of course. We notified the controller that we were rejoining the course. The controller then assigned us to proceed direct to the ZZZ airport again; which we selected in the FMS; confirmed; and executed; this time ensuring NAV mode was engaged. The flight continued without issue.This event was caused by lack of recent flight experience; as well as momentary task-saturation.This has not been a reoccurring trend for me during training or during revenue operations. I plan to continue to maintain a high level of situational awareness in the cockpit to trap threats before they become errors. I also plan to ensure I am making appropriate FCP selections when I am making an adjustment to our course.

Crew stepped off aircraft and was on jetbridge. When I looked at my handheld it said we were boarding. So I replied back we had not been cleaned or sprayed yet so we were not boarding. About 3 to 4 minutes later 2 cleaners came up the airstairs and boarded. One had an electric vacuum in hand the other had [a] trash bag in hand. Shortly after a 3rd cleaner came on board with a bag of supplies and boarded the aircraft and put [the] bag down by the First Class Lavatory. About 5 minutes later another cleaner (gentleman with nothing in his hands) came up the airstairs and boarded the aircraft. Within 10 to 15 seconds the first 2 cleaners came off the aircraft and right after them the other gentleman came off the aircraft saying 'we are good to go.' One of my crewmember's asked him was the aircraft sprayed. He repeated 'we were good to go' and proceeded to exit the jetbridge door and down the airstairs. I and the other crew member both said I don't think the aircraft was sprayed as neither of us saw the cone being put in place nor did we see the sprayer. At this time the last cleaner with the bag with supplies came off. I asked her has this aircraft been sprayed? She said what did he say; I said he said yes; she proceeded to exit the aircraft.  I said can you please show me what you have in that bag; because if there is no sprayer in there; then there is no way our aircraft has been sprayed. She then said 'I will tell him to come back.' At this time the Gate Agent came down to see if we were ready to board. I explained the situation to the Agent and she said 'oh no I am so sorry; that's not right.' The Captain was on the jetbridge with us and we were all in agreement that we needed to be sprayed before we could start boarding. The previous gentleman came back up the airstairs with sprayer in hand and sprayed aircraft. When he was done; without further delay; we boarded and departed. The Gate Agent was very apologetic about the incident.

As background; ZZZ was heavily congested with training and weekend aviators. The approach to the downwind leg was reported via UNICOM as 4nm SE to enter a 45 - downwind entry. We coordinated spacing with another crosswind aircraft to follow us for spacing in the pattern.Reported downwind uneventfully and recognized the aircraft we were to follow and minimized their pattern to minimize spacing conflicts. Reported [our] base turn to UNICOM; uneventfully. Our track on base leg was approximately 2 nm from the end of the runway. At no time was our location questioned by the conflicting near miss aircraft.Reported a base to final turn; uneventfully and unquestioned. Upon the completion of the turn to final; I focused on vertical alignment to the runway and did not see the conflicting near miss aircraft. The PNF (Pilot Not Flying); in a radio call questioned the conflicting near miss aircraft to determine the position upon hearing the UNICOM base report. For clarification; the safety pilot - PNF; is a seasoned 121 CA (Captain) and highly experienced in the PA24-250 with close to 2;000 hours in type. After it was determined the conflicting aircraft turned inside of our location; resulting in cutting us off placing our aircraft in a hazardous flight profile; PNF took control of the aircraft in a professionally coordinated manner.At this time; the conflicting near miss aircraft was well within 1/4 NM and somewhat above our altitude; which was approximately 300 ft AGL. Because of the proximity to us; and apparently being unaware of our location; the resolution was complex. Keep in mind in their base to final turn (RH pattern) with a low wing aircraft [they] would have then been unable to locate us.Additionally; as we both rolled out on final we were both nearly lined up on the centerline; the closure rate was approximately 42 FPS and without evasive action; contact would have occurred in about 30 seconds; short of the runway; all due to the difference in V-ref and approach speeds. The PNF recognized this before I had visual contact with the conflicting traffic and they took control of the aircraft and became the PF.The evasive maneuver was complex in that [the other aircraft's] intentions seemed unpredictable and we did not want to initiate a go-around with the associated high deck angle; which would interrupt visual conflict with the conflicting traffic. Communication from their aircraft was weak and seemed confused as they; on short final; radioed 'how many aircraft are in the pattern and where are you located'. We discussed the possibility that they may begin a go around and that would worsen the conflict since we may have lost visual contact and keep in mind... at this time we were behind their location and out of view.In the interest of safety; we chose to sidestep slightly left and to pass them on the level and then after we cleared the aircraft to begin a go around. The point we were trying to achieve was to maintain visual contact with the conflicting aircraft until we passed clear of their position.

Aircraft X took off RIL climbing to 15;000 feet; at around 10;000 feet; Aircraft X informed me he was having a landing gear issue; could not climb further and needed to return to RIL right away. I sent him direct to RIL. He had good sight of the terrain and once he got the visual he was cleared the visual approach. Aircraft Y was another aircraft going into RIL north of the airport level at 14;000 feet with no approach clearance. Aircraft X appeared to be circling to the runway so I did not clear Aircraft Y into the airport. Aircraft X then popped back on the frequency saying they cannot land at RIL and need to go to [another airport]. Aircraft X at this point was 8;000 feet climbing eastbound still in priority handling status. I re-radar identified Aircraft X and had them climb to 13;000 feet to try to maintain positive Minimum IFR Altitudes. They affirmed they had good sight of the terrain. I called the traffic to both Aircraft Y and Aircraft X hoping to apply visual separation. Neither saw one another so they both remained 13;000 feet (Aircraft X) and 14;000 feet (Aircraft Y).Once able; I vectored Aircraft X south to get him away from high Minimum IFR Altitudes and the RIL approach corridor. I asked Aircraft Y 'if I turn you direct to RIL; do you think you can get a visual with the airport?' Aircraft Y responded he could. I then said 'Aircraft Y; cleared direct RIL' I then went back to Aircraft X and asked fuel remaining; souls on board; what altitude they could climb to; and then explained why 15;000 feet was not feasible. I offered GJT etc. While discussing options with Aircraft X; I noticed Aircraft Y leave 14;000 feet and begin their descent while still 3 to 4 miles from Aircraft X. I gave a traffic alert to Aircraft Y and told them to climb back to 14;000 feet immediately. I then gave the traffic alert to Aircraft X. Aircraft Y climbed back up; I gained the 5 miles and climbed Aircraft X up and gave Aircraft Y the visual approach to RIL. I issued Aircraft Y advisories.In this session I had two separate situations. A pilot deviation; an arrival rush; a handful of ornery pilots on the ground at RIL clogging the frequency looking for releases off the ground. Had RIL sector been separated out through our split frequency procedures; this may not have occurred. There was a lot of frequency congestion between the sector's two frequencies which blocked transmissions to the priority handling aircraft. We used to do this procedure and have been recently barred from doing it again. This procedure could have helped!

Ten minutes into the flight; we received an ACARS message. It stated that oxygen cylinders were on-board. We did not receive a Dangerous Goods Form on the ground. Once we landed in ZZZ1; we reached out to Dispatch and received a copy of the Dangerous Goods form. I signed it; and it was faxed back to ZZZ.
Report_2_Narrative: Approximately ten minutes into our climb out from ZZZ; we received a message from Dispatch telling us we had four oxygen bottles in the cargo area. We did not receive a Dangerous Goods Form from the Ramp prior to our departure. We continued on to our destination. In ZZZ1 we were given the proper paperwork. Captain signed it; and paperwork was sent; via fax; to ZZZ.

Report_1_Narrative: While on the ZZZZZX Arrival we were at FL350. We were given a descent to FL330 and to cross ZZZZZ at FL240. I set FL24000 in the MCP and the Captain set cruise in the FMC to FL330 and we started down. I looked at the ND display and saw FL240 next to ZZZZZ and a Top of Descent point and thought we were set up correctly. In reality the FMC was still loaded with FL240; At or Above; which is the altitude loaded with the ZZZZZ1 arrival. We noticed the error and made the changes and started the descent; however we were too high at that point to make the crossing at FL240; I think we were about 2;000 feet high. The Controller did not say anything to us and handed us off to the next Controller. You can say there may be some currency issues in that I have only flown about 9 flights since March; and this was 3rd time at the controls in the last 30 days. However I have been on the aircraft for almost X years and I know what it's supposed to do. I just misread the ND display and didn't see the A (At or Above) next to FL240.

[We were] inbound to Ontario; CA. Prior to the descent the flight was brief following the [approach briefing]. Ontario was landing 8L and 8R; with good VFR weather and a light wind from the west. The crew descended on the Ziggy 7 arrival and was given a clearance to track outbound on 270 radial after Paradise VOR. There was a distraction trying to set up the tracking of a radial in the FMS by the pilot monitoring. As the pilot flying I decided to use the VOR and VOR mode to accomplish this. We complied with clearance and were cleared to 3;000 feet.After leveling at 3;000 feet a traffic report was issued for a helicopter at 1 o'clock and 4;000 feet. We were given a base leg heading of 320. After starting this turn an amber target showed up on the PFD (Primary Flight Display) at our altitude. I was looking out the window for traffic; in a matter of 2 seconds the Captain pushed forward on the controls; we got a TA/RA descend descend warning; I saw a small aircraft at 2 o'clock and 100 yards; I turned off the Autopilot and Autothrottle stopped the turn towards the small aircraft and descended to 2;500 feet. [We were] cleared to remain at 2;600 feet and received radar vectors to join the Localizer and landed 8L. The Captain was the pilot monitoring and the First Officer was the pilot flying. The ATC controller did make some kind of apology regarding the event. There was a minor distraction regarding the tuning of our radios. I had the VOR tuned and needed to make a switch to the ILS frequency. This took a matter of seconds to correct and we had the airport in sight the entire time.The Captain and I debriefed the event and felt we made all the correct decisions. The next day we confirmed how to track a radial using the FMS and LNAV mode. A note regarding small aircraft in vicinity might be a good idea. Knowing how to set up the FMS to track a radial would be helpful. Above all fly the airplane; keep your eyes outside; and expect the unexpected.
Report_2_Narrative: Descending on the ZIGGY ARRIVAL ATC gave clearance to fly the PDZ 270 radial after PDZ. PM (Pilot Monitoring) was unable to input the radial to fly in LNAV and the PF (Pilot Flying) flew the radial using raw data. ATC gave clearance to descend from 4;000 to 3;000 with a right turn to a heading of 340 for vectors to 08L. ATC advised traffic (helicopter) in the turn at 3;500 and gave clearance to continue turn to 020. Helicopter traffic was never observed. On TCAS PM saw a target which appeared to be at 3;000 feet on the 020 heading and advised PF to stop turn. TCAS aural warning TA 'traffic traffic' annunciated and PM saw traffic as TCAS aural alert advised an RA 'traffic descend now'. PF and PM both pushed controls forward as conflicting traffic continued to descend and turn into the jets path. PM advised ATC of the new heading altitude deviation due to the RA. Conflict was resolved at 2;300 feet; PF leveled off and started climb back up to 3;000. ATC advised stop climb at 2;600 and continue turn to 020 to intercept 08L LOC with an apology for conflict. PF intercepted GS inside NAROD (FAF 08L) due to ALT (Altitude) assignment by ATC of 2;600 feet. PF and PM had field in site and terrain in site during all phases. ATC reported 'helicopter' traffic at 3;500. Both PM and PF did not observe the traffic on TCAS at 3500 nor saw any helicopter traffic in the initial turn. Controller change during the descent from 4;000-3;000 feet. Weather was VMC daytime.The clearance received to fly the 270R outbound caused PM and PF to be distracted. PM could not get the radial to input in the FMC and therefore the PF flew raw data. This created additional threats which created additional distractions at the low ALT 2;600 with terrain all around. Having had to use RAW DATA the radio panel had mismatched frequencies and MCP had mismatched courses in the selectors which caused FD to give incorrect LOC info. PM was able to configure the MCP/radio panel and EFIS in time to capture LOC before crossing LOC. It caused the PM to be heads down instead of assisting PF in confirming the airport in sight for a visual backup. During debrief both pilots agreed that asking for a climb back to 3;000 may have helped with visual guidance to help with positional awareness. The following leg PM/PF practiced input of radials off VOR stations to create a less threatening environment for the 'next time.'

Report_1_Narrative: Departing ZZZ1 for ZZZ2 I was the PM (Pilot Monitoring) and the CA (Captain) was the PF (Pilot Flying). We planned a low altitude flight due to the shortness of the leg. Some altitudes in the legs page were edited to allow the lower cruise altitude to be selected. The flaps 10 takeoff went as normal (LNAV/VNAV). The PF called for flaps 1 as near as I can recall. I believe this is the case as most if not all of our previous legs had been flaps 5 takeoffs. I was distracted with a radio call or frequency change and I moved the flaps to 1 and did not catch the error. Shortly after that; the FMC changed to the descent phase and the speed bug moved to the flaps 5 maneuver speed; and the A/T (Auto Throttle) mode went from N1 to FMC speed; which resulted in the flaps 5 maneuver speed being the A/T target. As; or just prior to this happening the PF called for flaps up. I moved the flap lever to the up position; and since we were no longer accelerating got a momentary stick shaker. The PF instinctively lowered the nose reducing the AOA (Angle Of Attack). I increased the speed in the MCP window to get the A/T to command acceleration. The remainder of the flight was normal.Suggest crews be alert for unusual mode reversions when using low altitudes for cruise. I would probably use non VNAV takeoff procedures in the future. The big lesson is not new; vigilance absolutely has to be maintained during the 'red' parts of the flight path. The PM job is challenging and can be intolerant of errors.

While performing task card Passenger Compartment Seat Installation in conjunction with the STA (Station) 540 slot sealant inspection seat row 12ABC was inadvertently reinstalled in the wrong seat row position. Although we do not remove seat row 13ABC for this inspection; somehow row 13ABC and 12ABC were in swapped positions as reported by an inflight crew member due to tray table operation being impaired due to the armrest tray table seat row being installed in the wrong seat row position. Although I know I verified part numbers and serial numbers of all rows of seats after they were removed to facilitate the STA 540 slot sealant inspection; I cannot be certain that I verified part number and serial number of seat row 12ABC after final installation as required per the task card step. Seat row 12ABC being swapped with 13ABC is an anomaly that I can not explain as we do not remove seat row 13ABC during this process. I understand that I could have possibly transposed part numbers during my inspection and let the incorrect part number get reinstalled in the wrong position if that seat row arrived in the wrong position which is the only way I can account for this seat row being in the incorrect position per the drawing as we never removed row 13ABC.

We were approximately 20 miles SE of the airport in the mountains on a sight-seeing flight. I was PIC (Pilot In Command) and the only pilot on board with 2 passengers. At 4;500 MSL the AWOS was intermittent because of the mountains. The MEF (Maximum Elevation Figure) on the sectional chart was 4;400 MSL and I was working my around some shorter hills. While experiencing some lighter bumps over a ridge; the 'GEAR IN TRANSIT' annunciator light lit up. I immediately checked to make sure I hadn't selected the gear handle down because we were too far away from the airport to have the gear down. The selector handle was still in the 'GEAR UP' position so my next instinct was to grab the emergency gear override and hold it in the 'UP' position to keep the gear from extending so far out. I checked the circuit breaker and everything seemed normal. I cycled the gear down and got 3 green lights and when cycled back up the green lights went away but the transit light never turned off. The AWOS suddenly kicked in and led me to decide on Runway 8 which meant I would cross the airport midfield and enter a tear drop for a left downwind to Runway 8. On the sectional I saw the altitude as 1;907 and decided to over fly the field at 2;500. In my mind 1;907 was pattern altitude and not the airport altitude. My mind was still on making sure my gear didn't fall down and create more drag for me that far away from the airport. About 1.5 miles to the south of the airport I once again communicated on the CTAF of my intentions to over fly the field at 2;500. One aircraft said they were back taxiing and going to depart Runway 8. As we approached the airport I felt like I was too low even though I was at 2;500 and then realized my mistake by how well I could see details on the ground. I quickly announced my intentions to climb up to traffic pattern altitude of 2;900 feet just before reaching the south side of the airport pattern which meant I was then climbing into the pattern instead of already being at the correct altitude. Directly over the runway we were back to 2;900 feet and I continued out to do a right hand turn and enter a tear drop to the left downwind. The gear came down fine and we landed safely. The combination of trying to troubleshoot an annunciator light while listening to incoming traffic and the intermittent AWOS left me to be distracted and mixing up proper altitudes for the pattern.Better self-briefing prior to arrival to an airport would have greatly reduced the chance of this occurring. Along with remembering that I had plenty of fuel and could have circled at a safe altitude to troubleshoot and then with a clearer mind come up with a plan to land at the airport normally.

We took the aircraft that was returned to service after a large bird was ingested into the #1 engine on the previous flight. The #1 engine was started first prior to taxi out to ensure no issues with the engine. All engine indications after start were normal. The takeoff was normal with no abnormal engine indications. On departure just after retracting the landing gear; a strong smell from the previously ingested bird permeated throughout the aircraft. Shortly after; the forward lav smoke detector sounded. The aircraft was leveled off; and then we began to execute the Smoke; Fire or Fumes Checklist. Once stabilized on a left downwind with Departure Control notified; First Officer (FO) now flying; I contacted the Flight Attendants to confirm the status of the forward lav. They indicated that there was NO fire; just a very strong and pungent smell throughout the entire cabin. Fully aware that the cause of the alarm was the heavy fumes from the bird; we continued with the checklist and decided to discontinue the flight and return to the airport. We removed our masks; coordinated with Dispatch; and continued on downwind to set up for a visual approach and landing. Engine indications were normal the entire flight and the smell slowly dissipated. The forward lav alarm continued to sound; no fire though. No priority handling was requested. We landed uneventfully and taxied to the gate.The aircraft was returned to service after a bird was ingested on the previous flight. The engine contained a large amount of bird parts around the fan blades and some appeared to be ingested into the compressor section. Contract Maintenance cleaned and inspected the engine in coordination with Maintenance Control and signed off on the logbook. I did inquire of Maintenance about an engine run. They indicated it was not needed. I should have pushed them on it; as we were concerned about the large amount of bird guts all around the fan section. I did not inspect the engine after Maintenance completed their work and felt confident it was accomplished correctly; my mistake; should have crosschecked. The FO did a walkaround prior to departure and noted some bird parts still remaining. A borescope was not accomplished as Maintenance Control indicated it could be done within a couple of flight hours; most likely after the next flight since we were heading to a Maintenance base. Not sure Maintenance Control realized the significant amount of bird ingested into the engine. Could have communicated better with them.

I was the pilot flying into ROA at night. We were at 6;000 abeam the airport on a left downwind for Runway 6. We were cleared for the visual approach and I asked for 3;000 feet to be set into the FCP (Flight Control Panel). I was aware there was a ridge we would need to cross on our left base turn; charted at 2;000 feet on the RNAV (GPS) Y approach plate. I planned to descend to 3;000 while turning base and turn onto final at HIVLU; charted at 2;700 on the vertical profile of the approach plate. I began a descent at 2;000 FPM just prior to turning base. On the base leg; I believe due to the rate of descent; a GPWS terrain warning occurred. I immediately increased the power; increased pitch; and arrested the descent. The warning stopped. The ridge was visible throughout the event and the aircraft did not descend below 3;000 until past the ridge and beginning a turn to intercept the final approach course. We continued and made a normal landing.Being unfamiliar with this approach I should have communicated my plan to the Captain and asked if he agreed. After landing we discussed the approach and he explained that we could have descended on downwind to 5;200; continued past the ridge; turned base at or just inside DIXXY; and then followed the descent profile on the approach plate. I agree with his assessment; that would have been a better course of action. In the future I will verbalize my intentions; particularly on approaches I am unfamiliar with; and have a discussion to collaboratively determine the best course of action.

On the taxiway contacted Tower.  After check in we thought we heard 'clear for takeoff;' but neither of us were sure so I asked the First Officer to confirm if we were cleared for takeoff. The First Officer asked this question and the very clear reply was 'affirmative.'  We entered the runway and were subsequently told while finishing our line up on centerline to stop and hold position.  After a delay we were told of 'pilot deviation' and then cleared for takeoff.I allowed an 'affirmative' reply by Tower to suffice for a query on were we cleared for takeoff.  That is the root cause.  'Affirmative' is not the correct response and in retrospect I believe Tower perhaps misunderstood our query.  Choice of words and insistence on clarification.  I should have asked the First Officer to say 'Tower say again.'  Tower would then have repeated all instructions.  Looking back the word 'Affirmative' is not enough.  I allowed my leeway on foreign controllers English and accents to accept 'affirmative' for a cleared for takeoff query rather than insisting on hearing the entire standard 'cleared for takeoff' verbiage. The Tower could have easily misunderstood us and replied 'Affirmative' to what Tower believed was the question.

About 10 minutes prior to scheduled passenger flight pushback at ZZZ; received a HAZMAT NOTOC (hazardous material notice to Captain).  There were 6 lines of HAZMAT of which 5 were normal but one stood out with lithium metal batteries; ERG (emergency response guidebook) code 12FG.  I queried the messenger about them; he said he could take a picture of the box to show me.  While he went to do that I discussed the situation with the FO (First Officer).  The battery weight was.15 kg or just a bit over 5 ounces and contained in equipment.  That size battery is similar in weight to our iPad batteries.  12FG code is risk of fire/heat/smoke with the additional risk Z 'aircraft cargo fire suppression system may not extinguish or contain the fire.  Consider landing immediately'.  When carried in our cargo hold; with passengers on board; that sort of risk gets my attention.  When the messenger returned with the picture I queried him about carrying that cargo in the cockpit or passenger compartment where we at least have our BCB battery containment bag; in case something went wrong.  He said they didn't know which box batteries were in so that wasn't possible.  It was almost push time so I called and discussed the situation with Dispatch.  They agreed with my concern and said they weren't experts on HAZMAT but could research who was or we could just remove the batteries in the interest of expediency.  We then opted for removing the lithium batteries for simplicity and timeliness.  After this was accomplished; we got a revised load closeout and were on our way about 20-30 minutes late.  The rest of our flight was uneventful.  Having reviewed FOM guidance on this issue previously; I consider it somewhat vague.  While the likelihood for these batteries to start burning is slim; the impact would be catastrophic and the ERG  code even warns us of this.  This could easily be mitigated by having an item like this in the passenger cabin or cockpit where FAs (flight attendants) or pilots could at least deal with the threat with the BCBs which are provided for an unlikely event such as this.  FOM  deal[s] with this and state or imply that these types of things should not be in the cargo compartment.  FOM addresses HAZMAT codes but really gives no guidance other than inspecting NOTOC paperwork for date; tail number etc.I've been involved in carrying lots of hazardous materials in aircraft over my career and believe I'm well versed on the risk vs reward of it.  While technically the NOTOC I received was legal per our FOM; the risk of these batteries also noted on the NOTOC; didn't appear to make sense from a risk management or an SMS (safety management systems) standpoint.  Thus my questions about better potential options.  If we are intentionally doing something like this; I; Dispatch; other pilots etc would probably be better served with more of a heads up on the decision process and other options sooner in the departure process.  Were we just doing the minimum legal or is there a more appropriate way in hindsight?  It certainly seemed like pushback time induced pressure discouraged discussion/questions about this sort of thing.

Onboard the aircraft was a piece of Hazmat which I was unaware of.  The company manifest did not disclose it and I was not notified by any company personnel.  I am unsure of when the piece was loaded; if it was during the preload process or while I was present.  Upon finding the Hazmat during aircraft unloading; I reported it to Dispatch.

This was a second flight of the night shift; and was unusually busy; because the hospital we are associated with is understaffed because of COVID. We got the call to go to ZZZ; and the weather was marginal; but good enough to get in. We accepted the trip; and flew the RNAV approach to Runway XX. Later; I looked at the approach chart; and it said that the approach was not authorized at night. I think that being busy after a long period of hardly flying contributed to the error; and also that it was XA:00 in the morning. Also; due to COVID-induced rules; we had to wear masks flying; and it fogs up my glasses; making it harder to see. I need to make sure that I check all the notes on an approach before accepting a trip.

Passengers X and Y interfering with crew members duties FAR 121.580; refusing to wear a mask and consuming alcohol they brought with them on board the aircraft FAR 121.575.Causing disruption in the cabin.

Flight attendants reported 2 possible inebriated passengers acting belligerent not wearing masks and disturbing surrounding passengers. Flight attendants requested personnel meet the aircraft upon arrival in ZZZ.

It was an uncontrolled airfield; visual approach; left traffic to Runway 17. Daylight calm winds and ceiling and visibility unlimited. Terrain factors were discussed on our briefing. We built the escape maneuver on the secondary flight plan. On the UNICOM frequency we were aware of a (Air Carrier) jet that was north of the field landing south on 17. They decided to slow so we could go first. We made a shorter approach than usual and while turning towards the final approach fix and putting out flaps 3; we descended away from the terrain as it sloped down hill and we got a terrain warning and reacted appropriately. We were very aware of the terrain and were completely surprised that the GPWS sounded off. We continued the approach and landed normally.We should have just extended our downwind leg and went number 2 to the runway.Maybe a little better understanding of the normal traffic patterns on the company pages.

Passing FL230 in VNAV Climb FMC airspeed was set at 310 KIAS. As airspeed approached Vmo; power was reduced but the airspeed briefly passed Vmo by about 5 knots and for 3-5 seconds. Pilot flying was new to airplane and neither of us are flying much.  Pay closer attention to FMC inputs; fly more!
Report_2_Narrative: Entered climb speed in FMS of 310. Previous Econ climb was 257/.81.  I failed to enter .81 after 310/. As the aircraft climbed speed approached MMO at FL 230. Adjusted speed in FMS but the aircraft was empty and continued to accelerate. I manually reduced the throttles; but not enough we exceeded about 5 knots before we slowed down.New to the aircraft. I had not flown since OE ended. Fly more. But we all know the environment.

Report_1_Narrative: ATC said there was rain on the field. There was a B737 flight ahead of us on the approach by 5 to 10 minutes. We were listening to both Approach and Tower to monitor their approach and landing. We discussed holding; prior to the preceding aircraft landing. The preceding B737 reported safe on deck; medium braking; and no issues. They said they broke out 300 feet above minimums; 500 feet above ground. This gave us confidence to conduct the approach and landing. We complied with the wet runway requirements on the 10-7 page (max braking).We were fully configured with flaps 30 by the final approach fix with the landing checklist complete. We were stable at both the 1000 feet and 500 feet calls. Approach was mostly smooth in the clouds with no turbulence. Auto throttles moved very little. The rain was moderate; maybe moderate to high. Once we broke out; we could see the runway the entire time. Wiper were keeping up; but on high. We broke out about 300 feet. Saw some ground before that; so knew we were close to breaking out. Saw the Runway End Identifier Lights at 300 feet and the runway edge lights and center line lights shortly after. Captain called 'approaching minimums' just prior to breaking out and I said I had the field in sight. The auto pilot and auto throttles were disengaged around 200 feet.Landed a little left of center line; but the right main gear was to the right of the center line. Nose was pointed down the runway. Landing was firm to hard. Flare was normal. Raised nose to arrest descent at 30 feet and power was at idle at touchdown. We were both surprised by the firmness of the landing. Nose wheel was set down normally. I thought we might have skipped a little bit; but the Captain did not think so. Maybe we hydro-planed then the tires grabbed. We immediately started sliding left. I don't remember us turning to the left. It seemed we were pointed down the runway but going left. I stepped on the right rudder; but it didn't seem to have an effect. The left main mount went off the pavement on the left side of the runway. It seemed to take a while to get it back on the pavement with full right rudder and full manual braking. With both pilots stepping on full manual brakes; the aircraft did not seem to slowdown. Never felt the brakes grabbing; and with max brakes; we should have felt the brakes grab. I do not know if that Captain was using the nose wheel steering to assist getting it back to the right.Once back on the pavement; we started to veer to the right; but never left the pavement on the right side. Once on the runway; we added full reverse. We put in full left rudder to keep it on the runway. We got the aircraft direction under control; but it was still pulling to the right with full braking and full left rudder. I think the Captain was countering the pull with nose wheel steering. The aircraft was still difficult to slow down.We exited the runway on the high-speed taxiway on the right at a slow speed and came to a stop just off the runway. Tower asked about our position and if we needed assistance. The aircraft instruments; indications and controls were all normal. The only noticed adverse indication was an 'anti-skid inop ' light. The Captain released the brakes and rolled forward a little to check for a blown tire and brake function. All seemed fine so we said we can proceed to the gate. The taxi in was uneventful.On the taxi in we informed Tower that we may have departed the paved surface and there may be Foreign Object and Debris (FOD)on the runway.
