After shutdown; the Captain got up and checked on the Flight Attendants and passengers. All seemed fine. One Flight Attendant said she was scared while it was occurring; but otherwise no adverse comments. They were obviously a little shaken at the time; but not excessively so. We contacted Company maintenance and wrote up the maintenance items that we knew about. Several of the Flight Attendants were working our deadhead flight back the next morning. Theystill did not seem to have any after effects from the event and were friendly toward us.Maybe the initial left drift was due to the right gear hydro planning while the left gear had full auto-brakes using anti-skid. Once the left gear returned to the pavement; it's ant-skid may have been damaged; causing the veering to the right under full brakes.
Report_2_Narrative: Leg 3 of a transitioning First Officer on a 4 day 15 hour IOE (Initial Operating Experience). The First Officer had just flown the previous leg and done well. He shot the ILS Approach and flew a nice approach to a very nice landing. During the 3 hour sit prior to our flight we discussed the upcoming flight including the special airport requirements and 10-7 pages. Reviewed the flight plan NOTAMs and maintenance status of the aircraft. Noted the sequenced flashing lights to our planned arrival runway were out of service.First Officer made a nice takeoff and climb out to cruise and we continued to update the weather forecasts on the flight. Reviewed 10-7 pages decided to use max braking for training and for possibility of a wet runway. Worst weather report we saw was 4000 feet broken; light rain; and winds 240 at 8. Briefed the arrival and RNAV Approaches due to winds. We also briefed the ILS. Approaching top of descent asked Center which runway was in use. The ILS was the approach in use. Pulled up the landing data. Approaching with radar on noticed a cell and heard [an] aircraft ask for deviations. We asked for and received direct ZZZZZ on the arrival. Descending to 12;000 feet were cleared for the arrival and preceding aircraft ask for direct to a fix. There appeared to be cells just south of the approach. Approach informed us there was rain on the field; we discussed possible holding when we heard [another aircraft] cleared for the approach.I tuned VHF 2 to Tower frequency to get a heads up on the preceding aircraft. They were cleared to land and reported to Tower breaking out a few hundred feet above minimums landed and reported braking action medium. Approach cleared us direct to a fix and to report on the localizer cleared for the approach. Slowed toward 180 kts.; flaps 5 auto pilot on LNAV/VNAV; armed the app mode; made 2;500 feet call; reported established and was told to switch to Tower. Called Tower and reported 9 mile final was cleared to land. I believe Tower also said preceding aircraft broke out at 500 feet and braking action medium. Localizer and glideslope engaged set Touchdown Zone Elevation. Approaching the final approach fix gear down flaps 15 rolled speed back then selected flaps 30 and landing checklist.IMC and moderate rain but little to no turbulence or wind shifts. Passing approximately 1;000 to 800 feet saw ground and lights looking down called 1;000 feet then set Missed Approach altitude. Approach was stable and verified at 500 feet. Called approaching minimums slightly early approximately 400 feet to prepare for possibility of a go around. Approximately 300 feet broke out with Runway End Lights; High Intensity Runway Lights and runway center line in sight. First Officer disengaged the auto pilot and auto throttle approximately 150 to 200 feet. Still raining with wind shield wipers on high but could clearly see the runway environment. Short final dipped 1/4 dot low to try and land in the first 1;000 feet. Heard 50 feet just prior to crossing the threshold then 40; 30. At 30 the First Officer began the flare slowing the rate of descent then began reducing power. Everything appeared normal till the last 5 to 10 feet when it felt like the bottom dropped out to a firm to hard landing. Written up as a hard landing.There was no bounce. Power levers at idle and speed brakes deployed. I have used max auto brakes often due to the demo requirement for Operational Experience. Ordinarily they grab and push you forward in the seat. There was no such sensation other than a feeling the anti-skid was cycling and the aircraft did not appear to be slowing. I believe the nose was pointed straight down the runway and the touchdown had the nose just left of the center line. On touchdown the aircraft began to immediately slide to the left. I believe the aircraft hydroplaned after landing. The First Officer depressed full right rudder with no effect. First Officer was slow to go into reverse so I came on the controlsset max reverse and followed full right rudder yet the aircraft continued to slide left and the left main mount left the prepared surface. I used nose wheel steering to bring the aircraft back toward center line. All this happened immediately after touchdown or so I believe. As we continued down the runway with both of us standing on the brakes the aircraft began to slide toward the right side of the runway. With full reverse full manual brakes and what I believed to be full left rudder it required nose wheel input to keep the aircraft from leaving the right side of the runway. At no point in time did I feel the aircraft nose was pointed at or mostly toward either side of the runway from initial touchdown to stop.As we continued down the right side of the runway the aircraft still did not appear to be slowing normally. Approaching the high speed taxiway on the right side of the runway I elected to allow the aircraft to veer onto the taxi way. We had finally slowed where we were able to bring the aircraft to a stop. We were not 100 percent sure the left main mount had left the prepared surface so we discussed the possibility and the possibility of blown tires. Tower asked if we needed assistance. We did not note any usual indications or issues all instruments appeared normal. With the exception of the anti-skid fault light. I allowed the aircraft to roll forward checking the brakes which appeared to operate normally at slow speed and we had no indication of a deflated tire. I decided the aircraft could be taxied to the gate to deplane the passengers safely at slow speed and was cleared by Tower to do so. We also informed Tower there may be FOD (Foreign Object and Debris) on the runway. Uneventful taxi to parking with good clear area all the way into the gate. Performed the parking checklist then left the flight deck to check on the passengers and flight attendants. No injuries were reported. One flight attendant said she was a little shaken. Conferred with base ops then did a walk around with the contract mechanic. Called Company maintenance and made maintenance write ups.

Report_1_Narrative: Hand flying on climbout on the SID I exceeded the 12;000 feet restriction at ZZZZZ by a few hundred feet. There was no communication from ATC regarding the exceedance.Cause - Concentration on hand flying the aircraft.With as little flying as we are doing recently it would be better to engage the autopilot sooner and monitor.

While descending via the ZZZZZ2 STAR into ZZZ; we were given a direct; followed by a heading and a descent to 6;000 feet and a request to fly our best forward speed for spacing.  In complying with the instructions; I descended below 10;000 feet at 300 knots.  At approximately 9;000 feet; I recognized the error; leveled off and slowed to 250 knots.  We continued the flight to an uneventful landing.Although I had gotten a full night's sleep the night before; I was feeling somewhat fatigued by the time we began the flight to ZZZ.  I had been flying long days from the previous 4 day trip that started with a very early commute on the morning.  Compounding this is the fact that my recency of experience is lacking.  I had flown very little during the previous 3 months due to a problem with my international qualifications.I believe that if I had not gotten distracted with the revised clearances from ATC this would not have happened.  I had been flying in VNAV; but opened the speed window when a higher airspeed was requested by ATC.  My improper monitoring of the automation led me to descend below 10;000 feet at a speed above 250 knots.  Unfortunately; my First Officer did not catch the error either.

We were on the ZZZZZ1 arrival into ZZZ and we had a clearance cross the ZZZ1 VOR at Flight Level 260 and then to descent via the ZZZZZ1. We were in a managed descent with 4000 feet set for the bottom of the ZZZZZ1 arrival. Nearing ZZZ1 I thought that we were high on the profile. I selected open descent intending to level off at FL260 and then continue on profile but I did not select FL260 in the FCU window. The airplane was very light at 113;000 pounds and descended very quickly right through the assigned FL260. I intervened by leveling off and we continued in a managed descent on profile for the remainder of the arrival.My estimation that we were high on the profile was inaccurate due to the fact that the airplane was light and capable of descending very quickly; I had not taken that into consideration. The pilot monitoring was heads down and I did not tell him I was changing the mode of descent from managed to open and explain why I was doing so. Had I done so; he would have caught it. This was my first day back to work after two months of leave. I had prepared to return by studying flows; call; procedures; etc. and had briefed the other pilot hoping to prevent errors from happening.Suggestions - Keep the other guy in the loop. Tell him when making a change especially if he is heads down. Always check the altitude selected when changing modes. Always focus on the next restriction.
Report_2_Narrative: We were south of ZZZ1 arrival validation completed; arrival briefing completed clearance to cross ZZZ1 at FL260.  We were then cleared to descend via ZZZZZ1 Runway XXL.  The Captain set 4;000 feet in the FCU.  We both verified the altitude selected and the aircraft was in managed decent.  I verified the aircraft was on profile and would cross ZZZ1 at FL260.  I then updated the MCDU with the new ATIS; did an in range and verified arrival gate and set ramp frequency in the number 2 standby radio.  The Captain then said; 'I messed that up'; or something like that.  I didn't realize the Captain had changed modes to open descent and had descended through the FL260 restriction at ZZZ1.  He said he felt the aircraft was not descending quickly enough.  We crossed ZZZ1 approximately 1;000 feet low.  There was no call from ATC.  The Captain then went back to managed descent and we continued to ZZZ without and further incident.Cause - Lack of communication from the Captain letting me know he went to open descent.  Captain had been on extended time off the last 7 months.Suggestions - Communication of mode changes in decent to Pilot monitoring.  Resetting FCU to next altitude constraint when operating in open decent.

Report_1_Narrative: I wanted to take a few minutes to relate to you my personal experiences during the events that transpired.  We were at the tail end of a lengthy four day trip; with the usual holiday related challenges; weather; maintenance; crew-placarding; lengthy sit times; de-icing etc. On the third day of the trip; we were waiting in line for departure on [Runway] XXL at ZZZ; when the tower informed us that the operation was shut down for a 'deep-cleaning' at ZZZ1 Center.  Having personally experienced the same the same scenario last year when both the ZZZ2 Tower and ground were shut down for a COVID spread throughout their Tower; it didn't take long for us to figure out the reason for the closure.Like everyone one else in our situation; we opened up the FOM and began compliance with the notification processes for the passengers; the Flight Attendants; and ZZZ Ops. My First Officer (FO) and I immediately began working the 14 hour duty day issue; (FO A was my second FO during the course of the trip; and he had a less restrictive duty day constraint than I did).  After nearly two hours at the hold short line; we began the process of finding a gate; with the intent to avoid the prospective government fines levied for lengthy delays. It was the usual chaos; with ZZZ Ops being overwhelmed by this unforeseen event.  As we approach the two hour FOM deadline for locating a gate; we were assigned DXX via ACARS.  While taxiing to the gate; we made multiple attempts to get guidance from Ops; Ramp; Dispatch; etc.; to see what the intent was for the passengers; my crew; and the aircraft. We were unable to get any specific guidance. Following our arrival at the gate; an Agent opened the main door; and told us that the flight would be cancelled. As directed by the agent; the passengers disembarked with their bags. Since we had seen no changes to our crew schedule; nor had we received any kind of communication from crew tracking; we assumed that the agent was correct regarding the cancellation; and as such; we began reviewing our required duty time obligations. Absent any further guidance; we subsequently put the aircraft 'to bed;' and once again we attempted to contact crew tracking; (no luck there).  Rather than exit into the terminal; we elected to continue to wait on the aircraft.After 20 minutes or so; a new Agent came down the bridge; and asked me if we were ready to board the aircraft. Needless to say; I was a bit astonished.  I asked her what the plan was; and she said that we were going to resume the flight to ZZZ3; ASAP.  I reminded her that the airspace was still closed; and that we had not received any contact from either dispatch or tracking; regarding an updated release; and that we would need additional fuel; lavatory/water service; and a resupply of the delay rations that the Flight Attendants had used for the passengers during our time spent waiting on the taxiway. I also told her that both of us; along with two of the Flight Attendants had duty-time issues that we have to calculate and resolve before departure.  She promptly replied and asked me; 'Captain are you refusing to board the aircraft?'I immediately realized that this was the standard pass-the buck/assign the delay language; that some Agents use in an attempt to intimidate Pilots. I took several deep breaths; before I stated to her in no uncertain terms; that we would need to at least begin to address the aforementioned issues; before we would be comfortable boarding the aircraft again.  Her reply was; 'Captain; are you refusing to board the aircraft?'  I answered that until we get some of these issues resolved; yes.  What was clear to myself; my FO and the number one Flight Attendant; is that this was clearly an attempt at pilot-pushing. [In my opinion]; this Agent had one concern; and that was to make sure that these passengers would become someone else's problem; (namely ours).Long story short; and after significant efforts by all involved; we reconstituted the flight and determined that without further lengthy ground delays that we would likely arrive in ZZZ3 within our respective 14 hour duty day window. We then performed our usual checklists; flows; refueling; new clearance etc.After push-back; start and taxi instructions were received; I made my first mistake of the flight; and turned in the direct opposite direction of ground's instructions on the taxi clearance. It was at this point that I realized I was a lot more tired than I thought.  My FO and I discussed this; and we agreed that though we were both somewhat tired; it was safe to proceed; and that we should just take extra time and precautions going forward.Mistake number two on my part was that when ATC changed our departure STAR; we briefed it at the gate; had it up on our iPads; but I had failed to enter it in the MCDU. After wheels up; ATC gave us direct-to clearance to a fix that wasn't on the legs page. My FO quickly realized that I hadn't entered the new departure STAR; and he rapidly remedied it on the MCDU. I was mad at myself for making this series of unprecedented and inexplicable mistakes. Those mistakes continued as we descended on the type of complex STAR with a series of 'at or above' altitude constraints; that confounds the logic on some of the older 'Basic' airplanes.Needless to say; we landed safely in ZZZ3 later that evening. After block-in; I determined that I was within my duty legality; by 20 minutes or so. It had been a very long day.  We had departed ZZZ4 that morning before the sun had risen; and we had left the gate at ZZZ for the second time; well after the sun had set. In summary; I had made many embarrassing operational mistakes during the course of the last leg; beginning with my pre-flight; push back; all the way through the descent into ZZZ3.  The only explanation for my numerous mistakes was fatigue. In short; I will never; ever; again allow the 'system' to push to me beyond my own personal safety limits. I will take my First Officers counsel more seriously when it comes to considering just how tired we are.Additional factors include that when you consider the cumulative effects of four and five day trips on us; along with a 14 hour duty period; it can be well beyond the average pilot's limitations to safely operate.  And while I don't blame the agent personally; (like everyone else; she was just doing the job in the manner that she was trained); and like everyone else she's just under time pressure.  As time has gone by at our company; it has become clear to me; that the safety culture at the top of this airline; is in many respects; mostly honored and kept in place; by those who of us; who are at the pointy end of the spear.In retrospect; the main causal factor was fatigue due to a lengthy delay.Two suggestions.  The first is that [in my opinion]; we need to take into account the cumulative effect of a lengthy four and five day trip on pilot fatigue.  The other issue is Gate Agent conduct and verbiage regarding flight Ops as it regards to boarding the passengers.

I received 3 alerts the ramp was attempting to finalize the DSTG with unsecured Hazmat. [I received an error message in the load planning software.]The flight was already off the gate when the ramp lead attempted to finalize I then pulled up the DSTG and witnesses both the second and third attempt at finalization. It was then that the ramp lead noticed the DG was not secured. I called ZZZ operations to request a phone call from the ramp and while on the phone the lead moved the DG from pit 5 to pit 4 which had 40 bags and proceeded to finalize the flight. Around 8 minutes later the lead called and I inquired as to which pit the DG was actually loaded in and I told him I saw it was planned in pit 5 and a witness him try to finalize the flight 2 [times] then saw the DG move to pit 4 after the flight had departed. The lead responded he was told the DG had been loaded into pit 4. After that conversation I told my shift manager and released the final weights.

During passenger loading; ramp crew received a motorized wheelchair/scooter for an elderly passenger. The passenger declared that the scooter had a dry cell battery and the key was removed. Ramp agents filled out air carrier battery powered wheelchair form mobility device tag and gave the flight deck crew a copy.  Upon receiving this form and observing the type of chair through the cockpit window I became suspicious that this particular type of scooter probably contained a lithium ion battery and the tag was improperly labeled based on the passengers assumption.  The flight attendant also made a similar remark and I wanted to exercise caution before the scooter was loaded into the baggage compartment. We decided to have ramp personnel take the scooter out of the baggage compartment and with the assistance of the passenger open motorized mobility device to ascertain the actual type of battery. Upon opening the device we discovered it did have a lithium ion battery with 25.2 V and 11.5 a/h. Based on the formula the battery had less than 300 watts maximum for transport.  We elected to remove the battery and transport it in the passenger cabin to ensure we had access do it during flight. A new mobility form was signed indicating there was a lithium ion battery for the mobility device and I made a correction to the CLR [form] indicating the actual battery type.Members of the flight crew were able to spot a potential hidden dangerous good and miss labeled document based on passenger assumption. The flight crew worked with ground personnel to ensure the mobility device was not loaded onto the aircraft until we were able to ascertain the actual type of battery was confirmed and under the appropriate limits prescribed in our dangerous goods manual.

On route to the airport the First Officer's Multi Function display failed. We decided to continue destination airport because it was not required equipment. Then we were informed the weather at the airport was deteriorating unexpectedly. We completed all the required tasks and checklist before the we were cleared for the approach. On approach we realized that we were too high and our descent profile was going to be unacceptable for a stable approach. Around 11;000 ft. the Captain called for the Missed Approach. We executed the Missed Approach with no issues and cleaned up the aircraft and started climbing to the missed approach altitude. We were at the missed approach altitude and Captain called for the auto pilot on. During this time there was a bit of confusion and the right turn and swapping the navigation displays and setting the new course for the back course was slow to be executed. We turned to the heading too slow and never intercepted the back course. Once we figured out that we didn't intercept the back course we had the Course Deviation Indicator full-scale deflection. ATC gave us a new altitude and a new heading to start the restart the approach. We got vectored around to the Initial Approach Fix to restart the approach and completed the second approach stabilized without any issues. Failure to comply with the approach profile and the missed approach profile.
Report_2_Narrative: I neglected to call for the First Officer to start our descent. The First Officer noticed my mistake; but by this time we were very high. My initial reaction was to deploy the boards and steepen the descent in an attempt to get back on profile; however I quickly realized it wasn't going to work and we were unstabilized. I called for a missed approach when we were at roughly 11;000 ft.  Because we went missed early; I called for the autopilot on once we reached the published missed approach altitude of 14;200 ft. This caused confusion as to who should complete the tasks of spinning the heading knob to the 300 heading; swapping the RTU's; and setting the course to 303. I decided to have the First Officer complete these to stay as on-profile as possible. The first two tasks; the 300 heading and swapping the RTU's; were completed however the task of setting the 303 course was not. This led to ATC querying us about our progress on the missed approach and soon after issuing an altitude alert with an instruction to climb to 15;000 ft. By the time we figured out our mistake we were showing full-scale deflection on the Course Deviation Indicator. In addition to not setting the course; I made the mistake of accelerating to 200 kts. and calling climb sequence once level at 14;200 ft. instead of waiting until outbound per the profile. I should also add that the First Officer MFD failed enroute and this most likely caused a reduction in situational awareness throughout the rest of the flight. Failure to comply with the LOC/DME 15 approach profile; especially the missed approach segment.

Report_1_Narrative: While operating a part 135 trip from ZZZZ to ZZZ1 we experienced a runway incursion while departing on runway 14. The possible incursion happened at intersection Lima and runway 14 which is approximately 9000 ft. from the start of the runway. I am also confident that the other aircraft was a Medium Transport. At the time; the ground and tower controllers were operating on separate frequencies by two different people. Once holding short; we advised tower that we were ready for departure to which he responded for us to line up and wait as he had landing traffic a few miles out on runway 10. A few moments later; he cleared us for an immediate departure to which we thought he was trying to get us out before the traffic inbound for 10 landed. From our position on the runway; we could not see that landing traffic and could only go off what he told us earlier. As pilot monitoring; I completed the before takeoff checks and the pilot flying released the brakes and started to advance the throttles. As we rolled down the runway; I made the call of 'airspeed alive both sides;' and at that moment I could see in my peripherals that an aircraft far down the runway was approaching a hold short at intersection Lima. I called out to the PF (Pilot Flying) that there was an aircraft nearing the runway at a rather quick speed on the right side and keep an eye on him if possible. As we passed 80 kts and were quickly approaching V1 the aircraft began crossing the runway I called for an abort. It is hard to recall how long I waited for a response from the PF; but it had to have been about a half a second to a second and at that point I looked down again and we were passed V1. I called 'V1; rotate' and we took off and started the climbout. When we passed the aircraft that crossed; we were a hundred feet or so in the air and they were turning on to taxiway hotel. We climbed out as usual and once above 10;000 ft. the PF engaged the autopilot; and we began talking about the situation. My first question was if he heard me call the abort; to which he replied that he did. He then told me as he was processing everything; he made the decision that a high-speed abort would have been more dangerous as we were just about to rotate; and the incursion aircraft was still almost a mile down the runway from us. We had switched to Center at that point already and I took the second radio to call back Approach and let them know what happened. I informed the controller that we had a possible runway incursion; and that the Medium Transport was just finishing crossing the runway as we had taken off. They said the supervisor was following up with the situation; had no further questions and appreciated the report. When thinking back to the situation we were guessing that the tower controller wanted to get us out before the landing traffic on runway 10 while at the same time the ground controller wanted to get the Medium Transport across the runway before we took off. Due to runway crossing clearances being given on the ground frequency; we have no idea that was happening while we were given our takeoff clearance while on tower. Finally; we talked about the importance of when a decision is made; to verbalize it so that we remain an effective crew. When one pilot makes a decision without informing the other of that decision; especially at high speeds during takeoff; it is imperative that everyone is on the same page. We continued our way to ZZZ1 with no additional issues.The whole situation is complex and looking back it is hard to think of things we could have done differently. Due to operating procedures at the airport; we had no way of expecting that aircraft to cross in front of us down the runway as we executed out expedited departure. It is most important to maintain proper crew coordination and CRM through communication to remain a safe and efficient flight crew.

Minimum Crew Requirements were not followed during a crew change while through the Passengers were onboard.  We had two through Passengers onboard that were continuing on to ZZZ1.  I had a great conversation with them while I was tidying the aircraft.  Our D Flight Attendant was the first Inbound Crew Member to leave the plane.  There was a female Outbound Crew Member that came on to help clean; as well as a COVID-19 Cleaner from the Ramp. A second Outbound Crew Member came onboard as well; he stood in the forward entry area.  I heard the Inbound A Flight Attendant ask him if all of the Outbound Crew Members were at the gate; I guess he said yes because she collected her things walked off the plane.  The COVID-19 Cleaner and the Outbound Crew Member that helped us clean walked off the plane as well.  I also noticed that there was an Above the Wing Supervisor standing in the jet-bridge.  I think he was there because we arrived late and it was an aircraft booked FULL out of ZZZ to ZZZ1.  The two through passengers had moved from the back of the aircraft to the front of the aircraft and sat in row two.  They were seated below the overhead bin where my crew luggage was stowed; and they were asking me if they could use the lavatory.  I brought my things down and thanked the male Outbound Crew Member for stepping onto the plane.  I think that all of my other Crew Members had already walked off the plane; and that I was the last Inbound Crew Member to step off the plane.  This is the part were the COVID-19 Brain Fog comes in.  I'm not sure if the B Flight Attendant or myself was the last Inbound Crew Member to leave the plane.  I do know that there was only one Outbound Crew Member onboard an aircraft with through passengers onboard.  There was a lot going on during the crew change and I didn't think about having two Outbound Crew Members onboard before leaving the aircraft.  Three months ago I had an on the job COVID-19 infection; and I was off work for three months recovering.  I still have a couple of the COVID-19 'Long-haul' symptoms such as Brain Fog and some occasional fatigue.  I should have made sure that two Outbound Crew Members were onboard before I stepped off the plane.

Passenger X was reminded to keep mask on several times. Mr. X continued to pull his mask down and have conversations with other passengers around him right after takeoff after being asked to keep his mask over his nose and mouth. Passenger Y rang call light to inform us Passenger X was not wearing mask. Passenger X was traveling with Passenger Z. Both passengers were served several rounds of wine and asked for more and was told that were out of wine. During pilot break the D FA was blocking and signaled for more line. D FA did not move and told her to wait a second. Passenger X rang call light while D FA was blocking. B FA responded to call light and informed them they were cut off because they would not comply with face mask policy. Passenger Z also came to front galley and asked for more wine after being told we were out. During the pilot break C FA also came up and spoke with Mr. X about mask policy. Passenger X became argumentative and used profanity with C FA. Passenger X was given several warnings and was also issued a [warning] by C FA.Passengers X and Z should be banned from flying on [airline] from this point forward until the mask policy is over. Other passengers should not have to feel unsafe and crew member should to jeopardize their health by having to confront these passengers with their mask off.

Passenger X; [seat] X1; told D FA that he was continuing to order drinks so that he would not have to keep his mask on.  Reminded passenger more than once to keep mask on when not actively eating or drinking.Passenger should be banned from flying on [airline] for the remainder of mask policy because he purposely and knowing disregarded the mask policy.

Passenger Z; [seat] XX1; was ask and reminded several times by C FA and D FA to put his mask on. Passenger would have it down and put it back on when he saw a FA walking by. Passenger was not eating when found with his mask off.

Passenger X in [seat] XX1 brought his own alcohol. When I came by his row for drinks his mask was down; he was sleeping and he had an open can of mixed drink-whiskey and coke. I woke him up-asked him to mask up and asked him if he knew he couldn't bring his own alcohol on board. Mr. X nodded and I apologized but had to confiscate his drink. 3 times he was reminded of his mask and he grudgingly put it back up over his nose each time he was reminded. Upon landing he had his mask completely off and told the D FA that I ruined his trip. Alcohol brought on board and tried to be consumed.Mr. X may need a reminder for his trip back that he needs to comply.

Tower closed pilot to pilot radio. Announced intentions to take off. Another aircraft on short approach did not announce properly and on very short final shouted stop!". Stopped 5 ft. from hold line and turned around. Pilots need to properly announce position and intention in non towered circumstances."

Flight originated in ZZZ as a Part 135 scheduled flight to ZZZ1. There were no passengers on board.  ZZZ Center advised us that ZZZ4 Center would be going to an 'ATC Zero' condition for 1 hour 30 minutes at 15 minutes prior to our scheduled arrival due to a COVID situation in the Center facility. I made the decision to divert to ZZZ3 and accomplished training enroute and landing late afternoon. Weather in ZZZ3 was IFR with a report that fog was believed to be moving into the ZZZ3 area. We refueled and departed for ZZZ1. Training was again accomplished enroute...landing was at night with 2;500 broken; 3;100 overcast and 3 mile in R and BR. This was the end of a long day that started early morning for a departure to ZZZ5 from ZZZ1...scheduled time was 1 hour 50 minutes with a 20 minute turn. Due to waiting for a dead heading crew member we left ZZZ5 one hour late; arriving ZZZ1 at noon. I picked up another FO and departed for ZZZ for a planned 1 hour 40 minute flight. Landing at ZZZ; we then departed for a planned 1 hour 40 minute flight to ZZZ1. As noted previously; we were forced to divert to ZZZ3; due to ATC Zero. I exceeded 8 hours but; felt that; per our FOM and the FARs; I was legal to start and finish because the divert to ZZZ3 was not planned and the scheduled times were within legal limits. Note that I had over 14 hours rest before the flying the following day and was off duty after the days flying was complete and no other flying scheduled for several days.The issue that bothers me is that I was accomplishing training at night and in solid IMC conditions...never again. There was some unspoken pressure to get the training accomplished due to approaching holiday and a lack of aircraft to accomplish the training during daylight hours. I also should not have pressed the flying...the start of the problem was the unplanned ZZZ5 turn due to the scheduled Captain timing out and for whom I was asked to cover. As soon as we knew we would have to divert to ZZZ3; I should have terminated any further training and proceeded to ZZZ1 as soon as ZZZ4 Center re-opened. Several 'takeaways'...I will not allow this to happen to me again; even though I believed (and believe) I was legal...fatigue was not an issue. Training should only be accomplished in solid VMC conditions and preferably in daylight.

While being vectored over the water to the final approach course for TJSJ Runway 10 we noticed a momentary bright green LASER pointed directly at the flight deck from the 1:30 clock code at approximately 8 miles from our location. The source of the green LASER appeared to be from the center of the beach shore line (290 degrees magnetic at 15.8 miles from the SJU VOR at the town of Dorado. We notified ATC (Air Traffic Control) San Juan Approach of the LASER strike; and the Controller responded with words to the effect of; 'Roger; this happens all the time.  Were there any injuries?'  My F/O (First Officer) responded; 'No injuries (that we know of).' This is the second green LASER strike I have experienced while flying into TJSJ; during the past two months. Is there any way we can request that the authorities in San Juan look into these all too frequent green LASER strikes to commercial airline aircraft?The risks are as follows:1. A Pilot's eyes can be injured by the LASER.2. A Pilot may be temporarily blinded resulting in the inability of the Pilot to properly operate the aircraft.3. The LASER strike may cause a momentary distraction which could result in Pilot error.4. More aggressive; continuous LASER energy directed at the flight deck while the aircraft is on short final to landing could result in the Pilot losing situational awareness requiring a missed approach; or worse; loss of aircraft control and a mishap (crash).

Prior to departure; Operations Agent showed me paperwork showing oxygen cylinder onboard as HAZMAT and showed it denoted on the Release that it was carried in Bin A. I ask her if I need to sign said paperwork and she said no; after looking over the document there was no place for PIC (Pilot in Command) signature. The Operations Agent said all she needed was to show me that it was onboard and she keeps the paperwork. We fly to ZZZ1 uneventfully; and finish our trip.

ZZZ Ops Agent notified Captain of HAZMAT for our leg from ZZZ to ZZZ1. Operations Agent brought NOTOC paperwork for the Captain to review. Captain inquired as to whether he was supposed to take paperwork to accompany HAZMAT; Ops Agent responded that she needed to retain the paperwork. Upon landing in ZZZ; the person receiving the HAZMAT inquired as to where the paperwork was. We realized that we should've retained the paperwork to give to the receiving Agent.

Aircraft X called for push and were instructed to give way to Aircraft Y pushing from [an adjacent gate]. As the Aircraft Y was pulling up to the top of the ramp; the Aircraft X pushed in front of the Aircraft Y; cutting the aircraft off. The Aircraft Y had to stop abruptly. Ramp Controller had to make adjustments to the push instructions for both aircraft and also advised the Ramp Services representative in [Operations Control]. The representative will contact the gate supervisor and will advise. Both flights taxied out shortly thereafter without further incident.

When arriving in BOI we were cleared for a Cat 3 approach. The visibility was reported at 1200 RVR with freezing fog and temperature -2. As we were shooting the approach; I elected to fly solid bug even though we had no ice detected. Once we entered the clouds I didn't want to be distracted by a possible Ice Detect message near the ground. But after passing 500 ft. an Appch Warn message appeared so I missed the approach. After the missed approach; I was trying to figure out what was not right and it occurred to me that I didn't have the Ref Speed switch on. So I selected it on and came in for another approach. By this time the weather had gone down to 1000 RVR. We shot the approach and landed. During the rollout the visibility was pretty good and as I was braking and slowing down I plainly saw taxiway E and I made the turn at E. After I had made the turn; tower told us to continue to the end of the runway and turn off at the end. We told them we had already turned off at E. They proceeded to have us cross the left runway at E and continue to parking. It occurred to us that low vis ops were in effect and we should have gone to the end of the runway to follow the lead off lights but tower didn't tell us to go to the end until we had already gotten off at E. The main cause of this was being distracted by the missed approach and trying to figure out why we got the Appch Warn. After we figured it out; the weather had gone down to 1000 RVR and it didn't occur to us about rolling out to the lead off lights. I was more concerned about shooting a successful approach. Another thing that didn't help was that company had shot an approach in front of us and we heard them get off at E. But the weather was 1200 RVR when they landed. I think we were expecting to be able to do the same thing based on what they did. After researching a little we found in the Flight Manual a cautionary note saying that I didn't need to proactively fly at solid bug in anticipation of getting ice. I could've flown open bug if practical and if we did get an ice detect message to not destabilize the approach presumably by increasing speed to solid bug and selecting the Ref Speed switch on when below 500 ft. If I had done this originally I wouldn't have needed to miss the approach the first time. I learned to pay attention to RVR values as they pertain to roll out and taxing; not just landing. 1200 RVR and below is a clue to remember that low vis ops are in effect even if tower doesn't say anything about it and to anticipate looking for lead off lights.

After a lengthy delay of 4 hours in ZZZ1 due to weather and COVID in ZZZ we departed and the flight was normal and uneventful until descent on the ZZZZZ STAR into ZZZ. I was PM; so I was on the radio and ZZZ Center gave us cross ZZZZZ1 [Intersection] at 11;000 and 250 knots. I repeated the clearance then verified it with my Captain who began putting it into the MCDU and set 11;000 in the altitude selector. I glanced down and confirmed and then returned to looking for ice buildup. Within a half mile of ZZZZZ1 ATC called and re-cleared is to descend and maintain 11;000 and maintain 250 knots. I repeated the new clearance and then my Captain and I noticed that our initial clearance for ZZZZZ1 was inadvertently selected on the next fix ZZZZZ2 which is 7 miles from ZZZZZ1 and has 11;000 feet restriction on the STAR. While there was an error I do not believe an FAR was violated because ATC was diligent and noticed we were not going to make the assigned restriction and re-cleared us before the restriction. Furthermore when I apologized to ATC they said no worries have a good night and they shipped us to the next Controller. I believe this could have been avoided if my Captain and I spent an extra second looking at the MCDU before ZZZZZ3 it. I believe factors that played into this were two long legs with a 4 hour delay in ZZZ1. To reiterate I do not believe an FAR or clearance was violated; but it was close so my Captain and I decided to write a report.

Throughout all stages of the flight Passenger X and Passenger Y; kept removing their face masks to talk to each other and their daughter. Passenger X kept her mask below her chin throughout boarding and when I passed by her I told her she needed to keep her mask on. She initially complied but for the rest of the flight she kept pulling her mask down and placing her fingers in her mouth and on her nose. During final compliance checks before landing her mask was down again and she was touching her nose. I told her to pull her mask up again. She refused and said she needed to blow her nose because of an ear issue. I told her she can pull her mask up over her nose and mouth and then pinch her nose over the mask and blow to relieve the pressure. She refused. I told her again that regardless her mask needs to cover her nose and mouth and continued my compliance checks. She ignored me and kept her mask down for landing. The couple did not take the mask policy seriously and ignored my warnings as if they were merely suggestions. They were blatantly non-compliant and they did not require their daughter to wear her mask at any time either.I believe that the couple should be placed on the mask noncompliance list because of their blatant non-compliant behavior and the way they ignored my instructions. There was not time during the flight to issue the warning or to call the pilots to arrange for [customer service] to meet the flight because we were in final descent during sterile cockpit.

Flying direct to ZZZ I had an iPad drop off the side window and I bent down to pick it up. Unfortunately the screen cracked and I attempted to assess the damage as well as re-mount it to the window. As I did this; the aircraft (no autopilot) inadvertently descended into a cloud layer I'd had intentions of passing over (I was within VFR mins above it) and suddenly I was IMC. I had mountainous terrain below; so I couldn't immediately descend; I tried climbing back to 10;500 feet; but at that point was still in the cloud. The airplane being a single engine piston I didn't have extra power to climb much higher so I watched my map closely to ensure I was moving past terrain; then began a descent. I advised ATC because I wanted to be sure I was heard in case something happened (ATC can't talk with me where I was due to rad and radio limitations from terrain). I descended and noticed my windscreen was icing up; and the wings began accumulate icing also. I continued descent until under the cloud layer and was able to safely land aircraft without incident.

We were on a visual approach to Runway XXL at ZZZ at 3;000 feet. Approach cleared us for a visual approach about 7 miles out and we were giving a heading towards the FAF following an airbus. PF set 2;000 feet in the MCP and we began a slow descent heading inbound to the airport. ATC issued a 'low altitude alert' at about 2;300 feet. We leveled off and intercepted the G/S just past the FAF. In addition; we had to rapidly slow since the airbus in front slowed rapidly and ATC wanted us to slow to approximately 150 knots or less. They really did not leave us much lateral room. We slowed and configured while descending. We were never near an obstacle; nor were we a threat to another aircraft. We were still above the 1;000 feet stable approach criteria (1;300 to 1;500 feet) and met the criteria proof to reaching 1;000 feet. We were lower than what was normally flow for that area but still seamed safe. Again on a visual approach. PF stated that he read 2;000 feet at the FAF and misread the altitude. The FAF altitude is 2;800 feet. I did not reverify it since we were visual and I was working on setting up radios and configuration for landing. I assumed he saw it correctly.I can't find where we violated anything. We were a little low for the area but still in a safe position to land and had visual clearance from all obstacles. I know next time to verify the altitude in the MCP from the chart myself. That is that only thing that may have stopped the lower alt in the first place.
Report_2_Narrative: On receiving landing clearance; I set 2;000 feet in the altitude window and planned to descend to that altitude at ZZZZZ; the FAF.  I misread the approach plate. The correct altitude at ZZZZZ is 2;800 feet. The Tower gave us a Low Altitude Alert at 2;200 MSL in VMC and we maintained that altitude (we were nearly at glideslope intercept) until glideslope intercept.  We landed uneventfully.

Report_1_Narrative: During pushback; the tug driver did not use proper terminology. I understand that the contract ramp personnel work many different airlines; but they should have a working knowledge on the aircraft that they are pushing back; as well as some type of standard phraseology and verbiage.Upon completion of the pushback; the tug driver asked me to set the parking brake; which I did; and using proper terminology; I told him 'Brakes Set; Pressure Normal.' A few minutes later; he said to me; 'Have a good flight.' I asked him if the tow bar was disconnected and if the bypass pin had been removed; and he said 'Not yet; I'll tell you when they are.' A few minutes later; he said again; 'Have a good flight' and he disconnected the headset before I could ask him again if the tow bar had been disconnected and if the bypass pin had been removed. I rang the Ground Call button; and I was trying to use hand signals to have the wing walker inform the tug driver that I needed to talk to him on the headset. During this time; I did see the tug backing away from the aircraft; with the tow bar; and a ramp person walking away with the bypass pin in her hand. It appeared that the ramp personnel were finally starting to understand that I was trying to get someone back on the headset; when I gave them a thumbs up signal. The ramp person gave me the thumbs up signal; and they knew we were all set to go. I received the proper Salute; started engines and we taxied away.The tug driver never told me that the tow bar had been disconnected and that the bypass pin had been removed. The tug driver needs to have a working knowledge of the proper terminology to inform the Captain that the towbar has been disconnected and that the bypass pin has been removed; and to wait for the Captain to tell him that it is OK to disconnect the headset; not to just disconnect the headset on his own.Communication is paramount to prevent an accident; and in my opinion; this tug driver did not understand nor use proper verbiage/procedure during the pushback.

On the transition to an ILS Approach. Once we were handed over to Approach we were given different speeds along the approach which is typical. It was IMC with a reported ceiling of 700 ft. We were given 160 kts. and handed over to Tower frequency. The aircraft we were following at the time was 5 miles ahead of us. Approximately 3 miles outside of the final approach fix I saw on the TCAS an aircraft turning a right base and appeared to be sequencing in front of us. Tower shortly after instructed us to slow to approach speed which was 135 kts. We were then told we were following Aircraft Y. The Aircraft Y was approximately 2 miles ahead of us. I told the First Officer to plan on a go-around. I had fully briefed the approach prior to Top of Descent. Tower then asked the Aircraft Y to do a midfield landing due to separation. Around 1;400 ft. we were told to go-around. It was a normal go-around profile and we were vectored back for a normal landing.
Report_2_Narrative: Crew was on approach. Initial ATIS reported ceilings 400 feet and 1/2 mile visibility. Crew elected to transfer controls to the Captain and execute an auto-land approach for currency and to increase safety margin. Approach was commencing normally with positive airspeed control by ATC. Approximately 4 miles from Final Approach Fix ATC vectored an aircraft onto final ahead of us. Aircraft rolled out approximately 2 miles ahead on final as indicated by TCAS. We were handed off to Tower and given clearance to land number two. Preceding aircraft was instructed to expedite turnoff after landing due to close interval. At approximately 800 feet. and at the top of the IMC layer; Tower canceled landing clearance and directed a climb to 2;000 feet and track localizer inbound. Crew conducted a normal go-around. Aircraft was vectored to a second approach and a normal landing ensued.

Report_1_Narrative: During Exterior Preflight inspection it was noted that the Number 2 engine was actively leaking fuel.  Maintenance was consulted and it was determined continued operation was possible providing a successful engine run was completed and the fuel leak had stopped.  Maintenance Control was contacted and approval for an engine run was approved.  I briefed the ramp and maintenance personnel on the logistics of the gate engine run.  The engine run was completed at the gate.  Shortly afterwards we received verification from the Maintenance Technician that we were good as the leak had stopped.  We were informed that the maintenance release would be processed shortly.  Two minutes later we noted on the iPad that the deferral mentioned by the Technician was processed.  As the Number 2 engine was running at the gate and I briefed that we would push as soon as Maintenance said we were good we released the brake and commenced the push.  In-flight we received a message to contact the Dispatch.  After landing a Manager mentioned we may have departed without a maintenance release.  We retained the same plane for the subsequent flight with a valid maintenance release with no maintenance action.
Report_2_Narrative: Pre-flight planning completed and aircraft had valid maintenance release.  Fuel leak found on Number 2 engine during walk around.  Local maintenance consulted.  Technician told us engine can be started and run at the gate and if fuel leak stopped; issue would be put on deferral; and we could just push out and continue.  Received authorization from Maintenance Control for engine run and all personnel were briefed on procedure.  Number 2 engine was started and run at the gate with Technician on the headset. Technician told us that fuel leak stopped after startup so paperwork would be completed immediately; deferral issued and we were good to go.  Flight pushed back very soon after as briefed; taxied out and took off.  Message was received in flight to contact Dispatch management upon landing ZZZ1.  Called and [was] told apparently flight departed without valid maintenance release.  We received a valid maintenance release and flew same aircraft to ZZZ2 without incident.

Report_1_Narrative: While approximately 2 hours from our destination; we discovered that ZZZZ1 weather would not allow us to land at destination. We made a decision with fuel and time to hold and then subsequently divert to ZZZZ2. The communication barrier was difficult with the controllers and while that took place we were also using the radio for Dispatch. I was the PM (Pilot Monitoring) and was coordinating the divert with Dispatch and the handling in ZZZZ2. Task saturation after a long flight was certainly a challenge. I also was having freezing issues with Jepp FD pro. After being handed to ATC; we had the approach and plan in place for the approach Runway XX. On the decent the controllers left us very high even after multiple requests for lower. The PF (Pilot Flying) was using FLCH and max speed brakes most of the time. We were able to get to an appropriate altitude safely. I had a brief moment of thought about still being in FLCH on decent and through saturation; I failed to mention it. Inside the IAF I set the TDZE as normal and confirmed with crew; as we were cleared the approach. We had still been descending constantly from events before; we never really stopped descending since just after the divert was made. As we were in the arc; we were completely configured for landing and I noticed that we had descended below the published altitude on the ARC. I quickly realized we had been still in FLCH and immediately said to stop decent. We were approximately 400-500 ft. Low. And after arresting settled at about 600 below. The PF and crew were in VMC conditions. CAVOK. We were approximately 1/2 to 3/4 of the way through the arc once I noticed. We had the airport in sight and the PF elected to stay at the approximate pattern altitude as we were over water in VMC and had the airport in sight and about to turn final. The approach continued visually and the PF made a normal landing and taxi to hard stand.

I awoke at XA:00 on Date and was called just a few hours later and told I was being placed back on rest; and given a XP:00 assignment. I don't understand how I'm supposed to be able to function; with less than 12 hours to adjust. I attempted to go to sleep at XJ:00 but laid awake till almost XM:00. I was awakened just after XM:00 when my phone received a text alert that my schedule had changed. I have no way to silence text alerts without also silencing all phone calls. I attempted to go back to sleep but was stressed that I would sleep through the call and could never fall back to sleep. Scheduling finally called me at XR:00; giving me absolute minimum notice to show; when I was forced to call in fatigued. I understand that this type of assignment is currently legal on paper; it shouldn't be; but I have to tell you that I flew transatlantic flights for 8 years and was never as fatigued as I have been on Reserve under this domestic FAR 117 rules. This is ridiculous. This is the second time in only 10 days that I was switched from day to night duty with less than 12 hours notice and after being awake for less than 5-6 hours. I want to make our system work so that the flights can be covered; but this current system is a system of 'gamesmanship'. No one is on the same team. Why don't we have a system where everyone wants the system to work to get the planes and passengers where they need to be? It just seems like it has become a war between the company and the union and not a great place to work anymore.

In cruise I messaged Dispatch and asked the RVR for [Runway] XXR.  They told me 1400 for [Runway] XXR.  About 30 minutes from arrival we started our brief. We briefed the arrival and the approach plate for [Runway]. We then briefed the CAT2 checklist. We both put 705 feet in our DH and verified it. We did the decent check and verified it again. Did the approach check and was getting vectors to [Runway] XXR. Gear down flaps 45 before the marker.  I called 1;000 feet cleared to land when I knew something was wrong. I couldn't grasp why we only had 300 feet left to go missed. Told the First Officer something wasn't right. We hit minimums at 705 feet AGL and I called the missed. Did the missed approach; cleaned up aircraft and did the checklist. Didn't take me long to figure out what I did. The RA for [Runway] XXR is 116 feet. We put the new DH in and took our time to get set up for approach.  We got vectors on the LOC and shot the approach to a landing.We both missed the right number for the DH. I knew the approach would be to minimums so I was looking at chart in cruise so I wouldn't miss anything.  Ended up on a go around at 700 feet AGL because we had the MDA in. It was first flight of day we both felt good. Just the low visibility at destination caused a threat.Even after the lengthy briefing and checklist usage the DH was still put in there wrong. What I have learned over my career is not to get upset at the moment you realize you did something wrong.  Great training over the years and good CRM has trained me to fly the airplane. Run the appropriate checklists and then discuss what happened at a safe time later.
Report_2_Narrative: We incorrectly set the 705 feet in the DH for a CAT 2 approach and logically got the mins aural alert more than 500 feet early. We executed a go-around; set up the DH properly and flew the second approach normally.

Report_1_Narrative: I was the AFA (A Flight Attendant). Every part of the flight (boarding; climb; cruise; etc.) seemed to be going fine and seemed standard. When the pilots told us to prepare the cabin for arrival; I decided to look over the PAX (Passenger) manifest before tossing it. I had used it during the flight but only looked at part of it. To be honest; I didn't read over it before we departed. When I read over it toward the end of the flight; I noticed that one PAX was listed as a Special Assistance Passenger. This PAX was listed as needing a wheelchair and as blind. At no point was I aware that we had a PAX with a visual impairment. The PAX was boarded first and the person who pushed his wheelchair stowed the PAX's luggage. When interacting with the PAX on boarding; I handed him a sanitizing wipe and he seemed to have no trouble getting the wipe from me or getting to his seat; although I didn't really watch him go to his seat. At no point did the gate agent inform us that we had a PAX with a visual impairment nor did the person who pushed the wheelchair. When the B Flight Attendant asked the gate agent if we had any specials (for the PAX count form) the gate agent said we had one wheelchair. There was no mention of a PAX with a visual impairment.We did the standard briefings (announcements and safety demo); but neither of us provided a one-on-one briefing with the PAX. We were unaware of his visual impairment. After I realized our mistake on descent; I wasn't sure what to do. I had a brief conversation with the PAX; and he seemed to look me in the eye for the conversation. After I played the final descent announcement; I showed the B Flight Attendant the manifest and how it showed a PAX listed as blind. She was surprised to see it. We discussed our situation in regard to the PAX and the possibility of writing a  report.After we landed; the PAX gathered his belongings and deplaned without help. He also told the person waiting with the wheelchair that he didn't need it. He was traveling alone and without the assistance of a service animal. He only used a simple walking cane; not a red and white cane frequently used by people with visual impairments. He gave me no reason to think that he was visually impaired; but I understand that some disabilities are not apparent. When all the PAX had deplaned; I informed the Captain of what had happened and asked for advice. He said to fill out an report because we didn't do a required briefing. The B Flight Attendant and I knew that one-on-one briefings are required; we didn't know the PAX was visually impaired.I am concerned and upset that we potentially caused a safety incident with a PAX with a visual impairment. This is unacceptable. I hope to use this as a learning experience and pay closer attention to the PAX manifest before shutting the boarding door and proactively asking all gate agents about special assist PAX that we may have onboard. I say this with caution; though. We don't always receive the correct manifest; we sometimes receive incomplete manifests with no PAX information; and sometimes we don't even receive a manifest at all. While in the end; it was my responsibility to confirm with the gate agent about special assist PAX; I feel like there was a breakdown of communication between the gate agent and me. I wonder if the gate agent knew there was a visually impaired PAX on the flight. Perhaps there could be strengthened training for gate agents and flight attendants for recognizing special assist PAX. For flight attendants; also what to do if a special assist PAX is discovered during flight and certain briefings did not occur; as in our case. As stated before; I didn't know what to do in regard to the PAX.I understand the severe safety hazards not providing a one-on-one briefing could have caused if something went wrong during flight. I hope to use this as a lesson learned and not solely rely on being informed of all special assist PAX by a gate agent. In other words; I need to be more proactive; and I need to use my resources more wisely. My hope is that this can be used as a lesson for all flight attendants so that incidents like this don't happen again. We are first and foremost a safety industry; and we failed to keep to our safety standards.

While executing the ILS to [Runway] XXR at ZZZ; ATC informed us that we were following a heavy and instructed us to slow to 170kts. The Captain and I saw the other aircraft on TCAS and judged the distance to be six or seven miles. We discussed the possibility of wake turbulence but we didn't think the spacing was an issue. Then; after switching the tower; at around 3;000 feet; we were instructed to start slowing to our final approach speed. We then configured the aircraft for landing and started running the landing checklist. While listening and responding to the Captain's checklist callouts; I thought I heard someone say 'approach clearance canceled; maintain 3;000'; but I was unsure of the call sign. This was partly because the Captain and I were in the middle of running a checklist and partly because there was another aircraft on frequency that had a very similar call sign. We were Company X Callsign ABBC and the aircraft ahead of the heavy was Company X Callsign ADDC (there were at least three instances during the arrival where an instruction was given and we weren't 100% sure who it was for). I interrupted the Captain and told him that I thought tower had just canceled someone's approach clearance and asked him to query ATC to see if it was for us. The controller responded with a very urgent tone and said 'That is affirm; Callsign ABBC your approach clearance is canceled; maintain present heading and climb to 3;000. I'll have a turn for you in just a second.' As the captain was responding to ATC; I pressed the heading button to maintain our present heading as instructed; but I was confused as to what to do next. At this point we were around 2;400 feet (about 2;000 feet AFE); so a hard go-around using the TO/GA button was not required. I was not really mentally prepared for the go-around and was having trouble deciding what to do next; but ATC sounded like they wanted me to start climbing immediately to 3;000 feet; which was already set in the altitude window; so I reacted to his instruction by hitting FLCH. This caused the aircraft to apply full throttle and pitch up to maintain our current approach speed of about 145kts. We maintain this climb for several seconds until I was able to dial the speed up and get the aircraft accelerating. With everything happening so fast; and not really being mentally prepared for a go-around; I called gear up while the flaps were still at 5 which caused the landing gear warning to sound. It was a very sloppy go-around but I don't think the aircraft was ever in an unsafe state.This was my first go-around in the actual aircraft. However; I have mentally rehearsed and practiced enough hard go-arounds in sim training that the procedure is pretty much automatic and I can execute it without much thinking required. However; I can only recall doing a soft go-around once during FTD training and; as I recall; in that scenario we were not yet configured for landing and the altitude assigned was below us; so it was a simple matter of stopping the descent and flying the assigned heading. We also discussed soft go-arounds vs hard go-arounds in training and I had recalled that using TO/GA was optional but not really preferred during a soft-go around. I think there needs to be more training on soft go-arounds and the SOP should give more guidance as to preferred methods in various scenarios. Currently; the SOP does not give much guidance on the subject other than reminding the pilot to retract flaps at the F-bug or XX knots above green dot. After landing; the Captain and I discussed what I could have done better. He suggested; and I agreed; that the best course of action would have been to press ALT to stop the descent and then start accelerating before starting our climb. However; after replaying it in my head all night; I came to the conclusion that; given the fact that ATC wanted me to climb; it would have been perfectly acceptable to switch to heading mode and then press TO/GA and execute my normalgo-around actions and callouts; even though we were well above 1;000 feet AFE. This would ensure the aircraft was maintaining a safe speed while we began to reconfigure the aircraft and would have eliminated a lot of confusion that I created while I pondered what technique I should use to get us to 3;000 feet while also cleaning up the aircraft. Perhaps this should be the preferred method to execute a soft-go around when fully configured for landing and a climb is required. But perhaps there's another aspect of it that I'm not considering. The technique of pressing ALT and accelerating aircraft prior to starting the climb also seems like it would work in most situations. That's why I think the SOP should give more guidance on the subject. If there were a preferred method spelled out in the SOP; then it would eliminate a lot of the confusion and both pilots would be on the same page instead of debating on a technique for a procedure that is rarely used when there isn't really time to stop and think about it.This experience has also taught me to be prepared for a go-around at all times; not just when below 1;000 feet on an approach. Although; the spacing behind the heavy looked good to us; we should have been more prepared for the possibility that ATC would call for a go-around unexpectedly.

I was working the FWD cabin and Flight Attendant (FA) B was working the AFT. During the flight FA B approached the galley and states to me that she was going to give Passenger X a face mask warning; because he was having a hard time complying to keeping the mask over his nose and she asked on numerous occasions before pushback; taxing out to the runway; and during the flight to keep his mask over his nose. I called flight deck to inform the Captain what was about to happen. So FA B gave the written policy to Passenger X and he stated he didn't want to see it. FA B informs me what happened and I call the flight deck. I tell the Captain she gave the warning and Passenger X refuses to hear it. The Captain states we'll just call for supervisors to meet us at the gate. FA B and I both say 'okay.' I decide to do a walk through in the cabin and notice Passenger X has his mask on covering his nose and mouth. I inform FA B and the Captain. Captain states that's great; but so there's no further issues with Passenger X we'll still have the Supervisor meet us. I say 'fine.' I guess noticing that FA B and myself are talking to the Captain inflight; Passenger X states to FA B he now wants to read the policy. Once seeing the policy and reading it Passenger X states to FA B that he's sorry. FA B then calls the Captain to let him know Passenger X has a change of heart. Captain still states he'll have the Supervisor meet the aircraft at the gate. We say 'okay.' Once at the gate; I open the main door and the supervisors are there. They wait for Passenger X to deplane to talk to him. The supervisors also state they will inform his connecting flight.

I was in contact with Chicago Center on our flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1 on a direct flight plan at FL290. We were talking with Chicago Center when he transferred us to another Chicago Center frequency. I tried 3 times to contact the Controller as he was quite busy. The third time he said 'unless it is pertinent to the safety of flight; maintain radio silence.' I complied with his instructions. During this time I was working on other items pertaining to the flight. Some time had passed and I realized I have not talked to a controller in some time and the radio calls I was hearing were getting fainter. I called the Controller and asked if I needed to be transferred and was told that he did not have me on his list and was unsure of what frequency I needed to contact. I tried to go back to the previous frequency I was on but was not getting what I needed there. I then looked at my route and found that the closest larger airport was ZZZ2 and called their Controller asking for a correct frequency for Center and he asked for my position; squawk code; and tail number again. I gave him the information he requested and afterwards he recommended a new frequency to me. I contacted the Controller on the recommended frequency I was given and he was also unsure of who I was. He requested our altitude; tail number; and squawk code. I complied and he advised I contact the next control frequency he had. This next Controller was Cleveland Center. He seemed to have somewhat of a better idea of who we were but recommended the next Controller on Cleveland Center as well on a new frequency. As soon as I contacted this new Controller he knew who I was and kept me on the same altitude and course I had been on. Again; we were direct from ZZZ to ZZZ1 at FL290 and had not modified our course and ATC had not advised us of any course or altitude changes. Before we were switched to Potomac Center nearing our destination I was asked to take down a number and call it on the ground for a 'possible pilot deviation.' I complied and we were able to continue and land without any further incident. On the ground I called the number I was given and talked to ATC about the situation. They took my information and we talked about what happened. They advised me that in the future it is always a good idea to go back to your previous frequency if needed. I agreed with their advice and they said that I may be contacted in the near future about this incident. I said that is no problem and am more than willing to talk to whomever about it.To correct situations like this in the future I believe there are a couple things that can happen. For myself as a pilot that if I am ever unsure of who I need to be talking to that I can check in more often and make sure that we are all on the same page. The suggestion I would offer for the controller side would be that if anyone is in a NORDO aircraft situation that all the controllers in the area know of that tail number and are able to direct the aircraft to the correct frequency quickly rather than searching around.

I was notified about a report of a wrong surface landing. I was the Controller on the midnight shift.  I cleared Aircraft X for Runway XXC; but the Aircraft landed Runway XXR. Approach switched the aircraft on an extended base for the visual approach [to] Runway XXC. The pilot checked on the frequency for Runway XXC. I cleared the aircraft to land for Runway XXC. After I cleared the plane to land the janitorial guy comes to the Tower cab to vacuum the Tower and empty the trash. I usually go opposite of him so we can keep our space (we had our mask on) for social distancing. When he finished cleaning the Local 3 position I recognized the aircraft was short final to Runway XXR. I knew the runway had no vehicles on it because my strip bay showed no vehicle above Runway XXR bay header. I let the aircraft land Runway XXR due to the critical phase in flight. I taxied the aircraft to the ramp after the aircraft landed safely. In the past Approach would call me on the shout line to remind me that this aircraft is set up on the wrong approach. By me not being at the position walking around the Tower cab I did not recognize the aircraft on approach for the wrong runway.The recommendations I would use to prevent this wrong surface landing is to observe an aircraft turn onto the right approach for the right runway. If that pilot makes a mistake turning on to the wrong approach I can either send the aircraft around or correct the aircraft to land on the proper runway; or I can help the pilot get visual for the runway the pilot is lined up for and clear the plane to land for that runway. The key is catching the aircraft turning on the approach early enough so I can correct the pilot.

Climbing out on the ZZZZZ2 RNAV Departure off [Runway] XX in ZZZ. We did a precautionary takeoff for LLWS reported on the ATIS. It was a light aircraft. We climbed at a high rate and got distracted with doing the Climb Checklist. Also we were accelerating to 250 knots; which masked the level off prompt on the PF FD display. We did not honor the 10;000 foot hold-down at ZZZZZ Intersection. The PF was hand flying and climbed to 10;400 feet before noticing the hold-down symbol on the MFD maps display route. The PF immediately began a descent to 10;000 feet. Upon which ATC queried if we were climbing via the ZZZZZ2 Departure. We acknowledged our mistake and corrected.Enhanced pilot monitoring; higher situational awareness climbing with a light aircraft and notice that things are moving fast and hold off on Climb Checklist until higher altitude with autopilot on [would help prevent this type of event].
Report_2_Narrative: Normal departure and climbout. The Pilot Monitoring was slightly task saturated due to altitude approaching 10;000 feet; frequency change and checklist. The Pilot Flying failed to level off at an intermediate altitude restriction on SID.  The Pilot Flying didn't follow proper flight director guidance while hand flying the aircraft and could have used a higher level of automation. The aircraft climbed to 10;400 feet and immediately descended back to 10;000 feet when the Pilot Monitoring inquired and started to intervene. ATC asked to verify our climb clearance and stated the importance of the altitude restriction and said no other traffic was affected by our deviation.Use the highest level of automation to increase your situational awareness.

Report_1_Narrative: Passenger X in seat XX3 would not follow crew member instructions to wear her face covering at the appropriate times and manner.

Notified by Duty Officer after flight completion; that 243 kg of dry ice had been loaded into Position 13C without documentation. No performance limitations were exceeded according to ZZZ1 Operations. Unable to determine cause.I suggest two person independent verification procedures.

Boarding the flight from ZZZ-ZZZ1 half of the passengers on board either had their mask down or half down from their nose. We asked them several times as well as made all the announcements and I notified the Captain and the Gate Agent. The Captain made several announcements after the Agent brought down the flights designated Supervisor. The Supervisor proceeded to make an announcements to please follow the mask policy. They had to follow the company's mask policy or potentially be removed from the flight. Passengers complied but soon after we made a walk through; all the above passengers had their mask off; completely removed and not even visible. I notified the Captain and he told me to the write down seat numbers. Upon arrival he would hand it to the Station Managers. ZZZ1 Supervisors and Gate Agents were informed and said thanks for our great work and professionalism. They will take care of the incident with the Station Manager.

After going to the bathroom to clean my teeth; for a split moment; I forgot to put my mask back on.  I realized it quickly and put it back.  I don't think anybody realized it as most passengers were resting. It was a human mistake and I corrected it right away. It was a moment of forgetfulness.

I was pilot monitoring on the initial climbout from ZZZ when I noticed my FO (First Officer) was nose high and banking to the right approximately 40 degrees with airspeed decreasing from 95 KTS to 85 KTS. I asked why he was turning as were supposed to be climbing left to ZZZZZ. He seemed confused by my question and I realized that he had lost situational awareness. I declared 'My Plane' at which point he gave a positive transfer of controls. I leveled the wings and regained a positive rate of climb before turning back onto course. After regaining stable flight I offered the controls back to my FO. He refused controls and I continued to fly. I told him he had the radios and told him to check in with departure. Center asked us why we had turned to the right off of our expected heading and my FO was honest with the Controller; informing him that he had lost control of the aircraft and heading had diverged prior to me regaining control of the aircraft. The Controller acknowledged and told us to climb according to our planned route. After reaching cruising altitude I debriefed my FO and we discussed what went wrong. We ultimately determined that the loss of control was due to deficiencies in his scan as a result of his inexperience with analog instruments.

I was soloing a new student pilot at ZZZ. After completing several warm-up laps in the pattern dual; I informed Tower that my student was about to solo and had my student drop me off in the non-movement area of the east ramp. On my student's first attempt at a landing; he flared high and initiated a go-around. On his second attempt he landed; stopped; and then initiated a takeoff roll. I realized that my student had mistaken the command 'Cleared to Land' for 'Cleared Stop-And-Go.' I used a handheld radio to call out my student by name and told him to discontinue the takeoff. He stopped on the runway; and he was switched to Ground for taxi instructions. I asked Ground to instruct my student to return to my location and discontinue the flight. While taxiing back to my location on south end of the east ramp; my student missed a left turn from A to F; stopped; and was directed by Ground to complete a 180 turn and return to his original taxi instructions.
