If had continued forward there was the possibility of crossing Runway XX-XY and committing a runway incursion.There were several contributing factors to both of these new runway incursions/violations.1) My student's confusion about ATC clearances. We had previously been issued 'stop-and-go' and 'cleared to land' clearances earlier that morning; but I did not confirm that my student understood the difference.2) Poor Aeronautical Decision-Making: My student informed me after the flight that he had only slept four hours the night before. I believe this contributed to both errors he made during his solo.3) Unfamiliarity/Lack of Training on Towered Airport Operations: We had one previous flight at ZZZ; but it did not involve extensive taxi instructions or various ATC clearances. Even that morning my student had the same repetitive taxi clearance after each of our landings completed together.Corrective Actions (To be completed with this student and future students).1) My student and I are going to have an extensive ground covering various ATC clearances; communication procedures; and possible taxi instructions at unfamiliar airports. I will require my student to complete a FAASafety.gov course on runway incursions.2) While I have my students use an ADM (Aeronautical Decision Making) checklist before each flight; I will audibly confirm the 'IMSAFE' checklist is complete before each flight.3) We will conduct another dual flight or two and ask for various different taxi instructions to diversify my student's experience.
Report_2_Narrative: I made a call on the downwind 'Full Stop Touch and Go' and was cleared for landing. Did not correctly understand that Cleared For Landing does not allow for 'Touch and Go' unless given that prompt. Also missed a taxi-way.Not properly understanding 'Cleared for Landing' in full. After given Instructor not to take off; I missed my taxi-way; thus entering a different taxiway from Instructor. Unfamiliar with map + going too fast; missed the taxiway. Ultimately; challenged with receiving tower communications at their rate of communications.My Instructor realized I was not stopping and told me to not take off. I saw that I was about to cross a runway and realized I was missing something. I realized it and then was given Instructor to 180. My Instructor told me to not take off so I didn't take off. We exited the lesson and went back to our home airport. Need to slow down on taxiways. Mostly just incorrect decisions on incorrect understanding of definition 'cleared to land'. Will role-play / simulate communications and different scenarios to ensure I'm understanding and communicating properly with the tower.

Report_1_Narrative: Aircraft X was approved direct the initial fix for the RNAV approach. I forgot about approving direct to the initial fix; so the aircraft was farther east than I expected. I descended the aircraft to 3000 feet to get him below VFR traffic in his path; and he descended into a 3300 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude area. The handoff controller brought it to my attention; and I issued a turn away from the antenna. The aircraft had already descended below the safe altitude. I need to be more attentive and project flight path before issuing control instructions.

During the arrival; we were given a vector and then a direct to DSNEE. As we were still in the high teens; I suggested opening the speed window at 280 KIAS (assigned speed) and then spinning it down below 250 passing around 12-13000' (due to the Captain's relative inexperience). Sometime below 15000'; SoCal Arrival asked if we wanted the SNA 20R RNAV Z. We had already setup and briefed for the ILS but we discussed any benefits to switching and decided against it. I informed SoCal of our desire for the ILS and crosschecked my instruments and noticed we were descending through approximately 8700' still doing 280 KIAS. I gave a corrective command and immediately pressed ALT HOLD (Altitude Hold); spun back the speed to below 250 KIAS; and deployed the speed brakes. I did not say; 'My Aircraft' but time was of the essence. We slowed and continued the arrival and landing uneventfully: there was no communication from SoCal referencing the deviation.Using SPD INTV (Speed Intervene); without its protection; coupled with the distraction of SoCal's request set us up for the deviation. Additionally; the Captain is new to both the seat and the airplane.[I] suggest using all available automation to the maximum extent. We could have instead entered speeds into the CDU at the abeam waypoints and allowed the aircraft to gradually decelerate on its own (or the DESCENT page speed).
Report_2_Narrative: I was flying. Received a vector off the arrival. Chose to use speed intervene; 280kts. Just before I went to reduce speed to 250kts for 10;000; ATC asked if we wanted the Required Navigation Performance (RNP) Z. We had set up and briefed the ILS as I had been refused the RNP Z on a prior flight. First Officer (FO) and I got distracted discussing the pros and cons of accepting the RNP Z; descended below 10;000 ft. doing 280kt. The FO caught it. We leveled off; used speed brakes; got below 250kts; and continued decent. I am a new Captain. About 150 hours on the 737; which I haven't flown. I haven't flown in the last 2 months. These factors mainly contributed to a narrowing of the big picture. Additionally; I was tired. It was night. Last leg of 3 day pairing. More than anything; don't use speed intervene without a lot of extra caution. Putting the speed in the FMC is the better way to go; it only takes a couple of seconds longer.

Report_1_Narrative: Better monitor FO actions. Increase awareness of calculating descent points using distance from the runway to approximate a 3 degree descent path to the runway in order to avoid unnecessary and lengthy low level segments.  Flying at 3;000 feet about 15 miles WSW of IND direct to BIGRD on the RNAV Y 5R. Approach asked if we had the field in sight; we responded that we did; and then we were given a left turn pretty much direct to HUUPS; which is the final approach fix on the RNAV Y 5R; and cleared for the visual approach. Tower then handed us off to Tower. During this time while I was switching to Tower I became task saturated with my PM flying duties from the frequency change; getting a landing clearance; and the FO requesting flap configuration changes. After we were cleared to land I noticed we were just about to level off at 2;100 feet. Well below a 3 degree descent path given our current position to the runway. I had not noticed the FO had set 2;100 feet; which is the altitude at HUUPS; and started a descent as soon as we were cleared for the approach. We leveled off at 2;100 feet and continued for some time before reaching HUUPS and continuing the descent for the runway. We were level at approximately 1;300' feet AGL for a while before descending for landing. I don't think this was technically illegal or in violation of an FAR; as we were cleared for the visual and in the process of making a descent for landing; but I do feel it's not a good technique to simply set the FAF altitude; dive and drive to that altitude; level off for several miles; then continue descent once over the FAF. We were not on a published portion of an approach; and well below a 3 degree descent path to the runway. This wasn't an issue for this particular approach; but it could become an issue at night in mountainous terrain or if there are obstacles in the area.

I was flying with a student pilot; practicing simulated engine failure emergency procedures. Student selected Frazier Lake airport (1C9) as the landing point. We positioned the airplane directly towards 1C9 for runway 5 which is a grass runway. I scanned the area in front and right of us to make sure there was no other traffic nearby. We descended towards runway 5 and ended the practice by going around above runway 5 at about 800 ft AGL. The next day we received a phone call from another pilot who was operating in traffic pattern at 1C9 attempting to land on the hard surface taxiway parallel to runway 5. He told us that he saw us on final flying next to him only approximately 200 ft above. He made few radio calls trying to reach us but no response; because we were not on 1C9 CTAF at the time. What have caused this event was that the student was busy performing the maneuver; forgot to change frequency to 1C9 CTAF and announce our intention. I was too focused on the training but overlooked the safety aspect of the flight. The corrective action is I should have changed frequency to 1C9 CTAF and monitor it when the student decided to head over there.

SNA Tower cleared us for takeoff on 20R but changed our clearance altitude from 5;000 feet to 3;000 feet for crossing traffic. We were very light and the max power setting gave us a very very high climb rate. We had a LEVEL OFF RA at 2;800 feet MSL which was the easy part. We complied and did not go above 3;000 feet as a light aircraft passed directly overhead. I believe the separation was 400 feet. Complicating this situation; and the real reason for this report; was the fact that we were given a 3;000 feet clearance and our performance was so high; we got ALT ACQ around 1;000 feet. Our automation did not provide proper guidance as the speed reverted to flaps up speed (15) and the autothrottles wanted to go to max power (takeoff power 100.6%). Also; the cutback N1 was 26% less than takeoff power! My FO had to disconnect the Autothrottles to prevent them from surging back to max power after he set them to cutback and flight directors were commanding a climb well above 10-15 degrees even though my speed was below the shark tooth. I had to fly out of the situation and my FO gave great communication as he kept our SA in focus. I am not 100% sure what happened with the automation but I believe the ALT ACQ at such a low altitude caused some serious issues. This would not have happened with a normal 5;000 feet clearance; or if we were heavy with less performance or we were doing a normal takeoff profile.However; I am concerned that with a few more variables or a crew that did not work as effectively as we did today this could have been a much worse outcome. I am wondering if we should be given 3;000 feet clearances with crossing traffic. With a normal takeoff profile it would have been fine but the max power setting and with the large power cutback for noise abatement this was a tricky situation. Compliments to my FO who took action on the autothrottles to prevent an automated max thrust climb after he pulled for cutback by disengaging them and clearly communicating that part of the problem.

Early in the boarding process the Purser notified me of a family that was being defiant about wearing masks in compliance with company policy.  After speaking with the family; the Purser still felt uneasy about the family. I then asked for a Customer Service Supervisor to meet the airplane.  While discussing the issue with the supervisors and FAs; a separate family came forward to note their concern over the behavior of the first family. Two of the second family quickly began tearing up.  Additionally; there was a passenger in the vicinity of the problem family who had health issues. At this point; I decided to have the problem family removed.

On pushback the towbar broke. When I heard the noise I looked down and saw that the brakes were still set. I had failed to verify that they were released. Fortunately; maintenance found no damage on the inspection. The flight continued normally after we received a new release.There were numerous contributing factors that led to this place. This was my first international trip in quite some time. There were many issues related to COVID-19 and catering. Chief of which was no ice being available. I tried to coordinate with the station to get some even asking if the terminal restaurants had some they would spare. No luck. There was also a discussion between myself and the First Officer regarding the rarely used method to obtain the ATC clearance in ZZZZ.The First Officer said he wanted to get home early and I believe he initiated the call to ground for the push before I was ready. I hadn't yet removed my mask. I did not have my audio panel properly configured and my mask was blocking my view of those switches. Ground Control gave us a push clearance then added a last minute query as to which runway we wanted which the First Officer and I discussed and verified.I either missed the pushback crew saying they were ready or they did not initiate the standard sequence so I had to verify that they were ready. Ground then told us to hold our push and that stopped me from releasing the brakes at that point when I normally might have done so. There was also confusion with the pushback location. I could not locate it on the charts. We were also using a gate in a part of the terminal neither myself or the First Officer had been at before.As I was re-configuring my audio panel my watch flashed a [personal message.] Although I did not read it; I noticed it. That took my out of my routine and although I felt my legs relax after I thought I hit the pedals to release the brakes; they were still set.Although I have been with the company many years; I have also not been flying much lately; 4 legs in the last 90 days or so. I also don't know why my watch flashed a message when my phone was off.In the end it was a failure to verify the status of the parking brakes. I will redouble my efforts to ensure a verification that the brakes indicate they are released in the appropriate sequence going forward.

When we were turning into the parking space on hardstand; the ground personnel had the wrong lead in sign lit up; and that is the one we lined up with.  The Captain did a good job and was taxiing very slow cause it didn't look 100 percent; but the ground personnel just stood and watched us.  This led us to believe that we were parking where they wanted us to.  As we moved closer; a bus on the left side looked too close (but was behind the line) and that is when the Captain stopped the plane to wait for ground personnel to double check.  At that point they realized we were not on the line to the right where they actually wanted us.  A tug was brought over to push us back and line us up with the correct line and lead-in sign.  Even when we docked; the correct taxi-in guidance was never turned on.

The Captain briefed that he would fly the ZZZ ILS 27R. On the arrival abeam the FAF we called the airport in sight and were subsequently cleared #1 for the visual approach. In the turn towards the final approach I lost sight of the runway. I was expecting the Captain to maneuver the aircraft to align the airplane with the approach course prior to; or at; the FAF. He turned well inside the FAF to intercept a 3 mile final. I was not expecting this and felt behind the aircraft. While on the base leg we received a radio call from Tower 'low altitude alert; below minimum vectoring altitude.' This left me even more uneasy during the approach. I expressed my concern to the Captain. The Captain was able to re-cage my situational awareness and continued the visual approach. We rolled out on final; stabilized on glide path and made an uneventful landing. We thoroughly debriefed the flight; with much attention on the approach.
Report_2_Narrative: The FO (First Officer) had never been into ZZZ; and I hadn't been there for years. Briefing the approach; I handled the terrain" item by saying something silly like "I don't know of any." In fact there was no high terrain; but there were close-in obstacles. I mentioned that we might well get a slam dunk visual but I did not communicate to the FO my idea of normally being comfortable joining the final on visual approaches inside the FAF; about 3 miles out at 1;000 feet AGL. If I had done that fuller briefing; he would not have been surprised when I later pointed the aircraft inside the FAF. Descending on the arrival; we noted that the procedure put us on a long final for the ILS to 27R; but we both got the impression that we would be #1 to the airport and slam dunked. On downwind abeam the airport we were descended to 3;000 feet and then cleared for the visual approach. I headed over toward the ILS FAF and descended toward its crossing altitude; 2;100 feet; while I evaluated the position and energy. I saw that we were in a position to join a 3-mile final in a stabilized condition. Focusing visually on the right base leg and the airport; we were at about 1;700 feet on about a three mile base. At this time ATC transmitted; "Low altitude alert; minimum vectoring altitude is 2;100 feet." Our path looked clear ahead and on final to the right; and I did not consider ATC's radar vectoring altitude to exactly apply to our operation. Still; they were trying to tell us something! I looked to the left and saw downtown; laterally closer than desired but certainly more than 2;000 feet horizontally. I shallowed the descent as much as I could without going too high to be stabilized on final. We joined the final and landed normally.By inadequately studying and briefing the airport's visual flying environment; I did a night visual approach that was safe but that (1) concerned ATC; (2) concerned my flying partner; and (3) worst of all; surprised me about our proximity to downtown because I had not identified the high buildings as a threat."

Report_1_Narrative: Aircraft X had been blocked out for 12 mins at the time when they received an ACARS from load planning stating that 'Captain we have a ramp loading error with DG and need you to return to the gate. Zooming in on ZZZ in [Software] I could see Aircraft X was taking the runway. I immediately called the tower and asked them to tell him to come back to the gate as well as copy and pasted the message from load planning to him in another ACARS message. At that point it seemed like he had got the message and turned off the runway. The Load Planner also called me to inform me of the situation but at this point the plane had already turned off the runway. The issue is not load planning; the issue is ramp. This was a ramp directed loading flight and load planners didn't receive the information until it was almost too late. If the crew missed the ACARS or maybe if it were to have been rejected; they could have been airborne with the DG loaded improperly. Not only is this incredibly dangerous but happens entirely too often."

While on vectors for the ILS to Runway 33 at BTV; ATC cleared aircraft to descend and maintain 5;500 feet. Captain had TERRAIN display set on his MFD. While level at 5;500 feet the EGPWS aural annunciation TERRAIN PULL UP sounded. Flight crew disconnected the autopilot and initiated a climb. ATC was notified and once the warning was clear the flight returned to the original assigned altitude of 5;500 feet. The flight continued without further incident.The rising terrain while in level flight initiated the EGPWS annunciation. Flight crew immediately disconnected the autopilot and initiated a climb. Continued adherence to policy and procedures and respect for the EGPWS.

Inbound to ZZZZ on ZZZZZ Star.  Taken off STAR early and cleared direct to ZZZZ1 VOR.  We were instantly 1;800 feet above the path.  Proactively used speed brakes and planned to slow and extend flaps when below 20;000 feet; but were then told to keep speed at 250 KTS.  Lowered landing gear shortly after passing ZZZZ1 to get back on profile.  Was able to regain the VNAV path just prior to ZZZZZ1 and at Vref +10 per procedure.  Flaps were at 25. Frequency was very busy and sky hazy. Very late hand off to Tower.  Localizer did not correctly ID until just prior to FAF.  FO was very busy with frequency changes; configuration changes and checklist.  Tower did not clear us to land; but instructed us to continue as an aircraft was in position on our Runway; XXR. Another aircraft was holding short of XXR waiting to cross.  At 1;000 feet I was at approach speed and called 'stable;' but was distracted by no landing clearance and aircraft on runway.  Aircraft in position was cleared for immediate takeoff; we were cleared to land; and other aircraft was given clearance to cross XXR.  Departing aircraft was slow to accelerate.  Passing 500 feet FO made 'stable' call.  Shortly after we received an aural alert indicating we did not have landing flaps selected.  Flaps indicator showed flaps at 25 degrees verses the planned and briefed of 30 degrees.  I realized I had not asked for flaps 30 and was now below 400 feet. Elected not to make a configuration change to 30 degrees below 1;000 feet per procedure.  Hesitant to go-around per procedure because I wasn't sure if the crossing aircraft had not just departed the inboard parallel Runway XXL; and the late departing XXR aircraft had just rotated.  With the very hazy sky I was concerned about a conflict and /or overtaking the other aircraft during the go-around to increase speed to Vref+10 and land with flaps 25. Landing and rollout were normal.Despite adequate and recent experience into ZZZZ; these particular controllers were hard to understand.  FO had to ask for clarification a few times in the last 1;500 feet FAF. FO had just returned from 7 months of leave; was called out on reserve; and had only flown once earlier; and 4 times in the last 7 months.  I briefed the arrival and approach thoroughly and early; but it was a Max effort with the direct clearance.  I believe he got distracted from completing the checklist when the localizer did not ID until just before the FAF.  I was distracted from not calling for final flaps with the aircraft on the runway and the hazy conditions; and the late localizer ID.Add to the note of being at Vref +10 by the ZZZZZ1 fix; to be at final landing flaps. This may have helped me if I had a goal to complete that by the fix.

Flight Attendant was removed from flight due to COVID exposure. Pre-flight complete with review of AML. Airplane swap from a Type 1 to a Type 0; due too no FA replacement. Running behind schedule. Pre-flighted aircraft and apparently did not verify AML which was left behind and in Maintenance hands. Could have sworn both First Officer and I had hands on! Departed to ZZZ1 without AML.Rushed.I always turn around and confirm that AML is on board at the stage of the checklist when it's announced. Obviously a lack of attention on my part. Still can't believe it happened.
Report_2_Narrative: During pre-flight the CA and I conducted our respective preflight duties; including the Before Start Checklist. At the line item AML and flight forms; we both verified that the AML was on the aircraft. The CA then placed it in the CA side bin. At XA:25 I was notified by the Duty Chief that Maintenance reported the AML for aircraft and XXX was left in ZZZ.Unknown; as we do not know how the AML was left behind. In the future I will be more vigilant in checking that the AML is onboard once the flight deck door is closed.

Report_1_Narrative: While waiting for the airplane to be towed to the departure gate; I ate a heavy breakfast and did not consume any coffee as this was an Air Carrier X operation and I anticipated coffee being readily available on the plane.  In spite of my request; the FA never made any coffee.  ATC issued a 15 degree heading change for traffic.  I made the heading change without incident.  Subsequently; I began to feel a bit drowsy.  After approximately 5 minutes; ATC issued 'Direct SUNED' fix.  I selected direct SUNED in the FMS and activated after PM confirmed the change.  I forgot to select the NAV mode on guidance panel and we continued on the previous heading for 3 more minutes.  I snapped to attention ATC reissued the Direct SUNED clearance and after a moment of confusion as to why the clearance was reissued; I realized that the guidance panel was still set to HDG mode.  The proper NAV mode was engaged and we proceeded direct to SUNED without incident or any further mention of the issue by ATC. Driven by adrenaline; I remained mentally aroused and vigilant for the remainder of the flight.Cause - PF's Postprandial lassitude caused by failure to ingest a suitable amount of caffeine following heavy breakfast.  PM inattention/distraction was also a factor.Ensure personal availability of postprandial drowsiness countermeasures (aka Coffee) and remember to verify changes on flight mode annunciator.

I had flown into LXT to drop off passengers and then to head back to home base; ZZZ. I had made calls on 122.8 Frequency announcing my intention to taxi from ramp to Runway 36. When I approached Runway 36; I broadcasted over the 122.8 Frequency that I was taking off on Runway 36 for an east departure out of the area. I looked on final and down the runway and determined that it was clear. Runway 36 has an up slope and was not able to see the end. As I accelerated down the runway I noticed a yellow light; but because of the slope I was not able to determine where it was on the airport or what it was. As I approached my rotation speed I could see that it was a vehicle (truck) on the runway moving in the same direction as me. I felt it was safer for me to takeoff and climb over the vehicle than to abort my takeoff. I did not hear any announcements from that vehicle at any time of my taxi and taking off on Runway 36. As I departed the area VFR and announced my departure to the east I made a comment that it was not a good idea for the vehicle to be on the runway when I was taking off. The response that I received from the truck was; 'This is X vehicle clear of Runway 36.' I called on the UNICOM Frequency 122.8 and asked if the person at the FBO had heard my calls to taxi and takeoff and the said they did. I really don't know what I could have differently.

On climbout lost flight instruments ADI HSI both sides and navigation capability.  Was on standby instruments.  I took the aircraft from FO and requested priority handling. With help from ATC.  Landed VFR safely at ZZZ.  Found the IRUs not properly aligned.With constant interruptions preparing for the flight by flight attendants; fuel slips; security forms; I failed to properly align the IRUs.  I usual devote my attention to this process and can only imagine that I got interrupted.  I was also tired.  Everything seemed normal on taxi out.  I was able to update the position at the end of the runway.  I had a First Officer brand new to the airplane.  Just finished IOE.  Ultimately it was my fault I take full responsibility.  I feel terrible about it.  Pilot error on my part.

The incident took place at ZZZZ on runway XX; where aircraft must back taxi to use full length. The CA (Captain) and I had just deadheaded down; with our first flying leg of the trip being from ZZZZ to ZZZ. I was feeling refreshed as I had napped for about 2 hours on the [deadhead] down. We had 1.5 hours on the ground; so did not feel rushed; even though it was my first time to ZZZZ. Boarding; preflight; briefing; clearance; pushback; start and taxi to hold short of Rwy XX were uneventful. The CA briefed language as a threat; but the clearance and ground control went smoothly. Initially we held short at taxiway E. Following a departure by another AC we were cleared to back taxi down to Runway XX approach end; and lineup and wait. While in our turn around; an aircraft checked in on final for our runway; I visually identified that aircraft and said aloud that he looked to be on a 6 mile final. Tower told that aircraft to continue.Once aligned with the centerline; I confirmed with the Captain that he was ready to depart; then told Tower Aircraft X in position; ready for takeoff'. Tower responded that we were cleared for takeoff runway XX; and to contact departure when airborne. I replied that we were cleared for takeoff; runway XX; and echoed the departure frequency we were just given.The Captain and I confirmed with each other we were both ready to go and he pushed up the throttles. As we both were watching the engines spool up; we began to roll forward down the runway. Tower then said something to the effect of "Aircraft X; please do not takeoff; there is an aircraft...' As I started to look up; my first thought was about the aircraft on final. The CA and I both looked at each other; confused about the nonstandard radio call. Tower sounded a bit flustered; and then I saw 2 strobe lights at the far end of the runway. I said 'Reject' aloud; then answered tower "Aircraft X rejecting takeoff.' The CA executed the rejected takeoff; but at only 40 knots or so; was really just bringing throttles to idle stopping the aircraft. I called 'Remain seated; remain seated' on the PA.At that point I started to see an aircraft that went with those strobe lights and a white twin engine civilian airplane lifted off our runway. Somehow this aircraft had gotten onto or been cleared onto RWY XX for an intersection takeoff. There had been Spanish being spoken on the frequency but I did not connect that discussion with an aircraft on our runway. The same frequency is used for clearance; pushback; ground; and tower control.As we stopped...perhaps 500-1000 ft. down the runway; tower then told us we were cleared for Takeoff again. We replied negative that we would need to taxi clear or do a 180 turn and go to the approach end. Tower then told the aircraft on final to go around while we taxied. The CA called for the Rejected takeoff checklist; which we ran. We used the card first then QRH back cover. The CA also used the PA to briefly explain what had just happened. We quickly discussed our options; and decided that we could do a 180; and rerun the before takeoff checklist. Subsequent lineup; and takeoff were uneventful."

I was the pilot monitoring and my First Officer was the pilot flying on Aircraft X from ZZZ1 to ZZZ.  We were rather high and fast at 6;000 feet with 250 knots when ATC cleared us for a visual approach for [Runway] XXL (backed up by RNAV XXL).  Approach clearance included the altitude restriction to cross ZZZ2 VOR at or above 2;500 feet which is normal when landing south at ZZZ.  However; in our effort to lose excess altitude and airspeed quickly we got busy; and that created a distraction in the cockpit.  The result was our crossing the VOR at around 2;200 feet (RNAV FAF altitude).During our post flight de-brief; the First Officer told me that he was not aware of the safety alert memo on the chart page; which cautions pilots on being high and fast when landing south.  He also told me that he was not sure of the location of ZZZ2 VOR on his navigational display.  None of that was communicated while in flight.  My being very familiar with operating in and out of ZZZ; and the past experience of working well with this particular First Officer; led me to assume that he was well aware of the uniqueness of the local procedures.  I should know better than to make assumptions about my crew's awareness.  What I should have done was bring that alert memo to his attention when he did not include that in his briefing.  I should have realized that the First Officer's recent flight experience might have been lacking due to COVID pandemic induced schedule reductions.  Again; a thorough briefing without my assuming First Officer's knowledge and awareness could have prevented the altitude deviation.  I will be careful going forward.
Report_2_Narrative: We were vectored off of the ZZZZZ arrival (landing south) for the visual [Runway] XXL. At this point; we were still holding 250 KIAS at 4;000 feet. We were given a heading to intercept and a clearance for the visual approach with the restriction to 'cross the VOR above 2;500 feet.' Although we had the aircraft stabilized by 1;500 feet AGL; we may have missed the crossing restriction by approximately 300 feet. During my approach briefing; I failed to notice the safety alert box located on the chart page. When we received the crossing restriction in the clearance; I struggled to find the VOR on the approach plate (RNAV XXL). First; I did not know which 'VOR' the clearance was referencing. Second; the ZZZ VOR is not charted on the RNAV XXL approach plate. This caused a brief moment of confusion allowing me to get 'behind' the aircraft. Instead of arming NAV mode; I engaged APPR mode. The Captain objected and NAV mode engaged. Realizing that we were fast; high and becoming task saturated with automation; I elected to disconnect the aircraft and fly the approach manually. We used gear and flaps to reduce airspeed before I lowered the nose to put the aircraft onto a glide path. We intercepted the glide path from above and managed to establish a stabilized approach by approximately 2;200 feet AGL.  However; in my assumption that we were high on the approach anyway; I did not verify that we crossed the VOR at 2;500ft. The Captain made mention of this after the flight ended in the debrief. He believes that we may have actually crossed the VOR at 2;200 feet (300 feet low).I realize that my failure to thoroughly review and brief all necessary approach plates resulted in missing critical information that would have helped us avoid the threat of this unusual crossing restriction.

Report_1_Narrative: A personal COMAT shipment for Person A was found to contain some lithium ion batteries in several pieces of equipment. One backup charger;  handheld vacuum cleaner; four pieces of lithium polymer battery operated LED lights; one  stunt drone; and PED (Personal electronic device).

On a visual approach to UNV descending through 6500 ft. MSL on a 15 mile left base for runway 24 EGPWS activated over south ridge.EGPWS activated aural alert for Terrain pull up.[Caused by] passing over rising terrain while descending to approach altitude.[Executed a] max power climb at 15 degrees nose up until certain of accurate situational awareness; ridge passage; and terrain clearance.[We should] reduce rate of descent when crossing the ridge approaching UNV valley.

Made visual contact with a GA aircraft on final approach to SEA runway 16R ahead of us. Continuing down the glideslope; we came to within 300 feet of the aircraft and a TCAS RA was issued; we climbed clear of the conflict and were then able to return to our glide path and make a stabilized approach and landing.Given the location of the aircraft relative to the approach path it is likely the GA aircraft had violated Seattle's Class B airspace; which is obviously not something we can control. However the TCAS system worked as designed and conflict was avoided without any abrupt maneuvering. Beyond additional focus on airspace awareness within the GA community; I do not have any additional recommendations.

The flight was normal until we had almost reached our gate in Chicago. We were parking [Gate]; and we were taxiing clockwise on Taxiway A. We were passing A12; A11; A10; and then right before we reached A9; a baggage cart driver with 4 baggage carts in tow drove out right in front of us; from a service road on the left hand side of the aircraft. They cut in front of us maybe 15 feet in front of the aircraft. We had to slam on the brakes in order to not hit them. We saw them as they were leaving the road; and if we had not taken action it would have been an accident. This happened right at XA:05. The baggage cart driver drove west to east; up the XX gates; and then turned up and followed alongside Taxilane B up the XY gates going north; until I couldn't follow them anymore. Obviously this was a major safety hazard. Both the Captain and myself said it was the closest we had ever come to hitting anything on the ground.

Throughout the nearly two hour flight; customers part of X organization group refused to comply with the mask policy; repeatedly laughing and mocking us when we attempted to enforce the policy. Compliance simply did not occur. FA A and C spoke many times with various members of the group; to no avail. FA A and C also spoke with adult leaders of the group; who did nothing to encourage compliance. We requested leadership meet the flight upon landing; and we recommend the offending customers be denied further transport on our airline. Our request to deny them travel was not granted by leadership in ZZZ. The offending group of customers were allowed to continue on to their next flight.

This occurred during flight.  Passenger X is seated at X5. Another passenger flagged me and said that Passenger X was not wearing a mask; and that he was coughing.  Passenger X was sleeping and I roused him on the arm and gave him his mask to put on; as he had placed the mask on his armrest. He put on his mask.  But sometime shortly before arrival (maybe 30 minutes before); I found him again without a mask and tried to rouse him. But this time he was wearing huge noise cancellation headphones and would not respond to any tapping or rousing; and no response to my calling his name.  He had drank some liquor during the flight; but not enough that he could not be roused.  I believe he was ignoring me on purpose and pretending to sleep; and had put the headsets on as an excuse to not hear me. Then the Captain called me that we had to prep the cabin early due to expected turbulence before landing.We then proceeded to do prep for landing. I again attempted to rouse Passenger X as his seat was reclined.  And again; no response. I had to physically push his chair upright; as he would not respond to do it himself.  Then I tried to rouse him again; tapping him; calling his name; and telling him to put on his mask. He finally slowly came awake. I put his mask in his hand (he again had put it on his armrest) and told him repeatedly to put on his mask.  He finally complied. And as he was leaving the airplane; I saw him again take off his mask at the jetbridge.I believe Passenger X was doing everything on purpose; and that he was awake and just pretending to sleep.  He would have had to have taken copious amounts of liquor to not have been aware of me rousing him; tapping him; or calling his name.  He also had this huge headphones on.  I feel strongly concerned about the possibility that he had done this before; and likely gotten away with it too.   I am putting this on for the record; so there will be something to refer to in case he does it again.
FA B and FA D repeatedly requested all passengers in the same party to lift and secure face coverings. Passengers would only cover up when flight attendants walked by.

Passenger boarded with mask down but pulled [it] up before I could ask him to. He proceeded to pull down his mask and sit down. I asked him at that point to please pull up mask; it's airline's policy to wear mask over nose and mouth. He stared at me but pulled it up. Once in the air; during service; he had his face covering below his nose. I asked him if there was a reason he wasn't following Airline's policy; he replied 'Listen I don't know what you're talking about.' So I repeated the policy to him and asked him to pull up his mask. He was very defiant but pulled up his face covering.
Report_2_Narrative: Passenger X's mask was down when boarding I asked him to pull up; he complied. He got to seat and pulled it down again; I approached and he pulled it up. During snack service it was down again. I asked him why he couldn't keep his mask up; did he understand the Company's Policy? He stated he didn't know what I was talking about. I told him the policy is to wear his mask over his nose and mouth for the duration of the flight; did he understand. Passenger X pulled up his mask with attitude and said lady;' You don't want problems with me.'

Report_1_Narrative: Suffice it to say that after touching down on a good 3-point landing with full control of the aircraft; on the rollout my left sneaker briefly got stuck on the left rudder pedal and then it started to lose the footing as I did the left-right; left-right continuous tapping that is required to control a tailwheel on the ground. So; with the tapping that is required in a tailwheel; with each tap after getting stuck; the left foot came more and more off the pedal; but I had to keep tapping to control the aircraft. While I slowed the aircraft down a lot; when my foot finally popped off the rudder pedal; the plane did about a ¼ loop to the left and the left landing gear snapped (as it is supposed to do when it receives excess side stress) and the left aileron scraped the runway. There was no real other damage [to the aircraft]; or to the runway; or any other aircraft. There were no injuries at all. As soon as the plane stopped; I turned the master and mags (magnetos) off and rotated the fuel lever into the off position.

Inbound to FXE on the FISEL SEVEN ARRIVAL. We were given a vector off of the arrival track and cleared to 3000 ft. Several miles Northwest of FXE on approximately a left downwind for the approach to Runway 09; as we were approaching level off at 3000 ft. we received a 'Traffic' warning from TCAS. Target was noted on the display at about 10 o'clock less than 1000 ft. below our altitude and well within the 3 mile circle; with a climbing indication (up arrow). As we continued on assigned heading at 3000 ft. the target appeared to be closing on our track and continuing to climb. Shortly after level off the TCAS issued a 'Climb' command. At this point I noted that the target was indicating 300 ft. below our altitude. I initiated the climb and called for the PM (Pilot Monitoring) to advise ATC of TCAS CLIMB. ATC acknowledged by calling out VFR traffic to us and stating that it was 'never above 2000 ft.' I acquired the target visually (low wing piston single) and observed it to be converging with our track from the left and close to our altitude. It is difficult to judge distance in this context; but we routinely pass aircraft that are 1000 ft. above/below our altitude. In my judgement the target in this case was much closer. We reached about 3600 ft. before TCAS went to a neutral vertical speed command; and the target aircraft was still indicating less than 1000 ft. below. By this time target was behind our aircraft and distance was increasing. Shortly after that TCAS issued a 'Clear of Conflict' and we returned to 3000 ft. The rest of the flight was uneventful; except to note that the same controller put us on a vector for the left base and then sent us beyond the final for Runway 09 while dealing with multiple VFR aircraft.

Departing ZZZ1 airport after a couple touch and goes I noticed Aircraft Y flying in front of us a mile or so. Before coming to ZZZ1 from ZZZ2 airport; I checked ForeFlight and erroneously gleaned that the airspace surrounding ZZZ1 and ZZZ was an MOA. But upon further inspection saw it was a Military Area. I did not contact ZZZ Approach at all. And am very sorry for my mistake. I recently got back into general aviation a few months back because of a lack of flying at my commercial flying job due to COVID.

I am aware that the pilot-controlled lighting frequency at PDK is different from the CTAF frequency of 120.9. I was on a VFR approach to PDK at night with a commercial instrument-rated pilot in the right seat when the Tower was closed. We both found it extremely difficult to find the PCL frequency buried in the Chart Supplement. We tried keying on multiple frequencies including CTAF 120.9 and UNICOM 122.95 but could not activate the runway lights. We heard another pilot attempting to turn on PCL on the CTAF frequency as well. I was able to continue to a safe landing by referencing taxiway lights and the runway environment illuminated by my own landing lights. After landing; we finally found the PCL frequency of 120.0 in the Chart Supplement and found that the lights were working. I understand that it is the PIC's responsibility to be aware of such exceptions to common procedures; but I think it would be highly beneficial; especially to those unfamiliar with the airport; to publish critical information such as PCL frequencies in a more accessible manner; e.g. on the sectional/TAC or on the airport diagram. An even better solution would be to consolidate the PCL frequency onto CTAF to reduce pilot workload in a critical phase of flight.

I was providing a friend dual instruction in his J-3 Cub. At the conclusion of a landing transitioning to taxi and exiting the runway; the aircraft's left wing was lifted by the NW crosswind that existed. The student attempted to recover; and when a ground loop began; I called for the aircraft and attempted to recover. During the recovery; the left main landing gear stuck a taxiway exit sign and caused damage to the left main gear. A prop-strike occurred with engine at idle. We exited the aircraft and no further incident. Since this incident; I have spent a few days self-reflecting what I could have done as an instructor to prevent this mishap. I have taught tailwheel in various aircraft for years; and believe that I have too high of a tolerance to allow a student to get into trouble. Where my mindset existed that 'this helps them to learn from their mistakes;' while true; I have researched and explored with industry experts a better way to approach when I make the decision to intervene in a tailwheel aircraft. Moving forward; I plan to use a general 'two second rule;' that was discussed with me by an FAA DPE. In his opinion; if a student makes an error that isn't corrected by the time he counts to two; he will intervene on the controls. While simple; it's a great plan that I will start to incorporate into my dual instruction given.

This involves 3 near misses and being cutoff by the same pilot three times. I was at 5;500 feet with flight following coming south to north to reach ZZZ Airport from the ZZZ VOR. The wind was out of the north and the runway in use was Runway XX. As I was descending to fly past the airport and join the pattern with a 45 degree entry to downwind; I heard at least 3 planes in the pattern. Then; I heard a Bonanza behind me announce a straight in. I advised him that it was unwise given a full pattern; which would soon include me. I flew east of the airport and joined the Runway XX right downwind pattern with a standard 45 degree entry. The Bonanza then announced he would not go straight in. As I was in the pattern; downwind abeam the numbers; the Bonanza flew directly across my path. I was flying south in the downwind; and he was flying east. The Bonanza crossed about 200 feet above me and this was way too close to overfly me in the pattern. I announced downwind; base; and then final as I completed my pattern. I even called a redundant short final. As I was on short final; the Bonanza cut in front of me by taking a base entry. I was only about 400 feet above the ground; on short final. He was directly in front of me turning base to very short final. I called out for him to avoid and that I would be doing a go around. The Bonanza had overshot his approach and couldn't make the turn to the runway. Instead of landing; he then started to go around; pulling up directly into my path. I made an avoidance abrupt turn to the west to avoid colliding with the Bonanza. I then flew north; away from the field; and waited for the Bonanza to rejoin the pattern to land before rejoining the pattern well behind him and eventually landing. I have never turned in another pilot for making a mistake; but three mistakes in the pattern like this. Cutting me off; who had made multiple announcements to avoid a deviant pilot like this; still did not avoid an almost mid-air collision. I highly recommend investigation of the offending pilot. Among other things; he should know better than to try to fly a straight in to an extremely busy airport like ZZZ with multiple planes in the pattern and he should know the proper procedures for entering and flying a full pattern.

Just received a message stating I did not complete [the] COVID assessment for yesterday.  I believe I just forgot to send it due to flight deck distraction with maintenance in there.I arrived at the aircraft and Maintenance was still on it completing their First Flight of the Day (FFOD) check so I had to wait and work around them in the flight deck. I did pre-flight the thermometer and take my temperature and all was OK. But with the mechanic in the flight deck and the FO and Forward FA it was a little busy. I thought I had submitted on the COVID digital assessment; but I must have forgotten and when I got it my seat I continued with my set up believing I had done it. I must have forgotten to submit.For now add it onto the checklist as a FFOD item.  Or send us an ACARS message on our first flight. Maybe just have us when we check in on every leg to attest our fitness for duty that that counts as a COVID check.   I think there are solutions to this COVID affirmation than just pulling up the COVID assessment.   If we are not fit for duty for these reasons we should not be reporting to work anyway.  I think scanning the barcode every leg is a much better way for pilots to confirm COVID compliance without adding an extra step which can; by accident; be forgotten to [be] taken.

On the CHPPR 1 RNAV arrival assigned 180 knots after NAVVY. Shortly thereafter was cleared for the approach ILS 9R. Captured glide slope and continued the approach at 180 knots. Told to contact Tower and we were cleared to land. At 1 mile prior to final approach fix began to configure; slow down to 160 knots; and then VREF (Approach speed). Fully configured and approach checklist complete; stable approach. I recall a Controller asking the traffic behind us to slow down. At 1;200 feet AGL Controller told us to cancel landing clearance; fly runway heading; and then 15 right turn shortly thereafter.Go-around procedure completed and we asked reason for go-around. Controller said we slowed down 6 miles prior to runway. Note: Final approach fix is 5 miles from the runway and we do not recall a 180 knot assigned speed to the final approach fix. We were vectored back to the ILS 9R and landed; all else normal.Tower to request go-around; task saturation.

On or around [date] on Aircraft X; ZZZ-ZZZ1 a Captain wasn't wearing his mask ; I asked the Captain to please wear his mask if he wasn't eating or drinking and he complied. Passenger seated in front of the Captain wasn't wearing his mask as well. I asked the passenger and his wife to please wear their masks as well since they were watching a movie and 'NOT EATING NOR DRINKING!'' The passenger was seated in [seat] X4 and clearly didn't want to comply with the face mask policy. I received a complaint letter from this passenger who didn't want to wear a face mask when I was just doing my job. Other passengers in the cabin was complaining about the passenger in [seat] X4 who clearly wasn't wearing his mask over his nose and mouth. I made 1 announcement explaining our face covering policy.  The face mask policy announcement has since been changed to reflect the face mask must be worn except while eating or drinking for a brief moment.In the future I will report on any employee if they are non-compliant since employees set the tone and pace on the flight. When employees don't comply with policy or procedures passengers think they can do the same and not comply. The Airline should not ask employees to follow policy and procedures and when customers send in bad letters make employees accountable.

On [date]; Aircraft X; Passenger X; became very defensive and rude after I asked him to place his mask over his nose and mouth. His response for having it off was that he was drinking coffee; however; there was no coffee and he never disposed of any during trash pickup. After doing my compliance check; he ask if we had any mask that he could wear and drink at the same time in a condescending tone. I replied no and walked to the boarding door for departure.After door closure; Passenger X began to talk on the phone. After repeatedly asking him to get off; he did not comply. After the Captain made an announcement for all cellular devices to be in airplane mode; he still continued his conversation until he was done talking. His non-compliance and dismissive approached could be a risk in the unlikely event of an emergency in his future of flying.

While transitioning VNY Airport from the North I was told by ATC to remain on the east side of the 405 freeway while pattern traffic was restricted on the west side of the 405 (standard practice). The aircraft opposite direction never read back to remain on the west side of the 405 freeway and remained on the east side where I was causing a loss of separation. I took evasive action and immediately broke right around Aircraft Y which continued straight ahead. Had I not taken evasive action it would have been a midair collision. The pilot was a solo student pilot as well. I called the tower to ensure they spoke with the pilot and asked how to best mitigate this from happening again; we agreed the controller should have verified the read back and maybe in the future they will pay closer attention to closely separated planes.

After landing in ZZZ on Aircraft X with a cold soaked wing and full main fuel tanks; I asked the rampers to do an ice check. One of the rampers brought out the ladder; checked the wing; and then told me the wing was clean. From my vantage point by the L1 door I found this hard to believe as it looked liked frost extending along the entire length of the wing. I politely asked the Ramper to bring back the ladder so I could inspect the wing. After he brought back the ladder; I climbed the ladder and found obvious frost along the length of the wing. I told the Ramper what I saw and asked him if he would like to look at the wing again. He immediately got pissed off; refused to look at the wing; told me it was clean; declared 'we're going to agree to disagree but I'll deice you' and walked off. ZZZ deiced our aircraft without incident. Captains need to stand their ground and ensure deicing is completed when warranted.  Further; I have never experienced anything similar to this at (airline).

During the boarding process on Aircraft X from ZZZ to ZZZ1; I was working the AFT position. I noticed that the Passenger X seated in seat X4 had his mask down. He waited until he passed my flying partner to pull it down. He took his seat and was talking to a passenger across from him. Once I was done saying my 5 minute to door closing announcement I began walking to the front of the aircraft. Once I got to row X; [Passenger X] had his mask down under his chin. I told Passenger X he had to place his mask over his nose and mouth. He looked at me rolled his eyes and did a heavy sigh. I walked away and Passenger X began discussing the mask issue with the passenger seated across from him whom I learned was his friend who was original assigned to seat Y1; but decided to change his seat to seat X1 instead. Passenger Y had an oversized laptop in the back seat pocket and I told him that he had to stow it in a bag under the seat in front of him or in an overhead bin. He replied 'Yes; ma'am.' Once the doors were closed I began doing my compliment check passenger seated in X4; Passenger X had his mask down below his nose this time. I spoke to him once again. He wasn't happy about it so he rolled his eyes again at me and snatched the mask up over his nose and shrugged his shoulders. This was the second time Passenger X had to be reminded about his mask.

On flight ABCD from ZZZ to ZZZ1 while boarding passenger seated in seat XX1 came on that aircraft with his mask below his nose and between his lips. I told that passenger his mask must come over his nose and mouth. He responded 'He will try.' I then offered him a company approved surgical mask that were provided by the company. He responded 'No thanks.' That was the first time of me asking him to put his mask on properly. During my compliant check before doing the safety demo; the mask was down again. I told him once again about the mask. That was the second time. During cabin service the mask was down again and I had to remind him once more. Once cabin service was over and passengers were given time to eat and drink; I then went around to collect trash and he was asleep with his mask down. I called out to him and he opened his eyes and I told him for the third time about his mask. Once we got to the final descent his mask was down and I reminded him once again for the fourth time. Once I told him this time he just looked at me and shook his head. This passenger (XX1) and his wife seated in XX2 were both sick. They were both coughing without covering their cough.The couple kept coughing during takeoff; throughout the flight and landing. The coughing drew the attention of other passengers to look. People are very scared and when they hear coughing and other passengers [are] not wearing mask properly they worry. Many verbal warnings were given.

Enroute IFR to ZZZ1 from ZZZ2 we were advised by ZZZ Center that there was control issue at ZZZ TRACON and that we would either have to divert to alternate airport or fly VFR to destination. The weather at ZZZ1 was good so we said we could plan to go VFR into ZZZ1 and we were cleared direct destination. At approximately 35 SE of ZZZ1 at 10;000 feet ZZZ Center asked if we were VMC and we advised we would be upon reaching 9;000 feet. At 9;000 feet we advised Center we were VMC and could proceed visually to ZZZ1. At that point we were on the boundary of ZZZ Class B. I specifically asked if Center would provide flight following through Class B enroute to ZZZ1.  Center responded by saying something to the effect 'Class B airspace is down.' I assumed that ATC was saying that Class B airspace was not in effect due to ZZZ TRACON problem.We proceeded to ZZZ1 and landed without issue. I was then advised by ground control to call ZZZ Center. I called and explained what happened to man on duty. I later that day again spoke with the same individual and he advised me there was a COVID cleaning event that created the problem at TRACON. He advised me that the Controller who handled my flight was in error and did not handle the situation properly.In the event this situation ever happens again I need to make sure I work with ATC to obtain a clearance either out of or through Class B airspace. This was an unusual set of circumstances and that's when everyone involved should have a keener awareness of following proper protocol.

I was in the traffic pattern inbound for the second touch and go on Runway XX at ZZZ Airport. I was turning left base for Runway XX; before I noticed that an aircraft was inbound for left base for the opposite runway which is Runway XY. I tried to communicate with him that I was turning for the final of Runway XX but I did not hear any respond. Then I continued for the final of Runway XX. Then I called out for the short final of Runway YY then I corrected twice that I was on the short final of Runway XX. Upon the completion of the touch and go I called out that I was on the upwind of Runway XX and then the aircraft called out for left base of Runway XY. I pitched down to try to avoid the aircraft that was on the left base of Runway XY. I tried to see and avoid but I still did not have the aircraft in sight. I pitched up to climb and I saw the aircraft approximately 150 feet above me and I avoided the midair collision. I communicated with the other pilot and asked him if he had seen me and he replied that he thought I was landing on Runway YY.

Applying MEL for #2 engine anti-ice valve to be locked open. Due to the rain and wearing a mask; glasses fogged and missed the step to unplug the pressure switch.Suggestions - Remove mask so glasses will not fog. Get somewhere the rain will not affect tablet usage. Functionality and readability.

While transitioning from the KENNO2 Arrival to the ILS Z RWY (Runway) 16R (After KLOCK and before BELBE); FMA kicked off VNAV and defaulted to CWS. As a result; the aircraft descended below 9;000 ft. MEA. PF (Pilot Flying) flying detected cause at approximately 7;800 ft.; leveled the aircraft and climbed to 8;000 ft. Shortly thereafter ATC queried our altitude. We didn't receive any other indications (Terrain warning; etc.)PF didn't remove discontinuity between KLOCK and BELBE. Aircraft continued descent towards 7;500 ft.; the altitude set in the FMA. PF/PNF (Pilot Not Flying) didn't sufficiently/simultaneously review the waypoints in the FMC.Authorized/conducted a visual approach without incident.

Altitude report deviation. Doing the approach ILS [Runway] XX; we were cleared by ATC to descend to 3;500 ft. We set 2;500 ft. by mistake in the altitude window during instructions. While descending ATC contacted us due to the mistake. We realized we began to correct the altitude again to 3;500 ft.; so ATC gave us again a clearance to stay at 2;500 ft. We continue the approach without any incident from present position.Instruction were for the approach to maintain heading and altitude; after that we were cleared for the approach for ILS [Runway] XX. At that time the wrong altitude was set in the altitude window and we didn't realize the error until ATC notified for the deviation.We started to climb immediately climbing to the correct altitude; but ATC asked to stay at the altitude we were of 2;500 ft. without any problem.
Report_2_Narrative: On approach for ILS [Runway] XX; ATC cleared us to descend to 3;500 ft. 2;500 ft. was mistakenly set in the altitude window when given our instructions. We descended to 2;500 ft. and ATC contacted us regarding the error. We started to correct our altitude to 3;500 ft. when ATC gave us a new clearance to remain at 2;500 ft. The ILS approach to [Runway] XX was executed without further incident.We received our instructions for the approach to maintain heading; altitude; and were cleared for the ILS [Runway] XX. During that moment the wrong altitude was placed in the altitude window and was not caught until ATC notified us of the deviation.As stated our reaction was to correct immediately by climbing to the appropriate altitude of 3;500 ft.; but we were given a new clearance to stay where we were at 2;500 ft.

Report_1_Narrative: Flight planned through HF required airspace on a non-HF equipped aircraft.This flight was rerouted to avoid ZZZ1 [Center] airspace due ATC 0 for COVID cleaning. XA20z - XE01z. I looked at routing through the gulf and over Country Y but no overfly permit available for this flight number. Contacted Airnav / Overfly to confirm no overfly permit. They said we no longer apply for the ZZZ-ZZZ1 or ZZZ-ZZZ2 [airport] flights. So I planned the flight to go east of ZZZ1 [Center]. This route took the flight through ZZZ [Center] Oceanic airspace. I failed to recall that that section of ZZZ [Center] Oceanic airspace is HF comm only; no CPDLC or VHF comm. The aircraft assigned to the flight did not have HF voice. The flight was able to continue by relaying position reports though our Aircraft Y on Guard frequency flying the same route/airspace.With the frequent airspace closures due to COVID cleaning we need more routing options to and from the US - Country Z. We need to start applying for Country Y overfly permits for these southbound flights again so that is an option for us. In addition to usable Country Y routes; a canned route that takes us east of ZZZ1 [Center] and ZZZ2 [Center] needs to be added with route remarks stating that dual HF voice is required for that route. It has been some time since we operated in that portion of ZZZ [Center] airspace and I failed to recall the requirement for HF. I assume the majority of dispatchers would make the same mistake who work this sector less frequently.

On arrival into ZZZ; we were instructed to turn to a heading and descend from 11;000 ft. to 3;000 ft. We began a descent using speed mode set at 250 kts. Just below 4;000 ft.; we received an Obstacle Caution message. During the process of turning the descent into a climb; which removed the caution message; we were instructed to climb to 4;000 ft. I was able to identify the obstacle; an antenna; below and to the right of the aircraft. We passed the antenna above and to the left. We leveled off at 4;000 ft. From there; we were vectored to a visual approach to [Runway] XX and proceeded with a normal visual approach and landing.Descending in speed mode likely caused the aircraft to approach the level off altitude earlier than ATC anticipated thus causing the higher closure rate to the obstacle; generating the caution message. Better terrain awareness could have prompted a shallower descent rate; perhaps using vertical speed mode. Querying ATC about the level off altitude; suggesting a higher bottom altitude; could also have reduced or eliminated the occurrence of the caution message.

Got a TCAS RA just prior to glide slope intercept on ILS. [A] single engine small aircraft was crossing above at 500 feet over us. ATC pointed out the traffic. Clearance was to maintain 3;000 feet until established and cleared for [the] ILS Approach. We could have started our descent before capturing the glide slope before the final approach fix.

I was providing On the Job Instruction during the event. I had previously cleared Aircraft X to 17;000 feet prior to the trainee taking position. Another aircraft; Aircraft Y; was also receiving vectors for the approach and was given a descent to 14000 feet and a speed reduction to 210 knots. Aircraft X read back the instructions and then immediately Aircraft Y read back the instructions. On position; I did not hear the call sign of Aircraft X and thought that Aircraft Y read back the instruction a second time. It was clearly a different voice so I just thought that the pilots were confused as to who was talking and who was flying. A few moments later the Developmental recognized that Aircraft X was at 15;200 feet in a 15;500 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) and issued a low altitude alert. The pilot responded that it was blocked and the trainee gave an immediate climb to 16;000 feet. I recognized the the trainee did not use the full proper phraseology by not saying the 'MVA in your area is XXX;' but decided at this point the solution had been acknowledge and it was not worth correcting the phraseology in a third transmission. The aircraft continued to descend to 14;400 feet until we observed a climb. During that time the aircraft crossed into a 14;900 foot MVA; but being in a climb I decided it was not necessary to issue another safety alert since we knew it was being corrected.There was a bit of frequency congestion due to confusion about the weather; and by the time I was ready to issue a brasher warning the aircraft was on a 10 mile final. I felt it was best to let the aircraft fly and have Local Control or Ground Control issue the brasher warning; so I coordinated that with Ground Control.

Level at 10;000 ft ATC informed us of traffic at 1 o'clock at 9;000 ft. We did not have the traffic in sight. It displayed shortly after as a white target on TCAS. I reduced my ND (Navigation Display) range and saw it go to amber and indicate 500 ft below us and maybe 300 ft for a moment. I disconnected the autopilot in anticipation for a possible RA which we did get. It was a climb RA followed by a level RA which in total lasted maybe 5-10 seconds until we got the clear of conflict. We followed the RA guidance and saw the traffic as we were conducting the RA. We notified ATC as we climbed and once we had traffic in sight and clear of traffic. We received vectors for an uneventful landing at DEN on [Runway] 34R.

Shortly after pushing back from the gate; FA (Flight Attendant) called to ask if the last 2 rows needed to be empty.  I said that we had no idea; that this was the first we had heard of a weight and balance issue; and that I'd ask about it.  We contacted Load Planning (LP); and they told us that the rows needed to be blocked.  After we departed; FA asked if the rows needed to stay empty; or if they could now be occupied.  Again I contacted LP; and they said to keep the rows empty.  I responded that we had no idea of the balance problem until the FAs brought it to our attention. According to the Flight Ops Manual; this information is supposed to be included on the final weights document sent by ACARS.  If the flight attendants had not asked about the rows we would never have known; and would possibly have been out of balance limits.  One picture is the final weight manifest; the other is the reply from Load Planning when I asked how we were supposed to know about rows needing to be blocked.

Taking the runway; cleared for takeoff; when the FO (First Officer) called out 'FMC' I got to where I normally check 'NAV armed' and I realized I had never selected a runway in the FMS. We had V speeds and everything looked (almost) normal. I told the FO to select the runway; not thinking this would wipe out our V Speeds. We started the roll; I called '80 knots'; made one last check of the engine instruments and looked up. At this point I noticed the display wasn't right. We were already over 100 kts. So I elected to continue. I knew rotate speed was in the low 160 knot range (turned out it was 162) so I waited and called rotate at 170. FO called out he didn't have a Flight Director. He did a nice job of flying the jet. After some confusion we got PROF selected and the Flight Director was once again active. Causal chain started early. Last flight of trip. FO and I get along well; very comfortable with each other. When doing the numbers; I obviously missed that the FMS didn't have a runway in when I called 'runway'. I was just listening for the correct response from the FO.  Then; taking the runway; I should have stopped the airplane and solved the problem. There was no reason to rush. We could have taxied off the runway if necessary to make sure we had it all squared away. It was complacency on my part that missed the lack of a runway in the FMS. I usually stare right at it when I call 'runway' and obviously I wasn't. I've caught this same mistake in the past. Then when I realized the mistake taking the runway I should have stopped everything and solved the problem as a crew. Biggest set of errors in my career and most dangerous.

We blocked out for our XA:35 scheduled reposition flight to ZZZ1 at XA:25 at gate X in ZZZ.  Shortly after blocking out we received an MDC fault status message so getting a ramp crew to push us right away was not our top priority.  I called Maintenance and we were able to MEL the MDC through a pilot deferral.  As I finished up the paperwork I asked my FO to call operations to start rounding up a ramp crew for us as we would be ready to leave shortly; I am guessing this was around XA:45.  After a while I saw 2 rampers come out; they disconnected power then seemed to disappear again.  I knew we were a reposition flight; so understood if we weren't the highest priority as the weather wasn't the best; so I tried not to push too hard for them to hurry up.  At approximately XB:05; I was just about to call ops to ask them the status on our push crew when our main cabin door was opened unexpectedly by a cleaning crew that informed us they needed to do a sweep and clean our airplane.  I was baffled that they just came up to our aircraft unannounced and opened our door without making any contact with us first.  We had already completed our pushback checklist and everything 100% ready to go and our door just opens up.  They completed the security sweep and the COVID cleaning form (why we need COVID cleaning to send a plane into heavy check is beyond me).No one at the airport seemed to have any idea that this aircraft was going to be ferried out.  This led to ground personnel not having everything completed that they were supposed to complete prior to departure.  The gate agent told me we were good to go as it was a ferry flight; so I was under the assumption that the security sweep and COVID cleaning would not be needed.To prevent this from happening again I think it would be wise to revert our pushback checklist to how it used to be where we would turn the beacon on as a part of the pushback flow.  Those cleaners could have potentially found themselves in a dangerous situation very fast and I think the beacon would have stopped them.  As a former ramp worker I was always taught never to approach an aircraft with a beacon on.  Also; I think some more guidance on FFOD security sweeps and COVID cleaning requirements regarding reposition and charters would be nice.  I worked a charter flight recently and thinking back on it we were never given a security sweep on day 2 of our charter.  I have done several repositions lately and sometimes we aren't starting at a terminal and cleaners haven't had access to the airplane.

While on downwind for a Visual [Runway] 21 we received a terrain caution (that lasted for about one second) while flying over the mountains. We were in visual conditions and clear of all obstacles. We continued the approach into CHO with no further delays.  Casual Factor - Terrain around the Charlottesville airport.  Made sure we were clear of the terrain and then continued on approach. Be weary of taking the visual around the mountains at lower altitudes. We should have also climbed a little bit to give ourselves some more clearance.
Report_2_Narrative: While being vectored for a Visual Approach to Runway 21 at CHO on a left downwind; level; at an assigned altitude of 3;000 MSL we received a GPWS caution annunciation of: 'terrain pull up'. The flight conditions were day VMC; so both the Captain and I had the terrain we were overflying in sight.  Our vectored flight path took us over steep rising terrain. Upon hearing the GPWS annunciation; we verbally acknowledged the annunciation and the visual steep rising terrain we were overflying.  We maintained straight and level flight because we could visual ensure we were clear of all obstacles and/or terrain. Request ATC assigned an altitude of 4;000 MSL instead of 3;000 MSL when vectoring on this flight path for a visual approach to Runway 21 into CHO.

Report_1_Narrative: With a light load (XX passengers); Tower cleared for an expedited takeoff Runway XXL; Runway heading. Takeoff without incident; with a high climb rate due to weight. Configured and continued climb hand flying. Reaching 3;200 feet; at 200 knots; climb rate was 4;000 FPM+; leveled a bit to slow climb and increase speed to 250 knots.  Captain was completing after takeoff checklist; and made 1;000 foot call. Reached 250 knots at 4;200 feet; and proceeded to slow and increase climb simultaneously; while ATC was giving further clearance. In my mind I expected a further climb; but we only received direct ZZZZZ. Realizing we were not getting cleared higher; and at 4;600 feet with an increasing climb rate currently at 3;500 FPM; I immediately began correcting with pitch and power. Captain said watch your altitude; and I advised correcting. ATC advised maintain 5;000 feet; Captain responded correcting. The stable correction required a top altitude of 5;300 feet. ATC advised no problem; and that there was overhead traffic. There was no disruption to traffic or flight due to this 300 feet deviation. The rest of the flight was conducted without incident.I believe I slowed my deceleration in order to level at 5;000 feet when I heard ATC begin giving further clearance because I just expected higher to be given also. Obviously each clearance is specific; regardless of expectations; so pitch and power accordingly; and a correction won't be necessary. It didn't help that the climb rate was very high due to light load; adding hand flying to it just increased the workload. At light loads; in the busy ZZZ environment; probably best to utilize the automation a bit more to lighten the workload.
Report_2_Narrative: During initial climb via the ZZZZZ departure as I was completing the after takeoff checklist I noticed we were at a very high rate of climb as we were approaching our top altitude (maybe 4;800 feet) and reminded name (FO) of our 5;000 assigned altitude he immediately started correcting. As a result of the rate of climb we climbed through 5;000 feet to 5;300 feet and then back down to 5;000 feet. This happened during a busy time I was running checklist and communicating with ATC while the FO was hand flying. I feel that I could have paused the checklist from the 1;000 feet horn and call until our altitude was captured to make sure we wouldn't climb through.

Report_1_Narrative: Arriving Denver on the CLASH 3 STAR. Initially planning on 35R. We were given Runway 35L descending through 15;000 feet. Immediately after getting runway change; we were given a heading and cleared to 11;000 feet. ATC then cleared us for 9;000 feet. ATC asked if we had the airport in sight (we did) and turned us to a heading of 325 degrees and cleared us for the visual approach to 35L. That heading essentially aimed us directly at the FAF DYMON. I selected 7;000 feet in the altitude alert and continued the descent visually. Shortly after leveling at 7;000 feet; ATC advised us altitude alert; minimum altitude our sector 7;500 feet. I climbed up to 7;500 feet and remained there until glideslope interception. Flight continued uneventfully to DEN.I should have stayed at 9;000 feet until glideslope intercept.
Report_2_Narrative: Arriving DEN on the CLASH 3 STAR. Initially planning on 35R was given Runway 35L descending through 15;000 feet MSL. Immediately after getting runway change; we were cleared to 11;000 feet. ATC then cleared us for 9;000 feet. ATC asked if we had the airport in sight; we responded in the affirmative; and ATC turned us to a heading of 325 degrees; and cleared us for the Visual Approach to 35L. That heading essentially aimed us directly at the FAF DYMON on the ILS for Runway 35L.The Pilot Flying (PF) selected 7;000 feet in the altitude window and continued his descent visually. Shortly after arriving at 7;000 feet; ATC advised us Altitude Alert and stated minimum altitude was 7;500 feet in our sector. The PF immediately corrected our altitude to 7;500 feet and we stayed at that altitude until glideslope intercept for the ILS to 35L. We landed uneventfully in DEN.When cleared for the visual; stay at your last safe altitude assigned until intercepting glideslope; or have better awareness of the minimum altitude in the sector you are flying. Another option would be for visual clearance to land including a minimum altitude if required.

Report_1_Narrative: Flight departed the gate showing dangerous goods unsecured. The ramp lead tried to final multiple times without the required 15 bags and this is the alert that populated in my [Software] bar.[Error Message] The flight was already showing off the gate and had been off the gate for some time before the ramp attempted to final and they attempted to final the flight 4 times without the required bags. The lead realizing the issue then went into [Load Planning Software] and manually changed the bag count which of course allowed them to final the flight.

While operating our flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1 I was the Pilot Monitoring. At the time of the event; I was eating my catering and the Pilot Flying and I both found it easier for the PF to respond to ATC and manipulate the FMS. At some point at cruise altitude; ATC cleared us to fly direct from present position to 'ZZZZZ.' The PF responded to ATC and erroneously typed 'ZZZZZ1' into the FMS scratchpad and entered the waypoint as the direct-to navigation fix after both pilots checked the resulting dashed 'proposed course line' on the PF's ND screen. After several minutes; ATC inquired if we were proceeding direct to 'ZZZZZ' as he showed us drifting right of the intended course. This alerted us to double-check our navigation and to discover that we were navigating to the wrong fix; the misspelled 'ZZZZZ1.'
